The pipe band championship is the most prestigious in the world. Dunoon was a fashionable Victorian resort — the 'Madeira of the Clyde' — reached by paddle steamer from Glasgow.
dunoon.knownFor: The Cowal Highland Gathering (the world's largest Highland Games), the Clyde ferry from Gourock, the Cowal Peninsula, and an Argyll resort town that transforms every August for the world's greatest pipe band championship
dunoon.localTip: The Cowal Highland Gathering is genuinely extraordinary — the massed pipe bands playing on the stadium grass is one of Scotland's great spectacles. The Cowal Way walking route. The ferry from Gourock for the Clyde crossing. Benmore Botanic Garden for the giant redwoods.
dunoon.name: Dunoon

durham.history: Durham Cathedral was begun in 1093 — it is considered the finest example of Norman Romanesque architecture in England. Durham Castle, directly opposite, was a prince-bishop's palace
durham.knownFor: Durham Cathedral (Norman masterpiece), Durham Castle, the river walk and a small city whose university gives it a cultural life well above its size
durham.localTip: Walk completely around the peninsula — Prebends Bridge and the river walk is one of England's most beautiful urban walks. See the cathedral interior — the scale is staggering. Go early.
durham.name: Durham

dursley.history: Dursley's most famous export was the Pedersen bicycle — a revolutionary cantilever-framed bicycle designed by Mikael Pedersen and manufactured here from 1893. The Cotswold Way long-distance walk passes through the town. The town sits beneath the western Cotswold escarpment.
dursley.knownFor: The Cotswold Way passing through, the Dursley Pedersen bicycle (a revolutionary Victorian design made here), Cam Peak and Stinchcombe Hill, and a small Gloucestershire market town beneath the Cotswold escarpment
dursley.localTip: Stinchcombe Hill above the town has extraordinary views over the Severn Vale — one of the Cotswolds' great viewpoints. Cam Peak for the walk. The town market. The Cotswold Way through the town for walking.
dursley.name: Dursley

ealing.history: Ealing Studios, founded in 1902, is the world's oldest working film studio. The Ealing Comedies of the 1940s and 50s — Kind Hearts and Coronets, The Lavender Hill Mob, The Ladykillers — defined a particular English sensibility. The borough has consistently been one of London's most desirable places to live.
ealing.knownFor: Ealing Studios (the world's oldest working film studios), the Ealing comedies (Kind Hearts and Coronets, The Ladykillers, Passport to Pimlico), the green and pleasant suburb character, and one of London's most affluent outer boroughs
ealing.localTip: Ealing Studios tours are available — genuine film history. Walpole Park for the beautiful Victorian parkland and the Pitzhanger Manor gallery. The Broadway for excellent independent food. Southall's Golden Mile for the finest South Asian food in London.
ealing.name: Ealing

east-grinstead.history: The Queen Victoria Hospital at East Grinstead was where Archibald McIndoe pioneered plastic surgery techniques on burned RAF pilots during WWII — the Guinea Pig Club, the fraternity of airmen he treated, was formed here. Sackville College, built in 1609, is one of England's finest Jacobean almshouses. The High Street has extraordinary medieval and Tudor buildings.
east-grinstead.knownFor: The Canadian Burns Unit (where pioneering plastic surgery was performed on RAF pilots in WWII), Sackville College (a stunning Jacobean almshouse), the High Street (one of England's finest medieval commercial streets), and a West Sussex market town of real historical significance
east-grinstead.localTip: Sackville College is beautiful and open to visitors. The High Street is genuinely outstanding — medieval Wealden Hall houses at street level. The Guinea Pig Club story is told at the hospital. Standen House (NTS) for Arts and Crafts beauty nearby.
east-grinstead.name: East Grinstead

east-ham.history: East Ham developed as a Victorian suburb, home to successive waves of immigrant communities. The Central Park was opened in 1898. The area is now predominantly South Asian and West African, with one of the most diverse food cultures in London. The City of London Cemetery contains remarkable Victorian monuments.
east-ham.knownFor: The Central Park and Manor Park, the City of London Cemetery (with notable Victorian monuments), the diverse South Asian and West African communities, and one of East London's most vibrant multicultural neighbourhoods
east-ham.localTip: East Ham High Street for extraordinary South Asian food — some of the best and most authentic in London. The City of London Cemetery for Victorian monumental sculpture. Central Park for a local green space.
east-ham.name: East Ham

east-kilbride.history: East Kilbride was Scotland's first designated New Town in 1947 — built to rehouse Glasgow overspill. The town centre was designed with a network of pedestrianised streets. Hunter House was the birthplace of John and William Hunter, the 18th century physicians who transformed surgery and anatomy.
east-kilbride.knownFor: Scotland's first new town (1947), the National Museum of Rural Life, Hunter House (birthplace of the Hunter brothers who made vital contributions to medicine), and South Lanarkshire's largest town
east-kilbride.localTip: The National Museum of Rural Life is excellent — farm and country life in Scotland through the centuries. Hunter House for the remarkable medical history. The town centre shopping is extensive. Calderglen Country Park for walking.
east-kilbride.name: East Kilbride

eastbourne.history: Eastbourne was carefully planned by the Duke of Devonshire as a model Victorian resort — the seafront's Grand Parade, hotels and gardens are a remarkably complete example of Victorian seaside town planning. Beachy Head, rising 530 feet from the sea, is one of England's most dramatic natural landmarks.
eastbourne.knownFor: Beachy Head (the dramatic chalk headland, one of England's most iconic coastline features), the Grand Parade (one of England's finest Victorian seafronts), the Towner Gallery, and a South East resort of exceptional Victorian architecture
eastbourne.localTip: Beachy Head for the extraordinary clifftop walk — genuinely one of England's great coastal views. The Towner Gallery for excellent contemporary art. The Grand Parade for the full Victorian seaside experience. The Carpet Gardens in summer.
eastbourne.name: Eastbourne

eastleigh.history: Eastleigh grew from a small village to a substantial town solely because the London and South Western Railway relocated its carriage and wagon works here in 1891. The works built and maintained countless famous steam locomotives. The town has an important swan sanctuary on the River Itchen.
eastleigh.knownFor: The railway engineering heritage (the Eastleigh Works built and repaired steam locomotives), the swan sanctuary, the Point Royal development, and a Hampshire town that grew entirely because of the railway
eastleigh.localTip: The Eastleigh Lakeside Steam Railway for families. The swan sanctuary on the Itchen is excellent. The town centre market. The railway heritage is commemorated throughout.
eastleigh.name: Eastleigh

ebbw-vale.history: Aneurin Bevan — who created the National Health Service in 1948, the greatest achievement of post-war British politics — was born in nearby Tredegar and represented Ebbw Vale from 1929 until his death in 1960. The steelworks that defined the town closed in 2002. The Festival Park and regeneration have transformed the site.
ebbw-vale.knownFor: Aneurin Bevan's constituency (the father of the NHS represented Ebbw Vale), the Festival Park (built on the steelworks site), Warm Wales and the post-industrial regeneration, and a South Wales valleys town of profound political significance
ebbw-vale.localTip: The Aneurin Bevan memorial stones at Waun-y-Pound — three enormous standing stones commemorating Nye Bevan — are extraordinary. The Festival Park for shopping. The Ebbw Vale Heritage Walk for the steelworks story.
ebbw-vale.name: Ebbw Vale

eckington.history: Eckington's Church of St Peter and St Paul contains some of the finest Norman carvings in Derbyshire — the tympanum above the south door is extraordinary. The village grew as a coal mining community in the 19th century but retains its medieval heart around the church.
eckington.knownFor: The Norman Church of St Peter and St Paul (outstanding medieval carvings), the Rother Valley, the coal mining heritage, and a north Derbyshire village with remarkable ecclesiastical treasures
eckington.localTip: The church carvings are genuinely superb and entirely overlooked. The Rother Valley cycling and walking. Chesterfield is nearby for shopping and the Crooked Spire.
eckington.name: Eckington

edenbridge.history: Edenbridge sits in the Eden Valley — a peaceful Wealden landscape. Hever Castle, just a few miles away, was the childhood home of Anne Boleyn and is one of England's most romantic castles. The town's medieval High Street has excellent independent shops.
edenbridge.knownFor: The Eden Valley, Hever Castle (the childhood home of Anne Boleyn, nearby), the High Street (one of Kent's finest), and a small Kent market town in beautiful Weald country
edenbridge.localTip: Hever Castle is magnificent — the Italian Garden is extraordinary. The Eden Valley walking. The High Street for excellent independent shops. Chartwell (Churchill's home) is also nearby.
edenbridge.name: Edenbridge

edinburgh.history: Scotland's capital since the 15th century, home to the world's oldest arts festival and a city whose medieval and Georgian architecture is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
edinburgh.knownFor: The Fringe, the Castle, whisky, Leith's restaurant scene, Arthur's Seat and year-round cultural richness
edinburgh.localTip: Leith is where Edinburgh's best restaurants are. The Royal Mile is for tourists — Stockbridge is for living.
edinburgh.name: Edinburgh

edzell.history: Edzell Castle's walled garden, created by Sir David Lindsay in 1604, is one of the most remarkable Renaissance garden enclosures in Scotland — the walls are decorated with heraldic and symbolic carvings of extraordinary sophistication. The village itself is a planned estate village of great charm.
edzell.knownFor: Edzell Castle and Walled Garden (one of Scotland's most extraordinary Renaissance garden enclosures), the Glenesk valley, the North Esk, and a small Angus village of exceptional historical and natural beauty
edzell.localTip: Edzell Castle walled garden is genuinely extraordinary — the carved wall panels are unlike anything else in Scotland. The Glenesk drive to Tarfside for the finest glen in Angus. The Glenesk Folk Museum for local history.
edzell.name: Edzell

egremont.history: Egremont's Crab Fair, held every September since 1267, is one of the oldest fairs in England. The gurning competition — pulling the ugliest face through a horse collar — has been held since the 13th century. The winner is the person who can contort their face into the most grotesque expression. The castle ruins overlook the town.
egremont.knownFor: The World Gurning Championship (the annual crab fair where contestants pull the ugliest face through a horse collar), the castle ruins, and a west Cumbrian market town with the world's most eccentric annual tradition
egremont.localTip: The Crab Fair in September is genuinely extraordinary — the gurning championship is hilarious and unlike anything else in England. The castle ruins for views. The town centre for west Cumbrian market town character.
egremont.name: Egremont

elgin.history: Elgin Cathedral, founded in 1224, was one of the finest cathedrals in Scotland — its Chapter House was rated one of Europe's greatest. In 1390 the Wolf of Badenoch burned it in revenge for excommunication. The magnificent ruin is now one of Scotland's most beautiful. The town is the capital of Moray and gateway to Speyside whisky country.
elgin.knownFor: Elgin Cathedral (the Lantern of the North, one of Scotland's greatest ruined cathedrals), the Speyside whisky trail, Moray's commercial capital, and a small Scottish city of extraordinary medieval heritage
elgin.localTip: Elgin Cathedral is free and extraordinary — the octagonal Chapter House is outstanding. The Speyside Cooperage for whisky barrel making. Pluscarden Abbey (the only working medieval monastery in Scotland) is a short drive. The town centre market.
elgin.name: Elgin

elgin-town.history: Elgin Cathedral, founded in 1224, was one of the finest in Scotland. The Wolf of Badenoch burned it in 1390. Pluscarden Abbey nearby is the only working medieval monastery in Scotland.
elgin-town.knownFor: Elgin Cathedral (the Lantern of the North), the Speyside whisky trail, and Moray's commercial capital
elgin-town.localTip: Elgin Cathedral is free and extraordinary. The Speyside Cooperage for whisky barrel making. Pluscarden Abbey is a short drive.
elgin-town.name: Elgin

elie.history: Elie is one of the most beautiful villages in the East Neuk of Fife — a string of fishing harbours on the Firth of Forth. The Ship Inn's cricket pitch on the beach, only playable at low tide, is one of Scotland's most eccentric sporting venues. The village has been a fashionable summer resort since the Victorian era.
elie.knownFor: The beach cricket (the famous Ship Inn cricket ground on the beach, played at low tide), the beautiful East Neuk harbour, the watersports, and a small Fife coastal village of extraordinary charm
elie.localTip: The Ship Inn beach cricket is genuinely eccentric and wonderful — check the tide times. The beach is excellent. The Chain Walk coastal scramble for the adventurous. The Ship Inn itself for food and atmosphere.
elie.name: Elie

elland.history: Elland grew as a textile manufacturing town on the Calder and Hebble Navigation canal. The medieval bridge over the Calder is one of the oldest in West Yorkshire. The town's mills processed cloth for centuries.
elland.knownFor: The Calder and Hebble Navigation canal, the Elland Bridge, the textile heritage, and a West Yorkshire Calder Valley town with industrial canal character
elland.localTip: The Calder and Hebble canal towpath for walking and cycling. The medieval bridge. Brighouse and Halifax are nearby for wider amenities.
elland.name: Elland

ellesmere-port.history: Ellesmere Port grew as the terminus of the Shropshire Union Canal — the canal boats unloaded here for transhipment to larger vessels on the Mersey. The National Waterways Museum (formerly the Boat Museum) preserves the extraordinary canal heritage. Stanlow oil refinery dominates the skyline.
ellesmere-port.knownFor: The Boat Museum (the world's largest collection of canal boats), the Stanlow oil refinery, the Cheshire Oaks Designer Outlet, and a Merseyside canal port with extraordinary waterway heritage
ellesmere-port.localTip: The National Waterways Museum is genuinely excellent — the largest collection of historic canal craft in the world. Cheshire Oaks Designer Outlet for shopping. The canal basin for the heritage atmosphere.
ellesmere-port.name: Ellesmere Port

ellon.history: Ellon is the home of BrewDog — the craft beer brewery founded in 2007 that transformed British craft beer culture. The BrewDog brewery and visitor centre are based here. Haddo House, an elegant 18th century mansion designed by William Adam, is a short drive away.
ellon.knownFor: BrewDog's home (Scotland's most famous craft brewery is based here), the Ythan valley, Haddo House (National Trust), and an Aberdeenshire market town growing as an Aberdeen commuter centre
ellon.localTip: BrewDog's Lone Wolf bar and brewery tours at their Ellon HQ are excellent. Haddo House for the architecture and grounds. The Ythan estuary for birdwatching. The town centre for local Aberdeenshire shopping.
ellon.name: Ellon

eltham.history: Eltham Palace was a royal residence from the 14th century — Edward IV built the magnificent Great Hall in 1479. In the 1930s, Stephen and Virginia Courtauld built an extraordinary Art Deco mansion inside the medieval shell. Bob Hope was born in Eltham in 1903, spending his early years here before emigrating to America.
eltham.knownFor: Eltham Palace (the extraordinary Art Deco mansion built inside a medieval palace), Well Hall Pleasaunce, the Bob Hope connection (the comedian was born here), and a South East London suburb with a royal medieval core
eltham.localTip: Eltham Palace is genuinely extraordinary — the 1930s Art Deco interiors alongside the medieval Great Hall are unlike anything else in England. English Heritage managed and excellent. Well Hall Pleasaunce for gardens. Bob Hope's birthplace is marked.
eltham.name: Eltham

enfield.history: Enfield Chase was a royal hunting ground — the town grew at its edge. Forty Hall, built in 1629 for Sir Nicholas Rainton, is one of the finest Jacobean mansions in north London. The Lee Valley Regional Park runs along the borough's eastern edge, providing extraordinary green space.
enfield.knownFor: Forty Hall (a magnificent 17th century mansion), the Lee Valley Regional Park, Turkey Street (immortalised in Cockney rhyming slang), and London's most northerly borough with the finest green spaces in outer London
enfield.localTip: Forty Hall is free and beautiful — the formal gardens and the house interior are excellent. The Lee Valley Regional Park for cycling, walking and watersports. Forty Hill village for a charming historic enclave. The town market.
enfield.name: Enfield

enniskillen.history: Enniskillen Castle — built on an island in the River Erne — is one of the finest surviving plantation castles in Ireland. Oscar Wilde attended Portora Royal School here. The Enniskillen bombing on Remembrance Day 1987 killed 12 people and became one of the most tragic events of the Troubles.
enniskillen.knownFor: Enniskillen Castle (with the Inniskillings Museum), the Fermanagh Lakelands (the finest lake district in Ireland), the Remembrance Day bombing of 1987, Oscar Wilde's school (Portora Royal School), and the county town of Fermanagh
enniskillen.localTip: Enniskillen Castle and the Inniskillings Museum are genuinely excellent. The Fermanagh Lakelands boat trips on Lough Erne — the ancient monastic island of Devenish is unmissable. The town centre has good independent shops.
enniskillen.name: Enniskillen

epping.history: Epping Forest — once a royal hunting forest — was saved from development by the Epping Forest Act of 1878 and given to the public by Queen Victoria. The ancient pollarded hornbeams are up to 1,000 years old. Epping town has been a market centre since the medieval period.
epping.knownFor: Epping Forest (one of London's greatest ancient woodlands, 6,000 acres of ancient pollarded trees), the Epping-Ongar Railway, the town's market heritage, and an Essex market town at the edge of the ancient forest
epping.localTip: Epping Forest is genuinely extraordinary — the ancient pollarded hornbeams are like nothing else in England. High Beech tea room for a forest stop. The Epping-Ongar heritage railway. The town market on Mondays.
epping.name: Epping

epsom.history: Epsom Salts — magnesium sulphate — were discovered in a spring on Epsom Common in 1618 and became a fashionable medicinal treatment. The Derby, first run in 1780 and named after the 12th Earl of Derby, is the most prestigious flat race in the world. The racecourse on Epsom Downs hosts it every June.
epsom.knownFor: Epsom Downs Racecourse (home of the Derby, the world's greatest flat race), Epsom Salts (discovered here in 1618), the historic town centre, and a Surrey town synonymous with horse racing excellence
epsom.localTip: Derby Day at Epsom Downs is one of England's great sporting and social spectacles — the Downs are free to watch from. The town centre for good independent shops. The North Downs walking from Epsom.
epsom.name: Epsom

erith.history: Erith was a Victorian pleasure resort on the Thames before becoming an industrial town. The pier, dating from 1842, is one of the few original Victorian pleasure piers remaining on the Thames. The town is now being transformed as part of the Thamesmead regeneration.
erith.knownFor: The Erith Pier (one of the few remaining on the Thames), the Thames estuary views, the regeneration project, and a South East London riverside town in remarkable transformation
erith.localTip: The pier for Thames views and the Victorian heritage. The riverside walk. The town is genuinely changing — interesting to witness.
erith.name: Erith

eston.history: Eston grew as a mining and ironstone village serving the Teesside iron industry. The Eston Hills — the distinctive flat ridge visible from across Teesside — were mined for ironstone that fired the Victorian industrial revolution. The Nab gives extraordinary views over the Tees estuary.
eston.knownFor: Eston Nab (the flat-topped ridge giving panoramic Teesside views), the ironstone mining heritage that helped build Victorian Britain, and a Teesside community with a remarkable natural backdrop
eston.localTip: Eston Nab for panoramic views over the Tees estuary and Cleveland Hills — one of Teesside's great free viewpoints. The Eston Hills walking trails. The community has strong local identity.
eston.name: Eston

evesham.history: The Battle of Evesham in 1265 saw Simon de Montfort — the father of the English Parliament — killed by royalist forces. The Benedictine Abbey was once one of the greatest in England. The Vale of Evesham's fruit growing tradition has supplied England for centuries.
evesham.knownFor: The Vale of Evesham fruit growing region (asparagus, plums, strawberries), the Abbey ruins, the Almonry Heritage Centre, the Battle of Evesham (1265) and a Worcestershire market town at the heart of England's finest fruit growing country
evesham.localTip: The asparagus season in May is extraordinary — buy directly from farm shops on the Vale roads. The Abbey ruins for the Battle of Evesham site. The riverside walk on the Avon.
evesham.name: Evesham

exeter.history: Isca Dumnoniorum to the Romans — the most westerly legionary fortress in Britain. Exeter's medieval cathedral, Norman castle and Roman city walls all survive. The city was badly bombed in the 1942 Baedeker raids but retained its medieval street plan.
exeter.knownFor: The Cathedral Quarter, Gandy Street, the quayside, Roman walls, the underground passages and a city that packs 2000 years of history into a tight, walkable centre
exeter.localTip: Gandy Street is Exeter's hidden gem — a narrow medieval lane packed with independent shops, cafes and restaurants that few visitors find. The underground passages are extraordinary. The quayside at dusk.
exeter.name: Exeter

eyemouth.history: The Eyemouth Disaster of 14 October 1881 killed 189 fishermen in a sudden storm — devastating the fishing community almost completely. The town rebuilt. The Eyemouth Tapestry, created in 1981, commemorates the centenary of the disaster.
eyemouth.knownFor: The Eyemouth Disaster (the worst maritime disaster in Scottish history, 1881), the fishing heritage, the Gunsgreen House, and a small Borders fishing town on the Berwickshire coast
eyemouth.localTip: The Eyemouth Museum for the disaster tapestry — one of the most moving community art projects in Scotland. Gunsgreen House (a smuggler's mansion) for history. Fresh fish from the harbour.
eyemouth.name: Eyemouth

fakenham.history: Fakenham's gas works, built in 1846, is the only surviving example in England where the complete 19th century gas-making process is preserved and open to the public. The racecourse, founded in 1905, is one of the most charming small jump racing venues in England.
fakenham.knownFor: The Fakenham Racecourse (one of England's most rural and characterful small racecourses), the Gas Museum (the only surviving Victorian town gas works open to the public), the Thursday market, and a north Norfolk market town of genuine character
fakenham.localTip: The Gas Museum is genuinely fascinating and free. The Thursday market for local Norfolk produce. The racecourse on race days for an authentic country racing experience.
fakenham.name: Fakenham

falkirk.history: Falkirk sits at the crossroads of Scottish history on the Antonine Wall. The Falkirk Wheel (2002) is the world's only rotating boat lift. The Kelpies — 30-metre steel horse heads by Andy Scott — opened in 2013 as the largest equine sculptures on earth.
falkirk.knownFor: The Falkirk Wheel (world's only rotating boat lift), the Kelpies (world's largest equine sculptures), the Antonine Wall UNESCO World Heritage Site, and central Scotland's most dramatically engineered town
falkirk.localTip: The Falkirk Wheel is extraordinary engineering — watching 600 tonnes of water rotate is unforgettable. The Kelpies at The Helix are spectacular at any time. The Antonine Wall sections for Roman history.
falkirk.name: Falkirk

falkirk-town.history: The Falkirk Wheel (2002) is the world's only rotating boat lift. The Kelpies — 30-metre steel horse heads — are the largest equine sculptures on earth. The Antonine Wall UNESCO World Heritage Site runs nearby.
falkirk-town.knownFor: The Falkirk Wheel, the Kelpies, the Antonine Wall, and central Scotland's engineering heritage
falkirk-town.localTip: The Falkirk Wheel for extraordinary rotating engineering. The Kelpies at The Helix are spectacular. Antonine Wall sections nearby.
falkirk-town.name: Falkirk

falmouth.history: Falmouth's natural harbour is the world's third deepest — after Sydney and Halifax. Pendennis Castle, built by Henry VIII, guards the harbour entrance. The town has a thriving arts scene centred on Falmouth University, and the V&A has called Falmouth one of Britain's most creative small towns.
falmouth.knownFor: The world's third deepest natural harbour, the National Maritime Museum Cornwall, Pendennis Castle, the Tall Ships events, and a Cornwall port with one of Britain's most vibrant arts and food scenes
falmouth.localTip: The National Maritime Museum Cornwall is free and world-class — the tidal zone is extraordinary. Pendennis Castle for harbour views. The Events Square and Discovery Quay for the food scene. Gyllyngvase Beach for the town beach.
falmouth.name: Falmouth

fareham.history: Fareham's distinctive red brick was used to build Buckingham Palace and many London landmarks. The town sits at the head of Portsmouth Harbour. Fort Fareham is one of the Palmerston's Forts defending Portsmouth. Titchfield Abbey nearby is a fascinating ruined medieval monastery.
fareham.knownFor: The Fareham Red Brick (used to build Buckingham Palace), the historic High Street, Fort Fareham, Titchfield Abbey, and a Hampshire market town at the head of Portsmouth Harbour
fareham.localTip: The historic High Street with Georgian and Victorian buildings is charming. Fort Fareham for military history. Titchfield Abbey for medieval ruins. Wednesday and Saturday markets for local produce.
fareham.name: Fareham

faringdon.history: Lord Berners completed the last folly to be built in England in 1935 and dyed the local doves bright colours. Great Coxwell Tithe Barn (13th century) was called the greatest piece of architecture in England by William Morris. The Uffington White Horse is nearby.
faringdon.knownFor: Faringdon Folly (the last folly built in England, 1935, with blue-dyed doves), Great Coxwell Tithe Barn (13th century, described by Morris as the greatest architecture in England), the Vale of White Horse, and an irresistibly eccentric small town
faringdon.localTip: Faringdon Folly for the Oxfordshire views and eccentric history. Great Coxwell Tithe Barn is extraordinary and free. The Uffington White Horse is a short drive. The Tuesday market.
faringdon.name: Faringdon

farnham.history: Farnham Castle was built as a palace for the Bishops of Winchester. William Cobbett — radical journalist who wrote Rural Rides — was born here in 1763. The town's Georgian town centre is one of Surrey's finest. The Crafts Study Centre holds outstanding 20th century craft collections.
farnham.knownFor: Farnham Castle (a bishops' palace since the 12th century), William Cobbett's birthplace, the Crafts Study Centre, the Georgian town centre, and a Surrey market town of exceptional architectural quality
farnham.localTip: Farnham Castle for the extraordinary bishops' palace. The Crafts Study Centre for outstanding craft collections. The town centre for excellent independent shops. The North Downs Way from Farnham for walking.
farnham.name: Farnham

farnworth.history: Farnworth grew as a cotton manufacturing town on the Manchester Bolton and Bury Canal. It sits between Bolton and Manchester with strong working-class roots in the textile and mining industries.
farnworth.knownFor: The Manchester Bolton and Bury Canal, the cotton manufacturing heritage, and a Bolton suburb with working-class community pride
farnworth.localTip: The Manchester Bolton and Bury Canal for walking and cycling. The town centre for local community shopping. Bolton is close for wider amenities.
farnworth.name: Farnworth

faversham.history: Faversham's Shepherd Neame brewery — founded in 1698 — is Britain's oldest brewery. The Chart Gunpowder Mills, the oldest gunpowder factory in the world, operated here for 400 years. The medieval market town has a remarkable concentration of listed buildings.
faversham.knownFor: Shepherd Neame brewery (Britain's oldest, brewing since 1698), the medieval market town, Oare Marshes RSPB reserve, and a Kent market town of exceptional historical character
faversham.localTip: The Shepherd Neame Brewery tour is excellent — Britain's oldest brewery tells a wonderful story. Oare Marshes RSPB for the best birdwatching in North Kent. Faversham Creek at high tide.
faversham.name: Faversham

felixstowe.history: Felixstowe Port became the UK's leading container port in the 1960s when other ports were disrupted by industrial action. The Victorian resort survives alongside the vast container terminal — creating one of England's most unusual townscapes. Landguard Fort has guarded the harbour since 1540.
felixstowe.knownFor: The UK's busiest container port (handling 40% of Britain's container trade), the Victorian seafront, Landguard Fort, and a Suffolk resort that balances industry and pleasure in extraordinary proximity
felixstowe.localTip: Landguard Fort and Landguard Point for watching the giant container ships pass just offshore — one of England's most dramatic industrial spectacles. The pier for the Victorian experience. The beach huts are excellent.
felixstowe.name: Felixstowe

fence-lancashire.history: Fence is a small village in the Ribble Valley beneath Pendle Hill. The Fence Gate Inn has established the village's reputation for outstanding food, drawing visitors from across Lancashire.
fence-lancashire.knownFor: The Fence Gate Inn (one of Lancashire's finest gastropubs), the Pendle Hill backdrop, and a small Ribble Valley village with a disproportionate food reputation
fence-lancashire.localTip: The Fence Gate Inn is genuinely outstanding — one of Lancashire's best restaurants. Pendle Hill is walkable from the village. The Ribble Valley from Fence is beautiful.
fence-lancashire.name: Fence

ferryhill.history: Ferryhill sits near the site of the Battle of Neville's Cross in 1346 — where English forces captured David II of Scotland, returning him to Edinburgh only after a massive ransom. The Dean and Chapter Colliery served the local coal mining community for over a century.
ferryhill.knownFor: The Battle of Neville's Cross nearby (1346, where David II of Scotland was captured), the Dean and Chapter Colliery heritage, and a County Durham market town serving the central Durham community
ferryhill.localTip: The Neville's Cross battle site is just outside Durham city — a short drive. The town centre for local shopping. The central Durham countryside for walking.
ferryhill.name: Ferryhill

filey.history: Filey has been a seaside resort since the Victorian era — its beach, sheltered by the Brigg headland, is one of the finest in Yorkshire. The town has retained more traditional character than many Yorkshire resorts, with independent shops and a genuine community feel. The Brigg itself is a fascinating geological feature.
filey.knownFor: Filey Brigg (the dramatic rocky headland), the beach (one of Yorkshire's finest), the Filey Dams nature reserve, and a small North Yorkshire seaside town beloved for its traditional character
filey.localTip: Filey Brigg for the headland walk — extraordinary coastal views at the tip. The beach is excellent and less crowded than Scarborough. The Filey Dams for birdwatching. The town's independent shops on Murray Street.
filey.name: Filey

finsbury-park.history: Finsbury Park opened in 1869 as the People's Park — intended for working-class Londoners without access to the Royal Parks. The park has hosted major concerts from Bob Dylan to the Rolling Stones. The neighbourhood has been home to successive immigrant communities — Irish, Turkish, West African and Latin American.
finsbury-park.knownFor: Finsbury Park (the 115-acre Victorian park and major music venue), the Arsenal FC connection, the diverse food scene on Stroud Green Road, and one of North London's most vibrant and multicultural neighbourhoods
finsbury-park.localTip: Stroud Green Road for outstanding independent food — one of North London's best food streets. The park for the music events — the summer concerts are legendary. Manor House area for the Turkish and Latin American food culture.
finsbury-park.name: Finsbury Park

fishguard.history: Fishguard was the site of the Last Invasion of Britain in February 1797 — a French force of 1,400 men landed at nearby Carreg Wastad Point but surrendered within days, partly after the legend that local Welsh women in traditional costume were mistaken for soldiers. A tapestry commemorating the invasion (like the Bayeux Tapestry) is displayed in the town.
fishguard.knownFor: The last invasion of Britain (1797, French forces surrendered at Fishguard after meeting armed local women), the ferry to Ireland (Rosslare), the Pembrokeshire Coast National Park, and a small Welsh fishing town of remarkable historical and natural significance
fishguard.localTip: The Last Invasion Tapestry at the town hall is genuinely fascinating — the detail is extraordinary. The ferry to Rosslare for the Ireland crossing. The Pembrokeshire Coast Path from Fishguard is stunning. Lower Town harbour for the traditional fishing village atmosphere.
fishguard.name: Fishguard

fivemiletown.history: Fivemiletown — traditionally five miles from Clones — is a small market village in the Clogher Valley of County Tyrone. The Clogher Valley Railway, a narrow-gauge line that served the valley from 1887 to 1941, connected communities across this rural area.
fivemiletown.knownFor: The Clogher Valley, the Clogher Valley Railway heritage, and a small County Tyrone village serving the rural Clogher Valley community
fivemiletown.localTip: The Clogher Valley countryside for cycling and walking. The village centre for local community life. The surrounding Tyrone countryside is beautiful and quiet.
fivemiletown.name: Fivemiletown

fleet.history: Fleet developed primarily as a commuter town when the railway arrived in 1847. Fleet Pond — a Site of Special Scientific Interest and Local Nature Reserve — is the largest natural freshwater lake in Hampshire, with extraordinary birdlife year-round. The Basingstoke Canal runs through the area.
fleet.knownFor: Fleet Pond (Hampshire's largest freshwater lake and nature reserve), the Basingstoke Canal, and an affluent Hampshire commuter town with excellent access to both London and the Surrey Hills
fleet.localTip: Fleet Pond is genuinely excellent for birdwatching — reed warblers, great crested grebes and kingfishers all nest here. The Basingstoke Canal towpath for walking and cycling. The town centre has improving independent food.
fleet.name: Fleet

fleetwood.history: Fleetwood was planned and built from scratch in the 1830s by local landowner Sir Peter Hesketh-Fleetwood and architect Decimus Burton — one of England's most complete planned Victorian towns. The Pharos lighthouse and Mount Pavilion are key Burton landmarks.
fleetwood.knownFor: The planned Victorian town (designed in a single vision by architect Decimus Burton), the Wyre Light (Britain's first cast iron lighthouse), the tram service to Blackpool, and a Lancashire fishing town of real historic character
fleetwood.localTip: The tram to Blackpool is one of England's great tram rides. The Monday and Friday markets for the best fish market in Lancashire. The promenade for Morecambe Bay views.
fleetwood.name: Fleetwood

flint.history: Flint Castle was the first of Edward I's ring of Welsh castles. It was at Flint in 1399 that Richard II, lured from the castle, surrendered to Henry Bolingbroke — the scene that Shakespeare dramatises in Richard II. The Dee estuary views from the castle are extraordinary.
flint.knownFor: Flint Castle (where Richard II surrendered to Bolingbroke in 1399, ending his reign), the Dee estuary, the RSPB Burton Mere wetlands, and a north-east Welsh town with a pivotal place in English royal history
flint.localTip: Flint Castle ruins are free and the estuary views are wonderful. The RSPB Burton Mere wetlands for birdwatching. The Dee estuary for walking.
flint.name: Flint

flitwick.history: Flitwick grew as a market town serving the agricultural communities of mid-Bedfordshire. Flitwick Moor is a rare surviving fragment of lowland peat bog — one of Bedfordshire's most important nature reserves. The Greensand Ridge provides walking above the town.
flitwick.knownFor: Flitwick Moor (one of Bedfordshire's finest nature reserves), the Greensand Ridge walk, and a Bedfordshire market town serving the mid-Bedfordshire community
flitwick.localTip: Flitwick Moor for the rare lowland peat bog habitat — dragonflies and unusual bog plants. The Greensand Ridge walk for excellent views. The town centre for local mid-Bedfordshire shopping.
flitwick.name: Flitwick

folkestone.history: Folkestone was a major Victorian resort and cross-Channel ferry port. The port declined when the Channel Tunnel opened in 1994. The town's regeneration, led by Roger Dickens and the Creative Foundation, has transformed the Old High Street into one of England's most vibrant arts and food destinations.
folkestone.knownFor: The Creative Quarter (one of England's most celebrated arts-led regeneration projects), the Channel Tunnel terminal nearby, the Leas Clifftop promenade, Roger Dickens's Folkestone Triennial, and a Kent port town undergoing extraordinary cultural renaissance
folkestone.localTip: The Old High Street Creative Quarter is genuinely extraordinary — independent galleries, studios, cafes and restaurants in a perfectly preserved Victorian street. The Leas Clifftop walk for Channel views. The Folkestone Triennial art installations across the town.
folkestone.name: Folkestone

fordingbridge.history: Fordingbridge's seven-arch medieval bridge over the Avon is one of Hampshire's finest. Augustus John, the celebrated Bohemian artist, lived here for the last 30 years of his life. The town sits at the northern edge of the New Forest.
fordingbridge.knownFor: The medieval bridge (seven arches over the Avon, one of Hampshire's finest), the Augustas John connection (the Bohemian artist lived here), the New Forest proximity, and a Hampshire market town on the Avon
fordingbridge.localTip: The medieval bridge at dawn is beautiful. The New Forest is literally on the doorstep — excellent walking and cycling. The Tuesday market for local produce.
fordingbridge.name: Fordingbridge

forest-hill.history: Forest Hill developed as a Victorian suburb. The Horniman Museum, opened in 1901 by tea merchant Frederick Horniman, houses extraordinary anthropological and natural history collections — and one of the world's finest musical instrument collections. The gardens are among South London's best.
forest-hill.knownFor: The Horniman Museum (free and one of London's most loved local museums), Horniman Gardens, the South Circular Road arts scene, and an increasingly creative South East London neighbourhood
forest-hill.localTip: The Horniman Museum is genuinely world-class and free — the aquarium, the walrus, the musical instruments and the gardens are all outstanding. The Devonshire Road for independent food. Honor Oak Park for community feel.
forest-hill.name: Forest Hill

forfar.history: Forfar has been the county town of Angus since medieval times. The Forfar bridie — a distinctive horseshoe-shaped pastry filled with meat and onion — has been made here since the 19th century and is one of Scotland's most recognisable regional foods. Restenneth Priory nearby is one of Scotland's earliest Christian sites.
forfar.knownFor: Forfar bridie (the distinctive Angus meat pastry), the Loch of Forfar, Restenneth Priory, and the county town of Angus with strong agricultural and food heritage
forfar.localTip: A proper Forfar bridie from a local butcher is genuinely excellent — don't confuse with Cornish pasties. The Loch of Forfar for birdwatching. Restenneth Priory for early Christian history. The town market for local Angus produce.
forfar.name: Forfar

forfar-town.history: Forfar has been Angus's county town since medieval times. The Forfar bridie — a horseshoe-shaped meat pastry — has been made here since the 19th century. Restenneth Priory is one of Scotland's earliest Christian sites.
forfar-town.knownFor: The Forfar bridie (Angus's distinctive meat pastry), the Loch of Forfar, Restenneth Priory, and the county town of Angus
forfar-town.localTip: A proper Forfar bridie from a local butcher is genuinely excellent. The Loch of Forfar for birdwatching. Restenneth Priory for early Christian history.
forfar-town.name: Forfar

forres.history: Forres has won Britain in Bloom more times than any other town — the Grant Park floral displays are extraordinary. Sueno's Stone, standing over 6 metres tall, is one of the finest Pictish carved stones in existence, depicting a battle scene in extraordinary detail. Brodie Castle (NTS) is nearby.
forres.knownFor: The Sueno's Stone (one of the tallest Pictish carved stones in Scotland), the Grant Park (Britain's most floral town — Forres wins Britain in Bloom regularly), Brodie Castle, and a beautiful Moray town of remarkable horticultural and historical excellence
forres.localTip: Grant Park in summer is genuinely extraordinary — the floral displays are world-class. Sueno's Stone for the Pictish carvings. Brodie Castle for the daffodils in spring. The Forres area for Speyside whisky day trips.
forres.name: Forres

fort-augustus.history: Fort Augustus was built as a military garrison after the 1715 Jacobite rising. The Caledonian Canal, designed by Thomas Telford and completed in 1822, passes through the village — the five-lock flight is one of the most dramatic engineering sights in Scotland. Loch Ness begins here.
fort-augustus.knownFor: The Caledonian Canal locks (a spectacular flight of five locks lifting boats from Loch Ness), Loch Ness itself, Invergarry Castle ruins, and a small Highland village at the heart of the Great Glen
fort-augustus.localTip: The five-lock flight on the Caledonian Canal is extraordinary to watch — boats going up and down through the village. The Loch Ness shoreline walk for monster-spotting. Invergarry Castle ruins for the dramatic riverside setting.
fort-augustus.name: Fort Augustus

fort-william.history: Fort William sits at the foot of Ben Nevis — Britain's highest mountain. The town was established as a garrison after the Jacobite risings. The Jacobite steam train running from Fort William to Mallaig crosses the Glenfinnan Viaduct — the most photographed railway location in the world since Harry Potter.
fort-william.knownFor: Ben Nevis (Britain's highest mountain, 1,345 metres), the Jacobite steam train (used as the Hogwarts Express in Harry Potter), the West Highland Way terminus, and the Outdoor Capital of the UK
fort-william.localTip: The Jacobite steam train is genuinely extraordinary — book well in advance. Ben Nevis for the summit (allow 7-9 hours). The West Highland Way arrives here after 96 miles. The Commando Memorial at Spean Bridge is moving.
fort-william.name: Fort William

fowey.history: Daphne du Maurier lived at Menabilly near Fowey for decades — Rebecca was set in the area. The town's harbour, with its tidal estuary and medieval quaysides, is one of Cornwall's most picturesque. Fowey has been an important port since medieval times, exporting Cornish tin and copper.
fowey.knownFor: Daphne du Maurier's home town (Rebecca and Jamaica Inn were written here), the beautiful harbour estuary, the ferry to Bodinnick, the literary connections, and a small Cornwall port of extraordinary beauty
fowey.localTip: The Bodinnick ferry for the beautiful crossing and the Hall Walk above the estuary. The harbour walk at high tide. The du Maurier Literary Festival in May. Readymoney Cove for a secluded beach.
fowey.name: Fowey

fraserburgh.history: Fraserburgh was built around the harbour by Alexander Fraser in the 16th century. Kinnaird Head Castle was converted into Scotland's first lighthouse in 1787. The town remains one of Scotland's most important fishing ports — catching and processing white fish on an enormous scale.
fraserburgh.knownFor: The Lighthouse Museum (at Scotland's oldest lighthouse, built in 1787), the fishing heritage (Scotland's busiest white fish port), Kinnaird Head Castle, and a remote Aberdeenshire fishing town of genuine maritime significance
fraserburgh.localTip: The Lighthouse Museum in Kinnaird Head Castle is genuinely excellent — Scotland's lighthouse heritage told brilliantly. The harbour for fresh fish. Cairnbulg and Inverallochy for beautiful beaches. The town's fish market for the genuine fishing industry.
fraserburgh.name: Fraserburgh

frodsham.history: Frodsham sits on the edge of the sandstone ridge that gives the town its distinctive character. The hill above the town gives extraordinary views across the Cheshire Plain and the Mersey Estuary. The town has been a market centre since medieval times — its main street retains considerable historic character.
frodsham.knownFor: Frodsham Hill (the sandstone ridge with extraordinary Cheshire views), the Mersey Estuary views, the historic main street, and a small Cheshire market town on the edge of the Mersey Valley
frodsham.localTip: Frodsham Hill for the extraordinary views across Cheshire and the Mersey — one of the finest viewpoints in the county. The main street for independent shops. The Mersey Valley walks below the hill.
frodsham.name: Frodsham

frome.history: Frome's independent town council, elected in 2011 with no party political candidates, has become a model for independent community politics across Britain. The Frome Cheese Show is one of Somerset's great agricultural events. The town's medieval Cheap Street — with a water channel running down the middle — is one of Somerset's finest.
frome.knownFor: The most independent town in England (Frome elected an independent town council with no party politics), the Cheese Show, the extraordinary independent food and arts scene, and a Somerset market town that's become a byword for radical community spirit
frome.localTip: Cheap Street with its central water channel is extraordinary. The Frome Cheese Show in September is excellent. The independent shops and food are outstanding. The River Frome walk. Catherine Hill for independent retail.
frome.name: Frome

fulham.history: Fulham has been London's riverside village since medieval times — the Bishop of London's palace has stood here since the 8th century. Craven Cottage, Fulham FC's ground, dates from 1780 and is the most picturesque football ground in the Premier League. The area became affluent in the late 20th century.
fulham.knownFor: Craven Cottage (Fulham FC's beautiful Victorian riverside ground), the Bishop's Park and Palace, the Thames riverside walks, Parsons Green, and one of West London's most desirable riverside neighbourhoods
fulham.localTip: Craven Cottage for a Fulham FC match — the riverside ground with a neutral terrace is genuinely one of football's great experiences. Bishop's Park for the riverside and the palace. The New Kings Road for excellent independent food.
fulham.name: Fulham

gainsborough.history: Gainsborough Old Hall, built in 1480, is one of the finest surviving medieval manor houses in England — Henry VIII visited with Katherine Howard. The Trent's tidal bore — the Aegir — is one of England's most dramatic natural phenomena. The Pilgrim Fathers movement had strong local roots.
gainsborough.knownFor: The Old Hall (one of England's finest surviving medieval manor houses, visited by Henry VIII and Katherine Howard), the tidal bore on the Trent, the Pilgrims' connection, and a Lincolnshire market town of real historical depth
gainsborough.localTip: Gainsborough Old Hall is extraordinary and remarkably intact — one of England's great medieval buildings. The Aegir tidal bore in the right conditions is extraordinary. The Tuesday and Saturday markets.
gainsborough.name: Gainsborough

galashiels.history: Galashiels has been producing textiles since the 13th century. The Borders Railway reopening in 2015 reconnected the town to Edinburgh for the first time since 1969.
galashiels.knownFor: The Scottish Borders textile capital (cashmere and tweed), the Borders Railway (reopened 2015), and a mill town that's been weaving since the 13th century
galashiels.localTip: The Heriot Watt Textiles campus. The Borders Railway makes a day trip from Edinburgh easy. Scott's View for the Tweed valley panorama.
galashiels.name: Galashiels

garstang.history: Garstang declared itself the world's first Fairtrade town in April 2000 — a campaign that began in the local church and spread to become a global movement, with over 2,000 Fairtrade towns and communities worldwide following the model. The Lancaster Canal passes through the town. Greenhalgh Castle, a ruined 14th century fortress, sits above the town.
garstang.knownFor: The world's first Fairtrade town (declared in 2000, the template for thousands of Fairtrade towns worldwide), the Lancaster Canal, Greenhalgh Castle ruins, and a small Lancashire market town of global ethical significance
garstang.localTip: The Fairtrade story is celebrated in the town — the movement that changed global trade started here. The Lancaster Canal towpath for walking and cycling. Greenhalgh Castle ruins for history. The Thursday market for local Lancashire produce.
garstang.name: Garstang

gateshead.history: Gateshead's transformation from industrial town to cultural destination is one of England's great regeneration stories. The Angel of the North, erected in 1998, became an instantly beloved national icon. The Baltic — opened in a converted flour mill in 2002 — and the Sage music venue have made the Quayside one of Britain's great cultural destinations.
gateshead.knownFor: The Angel of the North (Antony Gormley's iconic steel sculpture), the Baltic Centre for Contemporary Art, the Sage Gateshead music venue, the Millennium Bridge, and the artistic twin of Newcastle across the Tyne
gateshead.localTip: The Angel of the North at dawn or dusk is extraordinary. The Baltic is free and has exceptional contemporary art. The Sage for world-class music. The Gateshead Millennium Bridge (the only tilting bridge in the world) for the Tyne views.
gateshead.name: Gateshead

giffnock.history: Giffnock developed as a wealthy Victorian and Edwardian suburb for Glasgow merchants. East Renfrewshire is consistently ranked as Scotland's most affluent local authority area. The suburb retains a distinct village feel despite being effectively in Glasgow's southern suburbs.
giffnock.knownFor: East Renfrewshire's most affluent suburb, the Rouken Glen Park, excellent independent shopping on Fenwick Road, and Glasgow's most prosperous commuter village
giffnock.localTip: Rouken Glen Park is beautiful — the waterfall and boating lake are lovely. Fenwick Road for excellent independent shops and cafes. The East Renfrewshire countryside for cycling.
giffnock.name: Giffnock

gillingham-kent.history: Gillingham forms part of the Medway conurbation with Chatham and Rochester. The Royal Engineers Museum tells the extraordinary story of the Sapper corps — the engineers who built Empire infrastructure worldwide. The Medway towns have strong naval and military heritage running through their identity.
gillingham-kent.knownFor: The Medway Historic Dockyard (Royal Engineers Museum), Chatham's maritime connection, the Medway towns' collective character, and a Kent town with deep naval and military heritage
gillingham-kent.localTip: The Royal Engineers Museum is excellent and free — one of the finest military engineering museums in Britain. The Medway waterfront walk. The Pentagon Centre for local shopping. The Strand Leisure Park for families.
gillingham-kent.name: Gillingham

girvan.history: Girvan sits on the Firth of Clyde with Ailsa Craig — the 1,114-foot volcanic plug — dominating the view. The granite from Ailsa Craig makes the world's finest curling stones. Turnberry — one of the world's great golf courses — is just north of the town. The harbour has been fishing since medieval times.
girvan.knownFor: Ailsa Craig (the dramatic volcanic plug visible from Girvan, source of the world's finest curling stones), the Turnberry golf course nearby, the harbour, and a South Ayrshire fishing town with an extraordinary backdrop
girvan.localTip: The Ailsa Craig view from Girvan harbour is extraordinary — particularly at sunrise or sunset. Boat trips to Ailsa Craig for the gannet colony (100,000 gannets breed here). Turnberry for the golf legacy. The harbour for fresh fish.
girvan.name: Girvan

glasgow.history: Built on shipbuilding and trade, Glasgow became one of the Victorian era's greatest cities. Its people have turned post-industrial decline into cultural renaissance
glasgow.knownFor: The Barras Market, Finnieston's restaurant strip, the Burrell Collection, the Mackintosh buildings and genuine world-class hospitality
glasgow.localTip: Finnieston on a Saturday evening. The Barras on a Sunday morning. Dennistoun if you want to see what's coming next.
glasgow.name: Glasgow

glastonbury.history: Glastonbury Abbey was one of the most important monasteries in medieval England — and the claimed burial place of King Arthur and Guinevere. The Tor has been a place of pilgrimage since Celtic times. The Glastonbury Festival, begun on Michael Eavis's farm in 1970, has grown into the world's most celebrated outdoor music event.
glastonbury.knownFor: The Music Festival (the world's most famous), the Tor (a mystical hilltop landmark), the Abbey ruins (claimed as the burial place of King Arthur), the New Age community, and a Somerset market town of extraordinary spiritual and cultural significance
glastonbury.localTip: The Tor at dawn or sunset is genuinely magical — the climb is easy and the views extraordinary. The Abbey ruins are atmospheric. The High Street for the extraordinary mix of crystal shops and genuine old market town. The Festival tickets sell out immediately.
glastonbury.name: Glastonbury

glossop.history: Glossop grew as a cotton mill town in the 19th century under the Duke of Norfolk's patronage. The old market cross survives from the medieval settlement. The Snake Pass connects the town to Sheffield. Bleaklow and Kinder Scout — England's wildest moorland — rise immediately above.
glossop.knownFor: Bleaklow and Kinder Scout (the wild Dark Peak on the doorstep), the Victorian mill town heritage, the Snake Pass, and a Derbyshire mill town between Manchester and the High Peak
glossop.localTip: The Dark Peak is genuinely accessible from Glossop — Bleaklow is one of the finest wild walks in England. The Snake Pass drive is extraordinary. Old Glossop village for the historic character.
glossop.name: Glossop

gloucester.history: Glevum to the Romans — a major legionary fortress. Gloucester's Cathedral dates from the 11th century; the 15th century cloisters were used as Hogwarts in the Harry Potter films. The Victorian docks are now a heritage quarter.
gloucester.knownFor: The Cathedral (used as Hogwarts in the Harry Potter films), the Docks, the National Waterways Museum and a Roman city whose street plan survives intact
gloucester.localTip: The Cathedral cloisters are extraordinary — even if you've never watched Harry Potter. The Docks for National Waterways Museum and independent food. The indoor market for local produce.
gloucester.name: Gloucester

godalming.history: Godalming lit its streets by electricity in 1881 — the first town in the world to do so. The Godalming Navigation — part of the Wey Navigation — is one of England's oldest canal systems, dating from 1653. Charterhouse School, one of Britain's great public schools, moved here from London in 1872.
godalming.knownFor: The first town in the world to have electric street lighting (1881), the Godalming Navigation canal, Charterhouse School, the River Wey, and a Surrey market town of genuine historical significance and beauty
godalming.localTip: The Godalming Navigation for the most beautiful towpath walking in Surrey. The High Street for excellent independent shops. The Pepperpot (the old Town Hall) for the town's history. The Wey for kayaking.
godalming.name: Godalming

gosport.history: Gosport faces Portsmouth across the harbour and has been inextricably linked with the Royal Navy for centuries. Fort Brockhurst is one of Palmerston's Forts. The Royal Navy Submarine Museum houses HMS Alliance — the only preserved Cold War submarine in Britain — and tells the extraordinary story of submarine warfare.
gosport.knownFor: The Royal Navy Submarine Museum (with HMS Alliance, the only preserved Cold War submarine in Britain), Fort Brockhurst, the Gosport Ferry, and a Hampshire peninsula town with extraordinary naval heritage
gosport.localTip: The Royal Navy Submarine Museum is genuinely outstanding — going inside HMS Alliance is an extraordinary experience. Fort Brockhurst for the Victorian engineering. The Gosport Ferry for the Portsmouth Harbour crossing. The High Street market.
gosport.name: Gosport

grantham.history: Margaret Thatcher, Britain's first female Prime Minister, was born above her father's grocery shop in Grantham in 1925. Isaac Newton attended King's School Grantham in the 17th century. The Angel and Royal Hotel, dating from the 14th century, hosted Richard III.
grantham.knownFor: Margaret Thatcher's birthplace (and the Thatcher statue controversy), Isaac Newton's school (King's School), the Angel and Royal Inn (one of England's oldest hotels), and a Lincolnshire market town of real historical significance
grantham.localTip: The Thatcher statue is a talking point. The Museum for the Newton and Thatcher stories. The Angel and Royal for a coffee and the medieval architecture. The market on Saturdays.
grantham.name: Grantham

grantown-on-spey.history: Grantown-on-Spey was designed and built by Sir James Grant in 1765 — one of Scotland's finest planned towns, with a central square and radiating streets. The town served as the commercial centre for Strathspey and is the gateway to the Cairngorms from the north.
grantown-on-spey.knownFor: The planned Georgian town (laid out in a single vision in 1765 by Sir James Grant), the River Spey salmon fishing, the Cairngorms on the doorstep, and one of Scotland's finest planned market towns
grantown-on-spey.localTip: The Spey for salmon fishing — one of Scotland's great rivers. The Cairngorms from Grantown give the widest perspective on the National Park. The town square is beautiful.
grantown-on-spey.name: Grantown On Spey

gravesend.history: Pocahontas, the Native American princess who came to England in 1616, died at Gravesend and is buried at St George's Church — the only known burial place of a Native American in England. Every ship entering the Thames picks up a river pilot at Gravesend.
gravesend.knownFor: Pocahontas (buried at St George's Church), the river pilots who guide every ship up the Thames, the Rosherville Gardens heritage, the ferry to Tilbury, and a Kent riverside town with extraordinary historical connections
gravesend.localTip: The Pocahontas statue in St George's churchyard. The Thames waterfront for extraordinary river traffic watching. The Tilbury ferry crossing. The town's multicultural Sikh community and their extraordinary temple.
gravesend.name: Gravesend

great-dunmow.history: The Dunmow Flitch Trials have been held since the 12th century — couples must convince a jury of six maidens and six bachelors that they have not argued or wished themselves unmarried for a year and a day. The winner receives a side of bacon. The trials were the inspiration for the phrase 'bringing home the bacon'.
great-dunmow.knownFor: The Flitch of Bacon (the world's longest-running competition — couples must prove they haven't argued for a year), the market town heritage, and a small Essex market town with an irresistible medieval tradition
great-dunmow.localTip: The Dunmow Flitch Trials, held every four years, are genuinely extraordinary and entertaining. The market town centre has good independent shops. The surrounding Essex countryside for walking.
great-dunmow.name: Great Dunmow

great-harwood.history: John Mercer — born in Great Harwood — invented the mercerisation process in 1844, which transforms cotton into the lustrous, strong fabric still used worldwide. The Mercer Park and Gallery commemorate this local inventor who changed the textile industry globally. The town grew as a cotton weaving centre.
great-harwood.knownFor: The Hyndburn district market town, the John Mercer connection (the inventor of mercerisation, the cotton treatment process), the Mercer Art Gallery, and a small Lancashire town with global industrial significance
great-harwood.localTip: The Mercer Art Gallery in Mercer Park is worth visiting. Mercer Park itself is lovely. The market days for local Lancashire produce. The town's pride in John Mercer is genuine.
great-harwood.name: Great Harwood

great-yarmouth.history: Great Yarmouth was one of medieval England's most important ports — the herring capital of Europe. The medieval Rows — 145 narrow lanes between two main streets — are the most extensive surviving medieval street pattern in England. Dickens set parts of David Copperfield here.
great-yarmouth.knownFor: The Golden Mile (one of England's finest seaside amusement strips), the medieval row houses, the herring fishing heritage, the Time and Tide Museum, and a Norfolk seaside town of real historic depth beyond the candyfloss
great-yarmouth.localTip: The Time and Tide Museum tells the herring and maritime story brilliantly. The medieval Rows are extraordinary and most visitors miss them entirely. The beach and Golden Mile for the seaside experience.
great-yarmouth.name: Great Yarmouth

greenock.history: James Watt was born in Greenock in 1736. His development of the steam engine changed the world. Greenock grew as a major shipbuilding and sugar refining centre on the Clyde.
greenock.knownFor: The birthplace of James Watt (the steam engine pioneer), the Cut Victorian reservoir walk, the Firth of Clyde views, and an Inverclyde town with a powerful industrial history
greenock.localTip: The Cut walk — a Victorian-era water channel cut into the Clyde hills — is extraordinary and few people know it. The Clyde views from above the town.
greenock.name: Greenock

greenwich.history: Greenwich was the birthplace of Henry VIII and Elizabeth I. The Royal Observatory, built by Wren in 1675, established Greenwich as the world's prime meridian in 1884 — setting GMT as global time. The Royal Naval College — Wren's greatest secular building — dominates the riverside. The Cutty Sark, the world's last surviving tea clipper, is preserved here.
greenwich.knownFor: The Royal Observatory (home of the Prime Meridian, Greenwich Mean Time and the Longitude Prize), the Cutty Sark, the National Maritime Museum, the Royal Naval College (Wren's masterpiece), and a UNESCO World Heritage Site
greenwich.localTip: Stand on the Prime Meridian at the Royal Observatory — straddling time zones is genuinely fun. The Cutty Sark is extraordinary up close. The Royal Naval College is free to walk through. Greenwich Market for independent food. The park for the London skyline view.
greenwich.name: Greenwich

gretna.history: Scotland's more permissive marriage laws allowed couples eloping from England to marry at Gretna Green from 1754. The Famous Blacksmiths Shop — where the blacksmith performed irregular marriages over the anvil — became the world's most famous wedding venue. Over 5,000 weddings still take place here annually.
gretna.knownFor: Gretna Green (the world's most famous wedding destination since 1754), the Famous Blacksmiths Shop, the border between England and Scotland, and a small Scottish village with a global romantic reputation
gretna.localTip: The Famous Blacksmiths Shop is genuinely fun — the anvil marriages and the romantic heritage are celebrated well. The border crossing itself is worth a stop. The outlet shopping centre for retail. The Solway Firth views.
gretna.name: Gretna

grimsby.history: Grimsby was once the world's greatest fishing port — in the 1950s and 1960s more fish passed through Grimsby than anywhere on earth. The Cod Wars with Iceland in the 1970s ended the distant water fishing industry. The town has been reinventing itself ever since, now home to the offshore wind energy industry.
grimsby.knownFor: The UK's largest fishing port heritage, the National Fishing Heritage Centre, the Fridge (Europe's largest chilled distribution hub), and a North East Lincolnshire town reinventing itself from its fishing industry past
grimsby.localTip: The National Fishing Heritage Centre is excellent — the story of the Grimsby trawlermen is genuinely moving. The No. 1 Fishing Heritage Centre. The fish market for fresh local seafood. The offshore wind industry transformation.
grimsby.name: Grimsby

guildford.history: Guildford's High Street follows a Roman road and the town was an important royal centre in the medieval period. Lewis Carroll was born nearby and is buried in the Mount Cemetery
guildford.knownFor: The medieval High Street, the ruins of the castle, the cathedral, the University of Surrey and a shopping and dining scene that serves one of England's most prosperous counties
guildford.localTip: Walk the High Street for the cobbles and medieval buildings. The castle grounds for views. The Yvonne Arnaud Theatre for culture. The market on North Street Thursday and Saturday.
guildford.name: Guildford

guiseley.history: Harry Ramsden opened his first fish and chip shop in a wooden hut in Guiseley in 1928, eventually building it into the world's most famous fish and chip restaurant. The town sits at the gateway to the Yorkshire Dales.
guiseley.knownFor: Harry Ramsden's fish and chip shop (where the world-famous chain began in 1928), the Wharfedale setting, and a West Yorkshire town at the edge of the Dales
guiseley.localTip: The Harry Ramsden's in Guiseley is genuinely worth visiting for the heritage. The Wharfedale walks from town. Ilkley Moor is nearby. The town has good independent food.
guiseley.name: Guiseley

hackney.history: Hackney has been transformed over the past two decades from one of London's most deprived boroughs into one of its most creative. The 2012 Olympics legacy at Stratford borders the borough. The area has attracted artists, musicians and creative businesses who have built extraordinary institutions from Arcola Theatre to Brilliant Corners.
hackney.knownFor: Broadway Market (one of London's most celebrated independent markets), London Fields, the extraordinary arts and creative scene, Victoria Park, and one of London's most dynamically creative and culturally diverse boroughs
hackney.localTip: Broadway Market on Saturdays is one of London's great market experiences — independent food, produce and creative businesses at their best. London Fields for the lido in summer. Victoria Park for the east London countryside. Ridley Road for the real market.
hackney.name: Hackney

hailsham.history: Hailsham was famous for its rope-making industry — supplying rope to the Royal Navy for centuries. The town sits in the Sussex Weald. Michelham Priory, one of England's most complete small monasteries, is nearby.
hailsham.knownFor: The rope-making heritage (Hailsham was the rope-making capital of England), the Cuckoo Trail (14 miles of level cycling on an old railway), the Michelham Priory, and an East Sussex market town serving the Sussex Weald
hailsham.localTip: The Cuckoo Trail for cycling — flat, traffic-free and beautiful. Michelham Priory is excellent. The Hailsham market on Wednesdays and Saturdays for local Sussex produce.
hailsham.name: Hailsham

halesworth.history: Halesworth's Thoroughfare — a pedestrianised medieval street — is one of the finest examples of a traditional market town street in East Anglia. The town grew as a market centre for the Blyth Valley agricultural community.
halesworth.knownFor: The Halesworth Gallery (one of Suffolk's finest), the Thoroughfare (one of East Anglia's finest pedestrianised shopping streets), the River Blyth, and a small Suffolk market town of exceptional independent character
halesworth.localTip: The Thoroughfare for independent shopping and food. The Halesworth Gallery for culture. The River Blyth walk. The Wednesday market for local Suffolk produce.
halesworth.name: Halesworth

halifax.history: The Piece Hall, built in 1779, is the only surviving Georgian cloth hall in England — its 315 rooms arranged around a vast courtyard where handloom weavers sold their cloth. Halifax grew as a centre of wool and worsted manufacture. The town's post-industrial regeneration, centred on the Piece Hall, has been extraordinary.
halifax.knownFor: The Piece Hall (the most magnificent Georgian cloth trading hall in England), Eureka! The National Children's Museum, the Banksy-inspired street art, the Dean Clough mills, and a West Yorkshire town of remarkable reinvention
halifax.localTip: The Piece Hall is genuinely magnificent — a free Georgian masterpiece restored to its former glory. The rooftop bar has extraordinary views. Eureka! for children. The Dean Clough mills for galleries and independent businesses.
halifax.name: Halifax

halstead.history: Halstead's Courtauld silk mill dominated the town for over a century — the same Courtauld family that later became the great art patrons. The town sits in the beautiful Colne Valley beneath Hedingham Castle, one of England's finest Norman keeps.
halstead.knownFor: The Courtauld silk mill (now luxury flats but one of Victorian England's most important industrial buildings), the Colne Valley, the Hedingham Castle proximity, and an Essex market town on the beautiful Colne Valley
halstead.localTip: Hedingham Castle is extraordinary — a Norman keep in near-perfect condition. The Colne Valley walk through to Colchester. The weekly market for local Essex produce.
halstead.name: Halstead

haltwhistle.history: Haltwhistle claims to be the geographical centre of Britain — though this claim is disputed. The town sits at the heart of Hadrian's Wall country, with some of the finest sections of the Wall — Housesteads, Vindolanda, Cawfields — within easy reach. The South Tyne railway passes through on its way from Carlisle.
haltwhistle.knownFor: The Centre of Britain (Haltwhistle claims to be the geographical centre of Britain), Hadrian's Wall (the best sections pass through), the South Tyne railway, and a small Northumberland town at the heart of the Roman frontier
haltwhistle.localTip: Housesteads Roman Fort — the best-preserved on Hadrian's Wall — is close by. Vindolanda for the extraordinary writing tablets. Cawfields for the best-preserved milecastle. The Centre of Britain pub for the geographic claim.
haltwhistle.name: Haltwhistle

hamilton.history: Hamilton grew as the principal town of South Lanarkshire. The Chatelherault hunting lodge was designed by William Adam in 1732. The racecourse has been a fixture since 1888.
hamilton.knownFor: Hamilton Racecourse, Chatelherault Country Park (the extraordinary William Adam hunting lodge), and the commercial hub of South Lanarkshire
hamilton.localTip: Chatelherault Country Park is extraordinary — the William Adam pavilion and the Avon gorge walks are spectacular. The racecourse for summer meetings.
hamilton.name: Hamilton

hampton.history: Hampton Court Palace — begun by Cardinal Wolsey in 1515 and seized by Henry VIII — is one of England's finest Tudor buildings. The Great Hall, the Tudor kitchens and the maze are extraordinary. Bushy Park, with its chestnut avenue and red and fallow deer, is one of the royal parks least visited by tourists.
hampton.knownFor: Hampton Court Palace (Henry VIII's magnificent Tudor palace), Bushy Park (one of London's finest royal parks with red deer herds), the Thames riverside, and a South West London community adjacent to one of England's greatest royal residences
hampton.localTip: Hampton Court Palace is magnificent — the Tudor kitchens, the Great Hall and the maze are the highlights. Bushy Park at dawn for the red deer herds — extraordinary and rarely crowded. The Thames Path from Hampton for beautiful riverside walking.
hampton.name: Hampton

harlech.history: Harlech Castle, built by Edward I between 1282 and 1289, sits on a rocky outcrop with sheer cliffs dropping to what was once the sea below. It is one of the finest examples of concentric castle design in the world, and was the last castle in Wales to fall in the Wars of the Roses. The song 'Men of Harlech' commemorates its 1461 siege.
harlech.knownFor: Harlech Castle (UNESCO World Heritage Site, the most dramatically sited castle in Wales), the Royal St David's Golf Club, Cardigan Bay views, and a small Welsh village of extraordinary historical and natural beauty
harlech.localTip: The castle at sunset is one of Wales's great views — the Snowdonia and Cardigan Bay panorama is extraordinary. Royal St David's Golf Course runs beneath the castle. The beach at Harlech Sands is magnificent and usually uncrowded.
harlech.name: Harlech

harlow.history: Harlow was designated a New Town in 1947 and its planners took public art uniquely seriously — commissioning sculptures from Henry Moore, Barbara Hepworth and others for public spaces. The result is one of England's finest collections of modern public sculpture.
harlow.knownFor: Henry Moore's sculptures (Harlow has one of England's finest public sculpture collections, commissioned by the New Town), the Gibberd Gallery, the Parndon Wood, and a post-war New Town that took public art seriously
harlow.localTip: The Moore sculpture trail is free and rewarding — the town centre and Mark Hall cycle park have significant works. The Gibberd Garden is extraordinary. The market on Tuesdays and Thursdays.
harlow.name: Harlow

harrogate.history: A spa town that grew around the sulphur springs discovered in the late 16th century. The Victorians built it into one of England's most elegant resorts. The International Conference Centre made it a business destination.
harrogate.knownFor: The Pump Room, the Valley Gardens, Bettys Tearoom, the RHS Harlow Carr gardens and one of England's most genteel spa towns with excellent independent shopping
harrogate.localTip: Bettys on a weekday morning rather than a weekend. The Montpellier Quarter for independent shopping. Valley Gardens are free and beautiful. The Turkish Baths are extraordinary.
harrogate.name: Harrogate

harrow.history: Harrow School, founded in 1572, educated Winston Churchill, Lord Byron, Robert Peel, Jawaharlal Nehru and countless other world-shapers. Harrow on the Hill retains its character as a proper English village — church, pub, school and Georgian houses on a hilltop — entirely surrounded by London.
harrow.knownFor: Harrow School (one of England's great public schools, alma mater of Churchill, Byron and Nehru), Harrow on the Hill (a remarkably preserved hilltop village within London), the South Asian community, and one of London's most diverse outer boroughs
harrow.localTip: Harrow on the Hill is extraordinary — the school buildings, the church, the views over London from the churchyard. The Harrow School Chapel for the Churchill connection. St Annes Road for excellent South Asian food. The Wednesday market for produce.
harrow.name: Harrow

hartlepool.history: The Hartlepool Monkey legend — that locals hanged a shipwrecked monkey during the Napoleonic Wars, believing it to be a French spy — has defined the town's identity for 200 years (and Hartlepool United's mascot is H'Angus the Monkey). HMS Trincomalee, built in 1817, is Britain's oldest floating warship and is preserved at the Historic Quay.
hartlepool.knownFor: The Hartlepool Monkey (the legend that locals hanged a shipwrecked monkey as a French spy), the Historic Quay (an extraordinary reconstructed 18th century waterfront), HMS Trincomalee (Britain's oldest warship), and a County Durham port town of remarkable naval heritage
hartlepool.localTip: The Historic Quay is genuinely excellent — an immersive recreation of an 18th century port. HMS Trincomalee is extraordinary. The marina for modern contrast. Seaton Carew beach for the traditional seaside.
hartlepool.name: Hartlepool

harwich.history: Harwich's Electric Palace Cinema (1911) is the oldest surviving purpose-built cinema in Britain. The Mayflower, which carried the Pilgrim Fathers to America, was built and partially crewed from Harwich. The Georgian town plan of the old port is preserved.
harwich.knownFor: The ferry port to Netherlands and Denmark, the Electric Palace Cinema (Britain's oldest surviving purpose-built cinema), the Mayflower connections (the Pilgrim Fathers' ship was built here), and a preserved Georgian port town of remarkable character
harwich.localTip: The Electric Palace Cinema is extraordinary — still showing films in a perfectly preserved 1911 building. The Ha'penny Pier for harbour views. The Treadwheel Crane in the yard.
harwich.name: Harwich

haslingden.history: Haslingden grew as a cotton weaving town in the Rossendale Valley. The Halo — a stunning illuminated walkway on the hillside above the town — was created as a regeneration art project and has become one of Lancashire's most distinctive landmarks.
haslingden.knownFor: The Halo (the illuminated LED walkway on Haslingden Grane that's one of Lancashire's most unexpected public artworks), the Rossendale Valley, the East Lancashire Railway, and a Lancashire mill town on the edge of the Pennines
haslingden.localTip: The Halo at dusk is extraordinary and completely free. The East Lancashire Railway at Rawtenstall. The Rossendale Valley walks from the town.
haslingden.name: Haslingden

hastings.history: The Battle of Hastings in 1066 — though actually fought at Battle, 6 miles inland — changed England forever. Hastings Castle, built by William the Conqueror, overlooks the town. The Old Town's net shops (tall black wooden huts for storing fishing nets) are unique. Hastings has become a significant arts destination.
hastings.knownFor: The Battle of Hastings 1066 (the most important battle in English history), Hastings Castle, the Old Town fishing stalls (Europe's largest beach-launched fishing fleet), the arts scene, and a Sussex coast town of profound historical significance
hastings.localTip: The Old Town for fresh fish directly from the beach fishermen — extraordinarily fresh. The net shops are extraordinary and unique. The Jerwood Gallery for excellent contemporary art. The West Hill for cliff railway and castle views.
hastings.name: Hastings
