letchworth.history: Letchworth Garden City, founded in 1903 by Ebenezer Howard following his visionary 1898 book 'To-morrow: A Peaceful Path to Real Reform', was the world's first garden city. Its principles of combining town and country, green spaces and planned streets influenced urban planning across the globe. The Broadway Cinema, built in 1936, is Britain's oldest purpose-built cinema still operating.
letchworth.knownFor: The world's first Garden City (founded 1903 by Ebenezer Howard), the Broadway Cinema (the first purpose-built cinema in Britain), the Norton Common, and a Hertfordshire new town that became the template for planned cities worldwide
letchworth.localTip: The First Garden City Heritage Museum tells the story of this genuinely world-changing urban experiment. Norton Common for walking and nature. The Broadway Cinema is extraordinary and still showing films. The town centre arts and crafts architecture.
letchworth.name: Letchworth Garden City

letchworth-garden-city.history: Founded 1903 by Ebenezer Howard, Letchworth was the world's first garden city — combining town and country living with green spaces. Its principles influenced urban planning globally. The Broadway Cinema (1936) is Britain's oldest purpose-built cinema still operating.
letchworth-garden-city.knownFor: The world's first Garden City (1903), the Broadway Cinema (Britain's oldest purpose-built cinema), and the urban planning template that influenced cities worldwide
letchworth-garden-city.localTip: The First Garden City Heritage Museum for the world-changing story. Norton Common for walking. The Broadway Cinema is extraordinary and still showing films.
letchworth-garden-city.name: Letchworth Garden City

leven.history: Leven sits at the mouth of the River Leven on the Firth of Forth. The town served the Methil docks — once important for coal export. The Fife Coastal Path passes through on its way to the beautiful East Neuk villages. The Levenmouth railway line is being reinstated.
leven.knownFor: The Levenmouth area, the Methil docks heritage, the Fife Coastal Path, and a Fife town on the Firth of Forth serving the East Neuk gateway
leven.localTip: The Fife Coastal Path from Leven towards the East Neuk is excellent walking. The beach at Leven. Methil docks history. The reinstatement of the railway is transforming connectivity.
leven.name: Leven

lewes.history: The Battle of Lewes in 1264 established the principle of parliamentary government in England. Tom Paine, who inspired the American and French Revolutions, lived here. The Bonfire Night celebrations — with effigies and massive torchlit processions — have been held since the 1850s.
lewes.knownFor: The Bonfire Night celebrations (the most spectacular in England, with six rival societies), the Castle, the Harvey's Brewery (one of England's finest independent breweries), Tom Paine's town, and a county town of real intellectual character
lewes.localTip: Bonfire Night in Lewes is one of England's great spectacles — extraordinary and unlike anywhere else. Harvey's Brewery tap for the finest Sussex ale. The castle and the town for independent shopping.
lewes.name: Lewes

lewisham.history: Lewisham has been home to successive waves of communities — Caribbeans, West Africans and more recently young professionals. The market has been the commercial heart since medieval times. The borough has some of the most diverse communities in London and a strong tradition of political activism.
lewisham.knownFor: The Lewisham market (one of South London's most vibrant), the diverse food scene, Hilly Fields park, the DLR connection, and a South East London borough of great community spirit and rapid change
lewisham.localTip: The covered market and street market on the High Street are excellent — diverse food and community feel. Hilly Fields for the park and views. Blackheath for the beautiful common. The DLR and National Rail for good connectivity.
lewisham.name: Lewisham

leyburn.history: Leyburn is the main market town for lower Wensleydale. Bolton Castle, 5 miles away, was where Mary Queen of Scots was imprisoned for six months in 1568. The Shawl — a natural terrace above the town — provides extraordinary views across the Dales.
leyburn.knownFor: The gateway to Wensleydale (the cheese valley), the Shawl (a remarkable ridgetop promenade walk), Bolton Castle (where Mary Queen of Scots was imprisoned), and a small North Yorkshire market town of great Dales beauty
leyburn.localTip: The Shawl walk above the town is free and extraordinary. Bolton Castle for Mary Queen of Scots. The Friday market for Wensleydale produce. Wensleydale Creamery at Hawes for the cheese.
leyburn.name: Leyburn

leyland.history: Leyland Motors — founded here in 1896 — manufactured the iconic London Routemaster bus and became one of the world's largest vehicle manufacturers before becoming British Leyland. The British Commercial Vehicle Museum preserves this extraordinary heritage. The town grew entirely around vehicle manufacturing.
leyland.knownFor: The British Commercial Vehicle Museum (the finest truck and bus museum in Britain), the Leyland Motors heritage (the company that built the London bus), and a South Lancashire town defined by its vehicle manufacturing legacy
leyland.localTip: The British Commercial Vehicle Museum is genuinely excellent — the history of British buses and trucks told through extraordinary vehicles. The town centre market. Preston is nearby for wider amenities.
leyland.name: Leyland

lichfield.history: Lichfield Cathedral — the only medieval English cathedral with three spires — has dominated the city since the 13th century. Dr Samuel Johnson, the dictionary-maker and father of modern English literature, was born here in 1709. The city was an important royalist centre in the Civil War.
lichfield.knownFor: The Cathedral (the only medieval cathedral in England with three spires), Samuel Johnson's birthplace, the Lichfield Gospels, the Bower Day civic festival, and a Staffordshire cathedral city of great historic significance
lichfield.localTip: The Cathedral is magnificent and free. The Samuel Johnson Birthplace Museum is excellent. The market square for independent food. The Bower Day in May for civic pageantry.
lichfield.name: Lichfield

lichfield-town.history: Lichfield Cathedral — with its three soaring spires known as 'the Ladies of the Vale' — is one of England's most beautiful. Samuel Johnson, author of the first English dictionary, was born in Market Square in 1709. The Lichfield Gospels, an 8th century illuminated manuscript, is one of Britain's greatest treasures.
lichfield-town.knownFor: Lichfield Cathedral (the only medieval English cathedral with three spires), the birthplace of Samuel Johnson, the Lichfield Gospels, and a beautiful Staffordshire city of great ecclesiastical and literary heritage
lichfield-town.localTip: The Cathedral Close is extraordinary — the three spires from any angle are magnificent. The Samuel Johnson Birthplace Museum. The market on Tuesdays and Fridays. The Lichfield Festival in July for arts.
lichfield-town.name: Lichfield

limavady.history: Jane Ross of Limavady transcribed the tune now known as the Londonderry Air — Danny Boy — from a passing fiddler in 1851, preserving what became Ireland's most famous melody. The Roe Valley Country Park provides beautiful riverside and woodland walking. The town is well-placed for the Causeway Coast.
limavady.knownFor: The Londonderry Air (Danny Boy was first written down here in 1851), the Roe Valley Country Park, the Causeway Coast gateway, and a County Londonderry market town of musical and natural heritage
limavady.localTip: The Danny Boy connection is celebrated in the town. The Roe Valley Country Park for excellent riverside walking. Binevenagh Mountain is a short drive for extraordinary views. The Causeway Coast is accessible.
limavady.name: Limavady

lincoln.history: Lincoln Cathedral — begun in 1088 — was the tallest building in the world for over two centuries. Lincoln Castle holds one of only four surviving original copies of Magna Carta (1215). The Roman Ermine Street still forms the city's spine. The Bailgate medieval quarter, at the top of the famous Steep Hill, is one of England's finest historic environments.
lincoln.knownFor: Lincoln Cathedral (the finest Gothic building in England according to John Ruskin), Lincoln Castle (with one of four surviving Magna Carta copies), the Bailgate medieval quarter, and a hilltop city of extraordinary beauty
lincoln.localTip: Steep Hill is genuinely extraordinary — the independent shops, galleries and cafes winding up to the cathedral are outstanding. The Cathedral is free and magnificent. The Magna Carta copy in the Castle is remarkable. The Christmas Market in December.
lincoln.name: Lincoln

linlithgow.history: Linlithgow Palace — begun by James I in the 15th century and completed by James V — was the birthplace of Mary Queen of Scots in 1542. The palace, now a magnificent shell after fire in 1746, stands on a promontory overlooking Linlithgow Loch. The town has been a royal burgh since 1138.
linlithgow.knownFor: Linlithgow Palace (Mary Queen of Scots' birthplace, one of Scotland's most magnificent royal ruins), Linlithgow Loch, the Union Canal, and a beautiful West Lothian royal burgh
linlithgow.localTip: Linlithgow Palace is extraordinarily beautiful — free to explore the exterior and always open. The palace interior with Historic Environment Scotland. Linlithgow Loch for the views. The Union Canal for walking and cycling.
linlithgow.name: Linlithgow

liphook.history: Liphook is a small Hampshire village on the London-Portsmouth railway. The Hollycombe Steam Collection, held in beautiful grounds, has one of the finest steam-powered fairground, agriculture and industrial collections in Britain. The village has good access to the South Downs and Hanger Hill.
liphook.knownFor: The Hollycombe Steam Collection (one of England's finest steam-powered collections), Bramshott Common, and a small Hampshire village with good rail links
liphook.localTip: The Hollycombe Steam Collection on open days is genuinely fun — steam fairground rides and working machinery. Bramshott Common for walking. The village has good independent character.
liphook.name: Liphook

lisburn.history: Lisburn was the heart of the Irish linen industry — Huguenot refugees who fled France in the 17th century brought their weaving skills here and created the industry that dominated Ulster's economy for 200 years. The Irish Linen Centre is the definitive museum of this heritage.
lisburn.knownFor: The Irish Linen Centre (one of Ireland's finest textile museums), the Lagan Valley Regional Park, the Cathedral, and Northern Ireland's second largest city after Belfast
lisburn.localTip: The Irish Linen Centre is exceptional — the working looms and the linen heritage story are brilliantly presented. The Lagan Valley for cycling. The Cathedral for the Huguenot heritage.
lisburn.name: Lisburn

lisnaskea.history: Lisnaskea is the main market town for the Upper Lough Erne area. Crom Castle and Estate — one of Ireland's most important nature conservation areas — is nearby. The Upper Lough is a remarkable landscape of islands, waterways and ancient oakwoods.
lisnaskea.knownFor: The Fermanagh Lakelands (the Upper Lough Erne gateway), the Crom Estate (NTS, one of Ireland's finest woodland landscapes), and a County Fermanagh market town serving the Upper Lough area
lisnaskea.localTip: Crom Estate (National Trust) is extraordinarily beautiful — the ancient oak woodland, the ruined castle and the lough views are exceptional. Upper Lough Erne for boating and fishing. The town centre market.
lisnaskea.name: Lisnaskea

liss.history: Liss is a quiet Hampshire village on the River Rother. The Shipwrights Way walking trail passes through, connecting the forest to the coast. The village has good rail connections and access to the South Downs National Park.
liss.knownFor: The Shipwrights Way long-distance trail, the Rother Valley, and a small Hampshire village on the edge of the South Downs
liss.localTip: The Shipwrights Way for walking through the Hampshire countryside. The Rother Valley for peaceful local walks. The village has a good local character and community feel.
liss.name: Liss

littlehampton.history: Littlehampton has been a seaside resort since the Victorian era. The East Beach Cafe (2007), designed by Thomas Heatherwick, won numerous architectural awards and features the world's longest bench — a sinuous rusted steel structure along the seafront. The River Arun estuary gives the town a beautiful harbour.
littlehampton.knownFor: The Beach (one of West Sussex's finest long sandy beaches), the East Beach Cafe (designed by Thomas Heatherwick, the world's longest bench), the River Arun estuary, and a popular West Sussex coastal resort
littlehampton.localTip: The East Beach Cafe is genuinely extraordinary architecture — the world's longest bench is remarkable. The beach is excellent. The Arun estuary walk. The harbour for fresh fish and crab.
littlehampton.name: Littlehampton

liverpool.history: At its Victorian peak Liverpool was the second city of the British Empire, processing 40% of world trade. Its Albert Dock is one of the UK's most significant heritage sites
liverpool.knownFor: The Beatles, Albert Dock, the Baltic Triangle's creative scene, Bold Street's independent food strip and proper Scouse hospitality
liverpool.localTip: Bold Street first, always. Then the Baltic Triangle after dark. Albert Dock is for the tourists — Lark Lane is for the locals.
liverpool.name: Liverpool

livingston.history: Livingston was designated a New Town in 1962 to house Edinburgh and Glasgow overspill. The Designer Outlet has made the town a major retail destination. Almondell and Calderwood Country Park, along the River Almond, provides excellent walking and wildlife. Livingston FC has competed in European football.
livingston.knownFor: Livingston FC (the West Lothian club with surprising European pedigree), the Almondell and Calderwood Country Park, the shopping (the Livingston Designer Outlet is Scotland's largest), and West Lothian's largest new town
livingston.localTip: Almondell and Calderwood Country Park for beautiful river walking. The Designer Outlet for retail. Livingston FC for Scottish football. The town centre has improving amenities.
livingston.name: Livingston

llanberis.history: Llanberis sits beneath Snowdon — at 1,085 metres, the highest mountain in England and Wales. The Snowdon Mountain Railway, opened in 1896, takes passengers to the summit. The Welsh Slate Museum, in the extraordinary Dinorwic quarry complex, tells the story of the slate industry that shaped North Wales. Dolbadarn Castle guards the Pass of Llanberis.
llanberis.knownFor: Snowdon (the highest mountain in England and Wales, accessible by mountain railway), the Snowdon Mountain Railway, the Welsh Slate Museum, and the village at the foot of Snowdon in the heart of the National Park
llanberis.localTip: The Snowdon Mountain Railway is a genuinely extraordinary experience. Walking up Snowdon via the Llanberis Path (the gentlest route). The Welsh Slate Museum is free and excellent. Dolbadarn Castle for the mountain views.
llanberis.name: Llanberis

llandovery.history: Twm Siôn Cati — the Welsh folk hero outlaw, Wales's answer to Robin Hood — was born near Llandovery in the 16th century. The town was an important droving centre where Welsh Black cattle were herded to English markets. The Heart of Wales Railway is one of Britain's most scenic lines.
llandovery.knownFor: Twm Siôn Cati (the Welsh Robin Hood, who was born nearby), the Castle, the Drovers' Road heritage, the Heart of Wales Railway line, and a small Carmarthenshire market town in spectacular hill country
llandovery.localTip: The Heart of Wales Railway is extraordinary — take the train to Llanwrtyd Wells and back. The Twm Siôn Cati cave above Rhandirmwyn. The cattle drovers' trail for walking.
llandovery.name: Llandovery

llandrindod-wells.history: Llandrindod Wells grew explosively as a Victorian spa resort after the railway arrived in 1865, with chalybeate and sulphur springs attracting visitors from across Britain. The Victorian legacy — hotels, bandstand and pump rooms — is remarkably intact.
llandrindod-wells.knownFor: The Victorian spa heritage (the finest collection of Victorian spa buildings in Wales), the Heart of Wales Railway, the Welsh Government presence, and the capital of Powys
llandrindod-wells.localTip: The Victorian Festival in August when the town dress in period costume is wonderful. The lake and Pump House for the spa heritage. The Heart of Wales Railway for spectacular journeys.
llandrindod-wells.name: Llandrindod Wells

llandudno.history: Llandudno was carefully planned as a Victorian resort — the seafront and promenade were developed from the 1850s to create one of the most elegant seaside towns in Britain. Lewis Carroll visited with the Liddell family — Alice Liddell, the inspiration for Alice in Wonderland, spent childhood holidays here. The Great Orme's Bronze Age copper mines are 3,500 years old.
llandudno.knownFor: The Great Orme (a massive limestone headland with a cable car and Bronze Age copper mines), the Victorian pier, the promenade (the finest in Wales), Lewis Carroll's Alice connection, and North Wales's queen of resorts
llandudno.localTip: The Great Orme by cable car or tramway is extraordinary — the views and the Bronze Age copper mines are outstanding. The promenade is genuinely magnificent. The pier for the Victorian seaside experience. The town centre for excellent independent shops.
llandudno.name: Llandudno

llanelli.history: Llanelli was the world's greatest tinplate-producing town in the 19th century — the town's tinplate works supplied the entire world. The Llanelli Scarlets RFC have won the Welsh Premiership and European trophies. The Millennium Coastal Park has transformed the former industrial coastline into one of Wales's finest green spaces.
llanelli.knownFor: The Scarlets (one of the great Welsh rugby teams), the tinplate industry heritage, the WWT Llanelli Wetland Centre, the Millennium Coastal Park, and a Welsh market town of industrial and sporting pride
llanelli.localTip: The WWT Llanelli Wetland Centre is genuinely excellent — the flamingos, wildfowl and wetland landscape are outstanding. The Millennium Coastal Park for cycling and walking. Parc y Scarlets for Welsh rugby. The town market.
llanelli.name: Llanelli

llanfairfechan.history: Llanfairfechan is one of North Wales's most dramatically situated small towns — the Carneddau mountains rise almost immediately behind the village, while the Menai Strait and Anglesey lie to the north. The village has been a quiet resort since Victorian times, attracting walkers and those seeking peace.
llanfairfechan.knownFor: The Carneddau mountains (the largest upland plateau in Wales, rising directly behind the village), the beautiful beach, the Menai Strait views, and a small Welsh village squeezed between mountains and sea
llanfairfechan.localTip: The Carneddau from Llanfairfechan is exceptional walking — the plateau above is remote and spectacular. The beach is good and uncrowded. The Menai Strait views. The village for local Welsh community character.
llanfairfechan.name: Llanfairfechan

llangefni.history: Llangefni is the county town and administrative centre of Anglesey. Oriel Ynys Môn — the island's heritage centre — holds extraordinary collections on Anglesey's history, from the Bronze Age to the present. The Afon Cefni nature reserve beside the town is an important wetland habitat.
llangefni.knownFor: The county town of Anglesey, Oriel Ynys Môn (the Anglesey heritage centre and gallery), the Afon Cefni nature reserve, and a small Welsh market town at the heart of the island
llangefni.localTip: Oriel Ynys Môn is excellent — the Anglesey heritage story told comprehensively. The Afon Cefni reserve for birdwatching. The town market. The centre of Anglesey for cycling.
llangefni.name: Llangefni

llangollen.history: The Llangollen International Musical Eisteddfod, held every July since 1947, attracts folk dancers, choirs and musicians from over 50 countries — one of the world's greatest multicultural festivals. Pontcysyllte Aqueduct, built by Telford in 1805, carries the Llangollen Canal 38 metres above the River Dee in an iron trough — a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
llangollen.knownFor: The International Eisteddfod (one of the world's greatest folk festivals), Pontcysyllte Aqueduct (UNESCO World Heritage Site, Telford's greatest engineering achievement), the Dee Valley, and a small Welsh town of extraordinary cultural and engineering significance
llangollen.localTip: The International Eisteddfod in July is genuinely extraordinary — colourful, joyful and unlike anything else in Wales. Pontcysyllte Aqueduct for the boat or walking experience — crossing the trough above the Dee is exhilarating. Valle Crucis Abbey ruins.
llangollen.name: Llangollen

lochcarron.history: Lochcarron village sits on the shores of Loch Carron — one of the most beautiful sea lochs in Scotland. The Lochcarron Weavers mill has been producing tartan here since 1984 and is one of Scotland's principal producers of authentic Highland textiles. The Bealach na Bà — the dramatic mountain pass to Applecross — is accessible from here.
lochcarron.knownFor: The Lochcarron Weavers (producers of the finest Scottish tartans), the beautiful sea loch, the Applecross Peninsula gateway, and a small Highland village of outstanding natural beauty and textile heritage
lochcarron.localTip: The Lochcarron Weavers visitor centre is fascinating — tartan production demonstrated. The Bealach na Bà over to Applecross is one of Scotland's greatest drives (not for the faint-hearted). The loch walk for extraordinary Highland scenery.
lochcarron.name: Lochcarron

lochgilphead.history: Lochgilphead sits at the head of Loch Gilp on the Kintyre peninsula. The Crinan Canal — built 1793-1801 — provides a 9-mile shortcut between Ardrishaig and Crinan, saving the long journey around the Mull of Kintyre. Kilmartin Glen, just north of the town, has the highest concentration of prehistoric monuments in Scotland outside Orkney.
lochgilphead.knownFor: The Crinan Canal (the most beautiful shortcut in Britain), Kilmartin Glen (one of Scotland's greatest prehistoric landscapes), and the administrative centre of Argyll and Bute
lochgilphead.localTip: Kilmartin Glen is extraordinary — standing stones, cairns and cup-marked rocks across a single valley. The Crinan Canal for walking or boating. The Kilmartin Museum for the prehistoric story. Crinan village at the canal end for exceptional seafood.
lochgilphead.name: Lochgilphead

lockerbie.history: On 21 December 1988, Pan Am Flight 103 exploded over Lockerbie — 270 people died in the UK's worst terrorist attack. The town was devastated but showed extraordinary community spirit in responding to the disaster. The Gardens of Remembrance, where victims are remembered, is a place of profound dignity and peace.
lockerbie.knownFor: The Lockerbie air disaster (Pan Am Flight 103, December 1988, the UK's worst terrorist attack), the Remembrance Garden, the M74 corridor, and a Dumfriesshire market town forever marked by tragedy
lockerbie.localTip: The Gardens of Remembrance are quietly moving — a dignified memorial to all 270 victims. The town has good local character. The surrounding Annandale countryside is beautiful. The M74 makes it accessible.
lockerbie.name: Lockerbie

london.history: Founded by the Romans as Londinium around 43AD, London has been one of the world's great cities for nearly two millennia
london.knownFor: Global finance, world-class museums, the West End, street food markets, independent culture and relentless reinvention
london.localTip: The most interesting London is neighbourhood London — skip the tourist trail and let Roam show you what's actually going on
london.name: London

long-eaton.history: Long Eaton grew as a major centre for lace and net curtain manufacturing in the 19th century — at its peak supplying a significant proportion of Britain's lace. The town sits on the Derbyshire-Nottinghamshire border on the River Erewash. The Erewash Valley provides walking and cycling.
long-eaton.knownFor: The lace-making heritage (Long Eaton was a major centre for lace and net curtain manufacturing), the River Erewash, and a Derbyshire town between Nottingham and Derby
long-eaton.localTip: The Erewash Valley Trail for walking and cycling. The town centre market for local produce. Attenborough Nature Reserve nearby is outstanding for birdwatching.
long-eaton.name: Long Eaton

longbenton.history: Longbenton is a suburban community in North Tyneside, best known as the location of the large HMRC tax office complex — one of the largest office employers in the North East. The Metro provides direct access to Newcastle and the coast.
longbenton.knownFor: The HMRC headquarters, the Longbenton community, the Metro connection, and a North Tyneside suburban community on the Tyne Wear Metro
longbenton.localTip: The Metro for easy Newcastle and coast access. The Four Lane Ends Metro interchange. Forest Hall nearby for community amenities.
longbenton.name: Longbenton

longridge.history: Longridge sits at the southern edge of the Forest of Bowland AONB. Jeffrey Hill above the town gives extraordinary views across the Ribble Valley and the Lancashire coast. The town grew as a market centre for the agricultural communities of the Ribble Valley.
longridge.knownFor: The Ribble Valley setting, the Jeffrey Hill views (panoramic Lancashire views), the Longridge Fell, and a small Lancashire market town above the Ribble Valley
longridge.localTip: Jeffrey Hill for panoramic Lancashire views — Blackpool Tower visible on clear days. The Ribble Valley walking from Longridge. The town centre for local Lancashire character. The Trough of Bowland is accessible.
longridge.name: Longridge

longton-lancashire.history: Longton is a small Lancashire village on the Fylde coast plain. The WWT Martin Mere centre, just outside the village, is one of the most important wildfowl and wetlands centres in England — famous for its wintering whooper swans and pink-footed geese which arrive in their thousands each autumn.
longton-lancashire.knownFor: The Longton area of the Fylde, the Martin Mere Wildfowl and Wetlands Trust (one of England's finest WWT centres), and a small Lancashire village near one of England's best wildfowl reserves
longton-lancashire.localTip: Martin Mere WWT is genuinely outstanding — the pink-footed geese coming in at dusk in autumn is one of England's great wildlife spectacles. The Ribble Estuary for additional coastal birdwatching. The village has local character.
longton-lancashire.name: Longton

lostwithiel.history: Lostwithiel was the administrative capital of medieval Cornwall — home of the Duchy of Cornwall's courts, the stannary parliament and the coinage of tin. Restormel Castle, perched above the town, has one of the most complete circular keeps in England. The town has become a centre for antique and craft shops.
lostwithiel.knownFor: The ancient capital of Cornwall (seat of the medieval Duchy of Cornwall), the Restormel Castle (the finest circular keep in the South West), the antique shops, and a small Cornish market town of royal and medieval significance
lostwithiel.localTip: Restormel Castle is extraordinary and rarely crowded — the circular keep is outstanding. The antique shops in the town centre are excellent. The River Fowey walk south towards Fowey. The town has genuine medieval character.
lostwithiel.name: Lostwithiel

loughborough.history: Taylor's Bell Foundry in Loughborough has been casting bells since 1839 — they cast Big Ben and bells for Westminster Abbey, Washington Cathedral and countless churches worldwide. The Great Central Railway, a heritage line running to Leicester, is the only double-track main line heritage railway in Britain. Loughborough University is consistently rated among the best in the world for sport.
loughborough.knownFor: The Great Central Railway (the only double-track main line heritage railway in Britain), the Taylor's Bell Foundry (the world's largest working bell foundry), Loughborough University (one of Britain's finest), and a Leicestershire market town of extraordinary industrial and academic heritage
loughborough.localTip: Taylor's Bell Foundry tours are genuinely fascinating — watching enormous bells being cast is extraordinary. The Great Central Railway for steam trains south. The university campus for sporting facilities.
loughborough.name: Loughborough

loughborough-town.history: Taylor's Bell Foundry has cast bells since 1839 including Big Ben and Westminster Abbey bells. The Great Central is Britain's only double-track heritage main line. Loughborough University is world-ranked for sport.
loughborough-town.knownFor: Taylor's Bell Foundry (the world's largest, casting Big Ben), the Great Central Railway, and Loughborough University
loughborough-town.localTip: Taylor's Bell Foundry tours are extraordinary — watching bells being cast. The Great Central Railway for steam south to Leicester.
loughborough-town.name: Loughborough

louth.history: St James's Church spire — at 90 metres — is the tallest parish church steeple in England. Alfred Lord Tennyson was born nearby and educated in Louth. The town sits at the heart of the Lincolnshire Wolds — an AONB of rolling chalk hills. The Wednesday, Friday and Saturday markets are among Lincolnshire's best.
louth.knownFor: St James's Church (the tallest parish church spire in England at 90 metres), the market (one of Lincolnshire's finest), Alfred Lord Tennyson's connection, and the unofficial capital of the Lincolnshire Wolds AONB
louth.localTip: St James's spire is extraordinary — climb it if it's open. The market is excellent for local Lincolnshire produce. The Lincolnshire Wolds walking from the town. Tennyson's birthplace at Somersby is a short drive.
louth.name: Louth

louth-town.history: St James's Church has the tallest parish church spire in England. Tennyson was educated here. The Wednesday, Friday and Saturday markets are among Lincolnshire's finest.
louth-town.knownFor: St James's Church (England's tallest parish spire at 90m), the excellent markets, the Lincolnshire Wolds, and Tennyson's hometown
louth-town.localTip: St James's spire is extraordinary. The market for local produce. The Lincolnshire Wolds walking.
louth-town.name: Louth

lowestoft.history: Lowestoft is the most easterly town in Britain — the sun rises here before anywhere else in England. Benjamin Britten, who went on to found the Aldeburgh Festival, was born here in 1913. The town was a major herring fishing port — the Scottish herring fleet would winter here. The Claremont Pier opened in 1903.
lowestoft.knownFor: The most easterly point in Britain, the birthplace of Benjamin Britten, the fishing heritage, the Claremont Pier, and a Suffolk coastal town where the sun rises first in England
lowestoft.localTip: The most easterly point sunrise is genuinely special — worth getting up for. The Britten birthplace connection. The Maritime Museum for the fishing heritage. The beach is good. Oulton Broad for the Broads access.
lowestoft.name: Lowestoft

ludlow.history: Ludlow Castle was one of the most important royal residences in medieval England. The town grew around it as a planned medieval new town in the 12th century
ludlow.knownFor: The food festival, medieval castle, the concentration of exceptional restaurants, the food market and surrounding Shropshire countryside of extraordinary beauty
ludlow.localTip: Book a restaurant before you come — the best places fill up. The castle at sunset. Walk to Bromfield for the Cookhouse if you want exceptional casual food.
ludlow.name: Ludlow

lurgan.history: Lurgan grew as a linen damask weaving town — the finest linen in Ireland was woven here. Alex Higgins, the Hurricane, was born here in 1949.
lurgan.knownFor: Lough Neagh shoreline, the linen damask heritage, Alex Higgins's hometown, and a County Armagh town at the heart of the Craigavon conurbation
lurgan.localTip: Oxford Island on Lough Neagh is excellent for birdwatching and walking. The town centre market. Alex Higgins is celebrated locally.
lurgan.name: Lurgan

luton.history: A hat-making town that made Luton synonymous with the straw hat trade in the 19th and early 20th centuries. The Vauxhall Motors factory brought manufacturing dominance. The airport has transformed it into a major transport hub.
luton.knownFor: The airport, the hat trade heritage, the Wardown Museum, Stockwood Discovery Centre and one of the UK's most ethnically diverse cities with an extraordinary food culture
luton.localTip: Bury Park is the heart of Luton's food culture — South Asian, Middle Eastern and African restaurants that are genuinely exceptional. Wardown Museum is free and excellent. Stockwood Discovery Centre for gardens.
luton.name: Luton

lyme-regis.history: Lyme Regis sits on the Jurassic Coast — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — where Mary Anning found the first ichthyosaur skeleton in 1811. The Cobb, the ancient stone pier, appears in Jane Austen's Persuasion and John Fowles's The French Lieutenant's Woman. The town has been a resort since the 17th century.
lyme-regis.knownFor: The Cobb (the ancient stone harbour and pier, immortalised in The French Lieutenant's Woman), the fossils (the Jurassic Coast is Europe's finest fossil site), the beach, and a small Dorset town of extraordinary literary and geological significance
lyme-regis.localTip: Fossil hunting on the beach is genuinely productive — especially after storms. The Cobb walk at any time is extraordinary. The Lyme Regis Museum for Mary Anning and the fossil story. The town centre for excellent independent shops.
lyme-regis.name: Lyme Regis

lymm.history: Lymm is a picturesque Cheshire village on the Bridgewater Canal. Lymm Dam, a Victorian reservoir now managed as a nature reserve, is the centre of the village's outdoor life. The medieval Lymm Cross in the old village centre is one of Cheshire's finest. The village is consistently regarded as one of Cheshire's most desirable.
lymm.knownFor: Lymm Dam (a beautiful Victorian reservoir and nature reserve), the Bridgewater Canal, the Lymm Cross (a medieval market cross), and one of Cheshire's most charming and affluent villages
lymm.localTip: Lymm Dam for the beautiful Victorian reservoir walk. The Bridgewater Canal towpath. The village centre for excellent independent shops and cafes. The Lymm Cross for the medieval heritage.
lymm.name: Lymm

lynton.history: Lynton sits high above Lynmouth — the two linked by the extraordinary cliff railway, opened in 1890, which uses no engine: water ballast from the top tank counterbalances the ascending car. The Valley of Rocks, a remarkable dry valley of jagged rock formations, inspired Shelley and Coleridge. Lynmouth's 1952 flood disaster killed 34 people.
lynton.knownFor: The Lynton and Lynmouth Cliff Railway (the world's only water-powered cliff railway), the Valley of Rocks, the Exmoor National Park, and a small Devon clifftop village above the famous Lynmouth
lynton.localTip: The cliff railway is genuinely extraordinary — the counterbalance system is elegant engineering. The Valley of Rocks walk from Lynton is spectacular. The South West Coast Path from Lynton is outstanding.
lynton.name: Lynton

lytham-st-annes.history: Royal Lytham & St Annes has hosted the Open Championship 12 times — more than any other course in England. The town is the most refined of the Fylde resorts — with Victorian and Edwardian architecture, the iconic green windmill on the seafront, and a distinctly upmarket character compared to nearby Blackpool.
lytham-st-annes.knownFor: The Royal Lytham & St Annes Golf Club (host of the Open Championship), the windmill, the Ribble Estuary, the most genteel of the Fylde coast resorts, and a Lancashire seaside town of enduring elegance
lytham-st-annes.localTip: The windmill on Lytham Green is iconic. Royal Lytham during the Open is extraordinary. The Ribble Estuary for birdwatching — one of Europe's most important estuaries. The town centre for excellent independent shopping.
lytham-st-annes.name: Lytham St Annes

macclesfield.history: Macclesfield was the silk-weaving capital of Britain — the Paradise Mill still has working Jacquard looms. Ian Curtis of Joy Division is buried in Macclesfield Cemetery — his grave has become a place of pilgrimage for music fans. The Macclesfield Canal, at 518 feet, is the highest canal in England.
macclesfield.knownFor: The Silk Museum (the Silk Capital of Britain), the Macclesfield Canal (the highest canal in England), Ian Curtis's grave, and a Cheshire mill town of significant industrial and musical heritage
macclesfield.localTip: The Silk Museum and Paradise Mill are genuinely excellent. Ian Curtis's grave in the cemetery is a music pilgrimage site. The Macclesfield Canal for beautiful walking. The Treacle Market for independent food.
macclesfield.name: Macclesfield

machynlleth.history: Owain Glyndŵr — the last native Prince of Wales — held his parliament in Machynlleth in 1404, the high point of the Welsh rebellion against English rule. The Parliament House is preserved. The Centre for Alternative Technology, founded in 1973, is Europe's leading sustainable living exhibition centre.
machynlleth.knownFor: The Parliament House (where Owain Glyndŵr was crowned Prince of Wales in 1404), the Centre for Alternative Technology (the UK's most visited eco-centre), the Dyfi Biosphere, and a small Welsh market town of extraordinary historical and environmental significance
machynlleth.localTip: The Parliament House for Owain Glyndŵr's story — genuinely significant Welsh history. The Centre for Alternative Technology is excellent and inspiring. The Dyfi Estuary for birdwatching. The Wednesday market for local Welsh produce.
machynlleth.name: Machynlleth

machynlleth-town.history: Owain Glyndŵr held his parliament here in 1404, the high point of Welsh resistance to English rule. The Centre for Alternative Technology (1973) is Europe's leading sustainable living centre.
machynlleth-town.knownFor: The Parliament House (Owain Glyndŵr's 1404 Welsh parliament), the Centre for Alternative Technology, and a small Welsh town of extraordinary historical and environmental significance
machynlleth-town.localTip: The Parliament House for Glyndŵr's significant Welsh history. The CAT is excellent and inspiring. The Wednesday market.
machynlleth-town.name: Machynlleth

maesteg.history: Maesteg grew as a coal mining town in the Llynfi Valley. The Town Hall, built in 1881, is one of the most elegant civic buildings in the South Wales valleys. The town has maintained strong community institutions despite the closure of the coal industry.
maesteg.knownFor: The Llynfi Valley, the coal mining heritage, the Maesteg Town Hall (one of the finest civic buildings in the valleys), and a South Wales valleys town of industrial pride and community spirit
maesteg.localTip: The Town Hall is architecturally impressive. The Llynfi Valley cycling route. The town centre market for local community character.
maesteg.name: Maesteg

maghera.history: Maghera contains one of Ulster's oldest Christian sites — the ruins of a medieval church with a remarkable Romanesque doorway. The town serves as a market centre for the mid-Sperrins area. The Sperrins mountains are accessible from the town.
maghera.knownFor: The ancient church ruins (one of the oldest Christian sites in Ulster), the Sperrins gateway, and a County Londonderry market town serving the mid-Ulster community
maghera.localTip: The medieval church ruins and the Romanesque doorway are extraordinary. The Sperrins walking from Maghera. The town centre for local community shopping.
maghera.name: Maghera

magherafelt.history: Magherafelt grew as a plantation town. Bellaghy, a few miles away, is the birthplace of Seamus Heaney — Nobel Prize-winning poet.
magherafelt.knownFor: The Mid Ulster Hub, Bellaghy (Seamus Heaney's village nearby), Springhill House, and a County Londonderry market town serving the Sperrins hinterland
magherafelt.localTip: The Seamus Heaney HomePlace in Bellaghy is exceptional. Springhill House (National Trust) is nearby.
magherafelt.name: Magherafelt

maidenhead.history: Maidenhead was the Victorian and Edwardian playground for London's wealthy — Boulter's Lock on summer Sundays was the most photographed scene in England. Cliveden, the great country house above the river (now a hotel), was at the centre of the Profumo Affair. Kenneth Grahame wrote The Wind in the Willows nearby.
maidenhead.knownFor: The Thames (Maidenhead's stretch is particularly beautiful), Boulter's Lock (the famous Victorian boating hotspot), Cliveden (one of England's most glamorous country houses), and a Berkshire Thames-side town
maidenhead.localTip: The Thames at Maidenhead is genuinely beautiful. Cliveden and its grounds (NT) are outstanding. Boulter's Lock for the historic atmosphere. The Norden Farm Arts Centre for excellent theatre.
maidenhead.name: Maidenhead

maidstone.history: Maidstone has been Kent's county town since the medieval period. The Museum holds a remarkable collection including Japanese armour, Egyptian mummies and the Maidstone Iguanodon. Leeds Castle — often called the most beautiful castle in the world — is 5 miles away. The Medway provides a beautiful waterfront.
maidstone.knownFor: The county town of Kent, the Maidstone Museum (one of England's best regional museums), Leeds Castle (one of the most beautiful in the world, nearby), the River Medway, and the Garden of England hub
maidstone.localTip: The Maidstone Museum is free and excellent — the collections are extraordinary. Leeds Castle is genuinely magnificent. The Medway riverside walk. The market for local Kent produce.
maidstone.name: Maidstone

maldon.history: The Battle of Maldon (991 AD) — where the Anglo-Saxons were defeated by Viking raiders — inspired one of the finest Old English poems. Maldon Salt has been harvested from the Blackwater Estuary since Roman times using traditional methods unchanged for centuries. The Thames sailing barges moored at the quay are a magnificent sight.
maldon.knownFor: The Maldon Salt (one of England's most celebrated food products), the Blackwater Estuary, the Thames barges, the Battle of Maldon (991 AD, one of England's great poems), and a small Essex market town of extraordinary character
maldon.localTip: The Hythe Quay for the Thames barges — extraordinary wooden sailing vessels. The Maldon Crystal Salt company for the salt story. The High Street for independent shops and cafes. The Promenade Park.
maldon.name: Maldon

mallaig.history: Mallaig was built as a fishing port in 1901 when the railway arrived. The West Highland Line from Fort William — crossing the Glenfinnan Viaduct — is one of the world's great railway journeys and became the Hogwarts Express in Harry Potter. The ferry connections to Skye, Eigg, Muck, Rum and Canna make Mallaig the gateway to the Small Isles.
mallaig.knownFor: The Jacobite steam train (the Hogwarts Express termination, crossing Glenfinnan Viaduct), the ferry to Skye and the Small Isles, the kippers (Mallaig kippers are famous throughout Scotland), and a remote Highland fishing port of great character
mallaig.localTip: The Jacobite steam train arrival from Fort William is spectacular. The ferry to Skye is a beautiful crossing. Mallaig kippers are genuinely extraordinary — buy from the smokehouse. The harbour for langoustines.
mallaig.name: Mallaig

malmesbury.history: Malmesbury claims to be England's oldest borough, having been granted its charter in 924 AD by King Athelstan. In around 1010, Brother Elmer attached homemade wings and jumped from the abbey tower, gliding 200 metres before breaking his legs — one of the earliest recorded attempts at human flight. The Market Cross is one of England's finest.
malmesbury.knownFor: The Malmesbury Abbey (Wiltshire's finest Norman abbey), Elmer the Flying Monk (an 11th century monk who attached wings and jumped from the abbey), the Market Cross, and the oldest borough in England
malmesbury.localTip: The Abbey is magnificent and free. The Market Cross is exquisite. The Athelstan Museum for the oldest borough story. The River Avon walks around the town. The Abbey House Gardens for spectacular spring bulbs.
malmesbury.name: Malmesbury

malton.history: Malton was the Roman fort of Derventio, later a Viking centre. The town's reinvention as a food destination began in 2012 and has been remarkable.
malton.knownFor: The food capital of Yorkshire, the fortnightly Artisan Market, Viking and Roman heritage, and a small North Yorkshire market town with a disproportionate food culture
malton.localTip: The Artisan Market on the last Saturday of the month is one of Yorkshire's great food events. Talbot Yard food court for daily independent food.
malton.name: Malton

malvern.history: The Malvern Hills — a ridge of ancient rock rising to 425 metres — provide the backdrop to one of England's finest hilltop walks. Edward Elgar was born near Malvern and composed much of his greatest music here. The Morgan Motor Company — makers of hand-built sports cars — has been based in Malvern since 1910.
malvern.knownFor: The Malvern Hills (one of England's finest hill walks), Edward Elgar's home town, the Malvern Festival, the Morgan Motor Company, and a Victorian spa town of exceptional natural and musical heritage
malvern.localTip: The Malvern Hills ridge walk is outstanding — the Worcestershire Beacon gives extraordinary views. The Elgar Birthplace Museum at Broadheath. Morgan Motors factory tours. The Great Malvern Priory.
malvern.name: Malvern

manchester.history: The world's first industrial city, birthplace of the Co-operative movement and the Hallé Orchestra, and the city that gave the world the Haçienda
manchester.knownFor: Music heritage, football, the Northern Quarter's independent scene, world-class food and a creative energy that's hard to match
manchester.localTip: Ancoats is where serious food happens. The Northern Quarter is where culture lives. Chorlton is where locals go when they want a quieter pint.
manchester.name: Manchester

mansfield.history: Mansfield is the principal market town for Nottinghamshire's coalfield area. The town has been a market centre since the medieval period. Sherwood Forest and Robin Hood country are accessible from the town. The Field Mill stadium is Mansfield Town's home — a Football League club with strong local support.
mansfield.knownFor: The Forest Town heritage, the Robin Hood country setting, the market (one of Nottinghamshire's finest), and a Nottinghamshire market town at the heart of Sherwood Forest
mansfield.localTip: Sherwood Forest and the Major Oak are close. The market for local Nottinghamshire produce. The Mansfield Museum for local heritage. The town centre for independent shopping.
mansfield.name: Mansfield

mapplewell.history: Mapplewell is a former mining village north of Barnsley. The area has strong South Yorkshire mining heritage and community character.
mapplewell.knownFor: The Barnsley area setting, the mining heritage, and a South Yorkshire village north of Barnsley
mapplewell.localTip: The village centre for local community shopping. Barnsley is close for wider amenities and food. The surrounding countryside for walking.
mapplewell.name: Mapplewell

march.history: March's St Wendreda's Church contains what Nikolaus Pevsner called the finest angel roof in England — 120 carved oak angels soaring across the nave in a double hammer-beam structure. The town sits on the River Nene in the heart of the Fens. March was an important Fenland trading centre.
march.knownFor: The finest angel roof in England (St Wendreda's Church has 120 carved angels), the Nene Valley, and a small Cambridgeshire Fen market town of surprising ecclesiastical glory
march.localTip: St Wendreda's angel roof is extraordinary and free — the finest example of medieval carpentry in England. The Nene Valley walk. The town market. The Fenland cycling routes from March.
march.name: March

margate.history: J.M.W. Turner loved Margate — the quality of the light over the Thames Estuary inspired his late work. The Turner Contemporary gallery, opened in 2011, began the town's extraordinary arts-led renaissance. The Shell Grotto — discovered in 1835 and covered in 4.6 million shells — remains unexplained. Dreamland, the amusement park opened in 1920, has been revived.
margate.knownFor: Turner Contemporary (the gallery that sparked one of Britain's greatest arts-led regenerations), Dreamland (the legendary amusement park), the Shell Grotto (a mysterious underground chamber covered in shells), and a Kent seaside town reinvented as an arts destination
margate.localTip: Turner Contemporary is free and excellent. The Shell Grotto is genuinely mysterious and wonderful. Dreamland for the revived heritage amusement park. Margate Old Town for outstanding independent food and galleries.
margate.name: Margate

market-bosworth.history: The Battle of Bosworth Field, fought on 22 August 1485, ended the Wars of the Roses and established the Tudor dynasty. Richard III — 'A horse, a horse, my kingdom for a horse' — was killed here. Richard's skeleton was found under a Leicester car park in 2012. Market Bosworth itself is a charming small town with an excellent park.
market-bosworth.knownFor: The Battle of Bosworth Field 1485 (where Richard III died and the Tudor dynasty began), the Bosworth Battlefield Heritage Centre, and a small Leicestershire market town of world-historical significance
market-bosworth.localTip: The Battlefield Heritage Centre is excellent — the battle is brought to life brilliantly. Bosworth Country Park for walking. The town market. Market Bosworth Country Park for picnics.
market-bosworth.name: Market Bosworth

market-drayton.history: Market Drayton has been famous for gingerbread since the 18th century — local schoolboys used to sell it at the market. Robert Clive — 'Clive of India', who established British India — was born here in 1725 and allegedly climbed the church tower as a boy. The Shropshire Union Canal passes through the town.
market-drayton.knownFor: The gingerbread (Market Drayton has been famous for it since the 18th century), the birthplace of Robert Clive ('Clive of India'), the Shropshire Union Canal, and a Shropshire market town of great character
market-drayton.localTip: The gingerbread is genuinely excellent — try from local bakers. The Clive of India connection celebrated locally. The Shropshire Union Canal towpath. The Wednesday market for local produce.
market-drayton.name: Market Drayton

market-harborough.history: The Old Grammar School — built 1614, standing on oak columns above the butter market — is one of the finest timber-framed buildings in the Midlands. Market Harborough grew as a coaching town on the London-Leicester road. The Kibworth area nearby was home to the Symington family — makers of corsets and later tinned food.
market-harborough.knownFor: The Old Grammar School (a magnificent timber-framed building perched on wooden columns in the middle of the High Street), the Symington Collection, and a Leicestershire market town of exceptional architectural quality
market-harborough.localTip: The Old Grammar School is extraordinary — the timber-framing above open arches is unique. The market for local Leicestershire produce. The Manor House Museum. Foxton Locks (nearby) for the extraordinary staircase canal locks.
market-harborough.name: Market Harborough

market-rasen.history: Market Rasen Racecourse is one of England's smaller National Hunt courses — intimate, friendly and popular for a day out. The town sits on the edge of the Lincolnshire Wolds. The Tuesday market has been the commercial heart since medieval times.
market-rasen.knownFor: The Market Rasen Racecourse (a popular National Hunt course), the Lincolnshire Wolds setting, and a small Lincolnshire market town serving the Wolds community
market-rasen.localTip: Market Rasen Racecourse is excellent for a day's jumping racing. The Lincolnshire Wolds walking from the town. The Tuesday market for local produce.
market-rasen.name: Market Rasen

marlborough.history: Marlborough's High Street — one of the widest in England, with arcaded buildings along one side — was laid out after the great fire of 1653. Marlborough College occupies the site of a mound that may have been Merlin's burial place. Savernake Forest, the only privately owned forest in England, surrounds the town with ancient woodland.
marlborough.knownFor: The widest High Street in England, Marlborough College (one of England's great public schools, whose alumni include Kate Middleton), Savernake Forest (a royal hunting forest since the Norman Conquest), and a Wiltshire market town of extraordinary beauty
marlborough.localTip: The High Street is genuinely magnificent — the arcades and the scale are extraordinary. Savernake Forest for walks among ancient oak and beech. The Wednesday and Saturday markets. The college mound for the Merlin legend.
marlborough.name: Marlborough

marlow.history: Mary Shelley completed Frankenstein while living in Marlow in 1817. The town's suspension bridge (1832) is one of the most photographed in England. Marlow is a famous rowing town — the Marlow Regatta is one of England's oldest. The Compleat Angler hotel on the Thames bank is a beloved institution.
marlow.knownFor: The suspension bridge (a perfect Victorian span over the Thames), the Compleat Angler hotel, the rowing heritage, and a beautiful Thames-side market town where Shelley wrote Frankenstein
marlow.localTip: The suspension bridge and the Thames walk are beautiful. The Compleat Angler for a special Thames experience. The town centre for excellent independent food. Rowing on the Thames.
marlow.name: Marlow

masham.history: Masham has two of England's most celebrated breweries: Theakston's (founded 1827, makers of Old Peculier) and Black Sheep (founded 1992 when Paul Theakston left the family firm). The Black Sheep name is Paul Theakston's self-deprecating joke. The town's large market square reflects its former importance as a cattle market.
masham.knownFor: The two breweries (Theakston's and Black Sheep — both making exceptional Yorkshire ales), the large market square, the Dales setting, and a tiny North Yorkshire market town of global ale significance
masham.localTip: The Theakston's brewery tour is excellent — Old Peculier in the town is unmissable. The Black Sheep brewery tour equally good. The market square is one of the Dales' finest. The Swinton Estate walks.
masham.name: Masham

matlock.history: Matlock grew as a Victorian spa town along the dramatic Derwent Gorge. The Heights of Abraham — reached by cable car — have been a tourist attraction since Victorian times. Riber Castle, built by John Smedley (the hydropathy pioneer), dominates the skyline. The town sits at the gateway to the Derbyshire Dales.
matlock.knownFor: The Heights of Abraham (cable car to limestone caverns above the Derwent Gorge), Riber Castle (a ruined Victorian folly on the skyline), the Peak District gateway, and a Derbyshire spa town of dramatic gorge scenery
matlock.localTip: The Heights of Abraham cable car and caverns are genuinely excellent. Riber Castle ruins for the dramatic views. The Derwent Valley Mills UNESCO heritage trail begins nearby.
matlock.name: Matlock

maybole.history: Maybole Castle, a 17th century tower house on the High Street, is one of the finest town castles in Scotland. Robert Burns had family connections to the Maybole area — his grandfather was from Kirkoswald nearby. Culzean Castle (NTS) — Adam's masterpiece on the Ayrshire coast — is just 6 miles away.
maybole.knownFor: The castle (one of the finest tower houses in Ayrshire), the Burns connection (his family had connections to the area), the Culzean Castle proximity, and a small Ayrshire market town serving the Carrick area
maybole.localTip: Culzean Castle is one of Scotland's great buildings — the Adam architecture and cliff-top setting are extraordinary. Maybole Castle on the High Street. The Burns connections at Kirkoswald nearby.
maybole.name: Maybole

melrose.history: Melrose Abbey, founded in 1136, is the finest ruined abbey in Scotland — Robert the Bruce's heart is said to be buried here (a lead casket was found in 1921). The Melrose Sevens, first played in 1883, is the world's original Rugby Sevens tournament. The Eildon Hills — three volcanic peaks — give extraordinary Border views.
melrose.knownFor: Melrose Abbey (where Robert the Bruce's heart is buried), the Borders Rugby Sevens (the original Rugby Sevens tournament), the Eildon Hills, and a small Borders town of profound historical and sporting significance
melrose.localTip: The Abbey is magnificent — one of Scotland's great ruins. The Eildon Hills for the summit views and Roman camp. The Borders walking is outstanding. Priorwood Garden (NTS) for the dried flower collection.
melrose.name: Melrose

melton-mowbray.history: Melton Mowbray has been producing hand-raised pork pies since the 18th century — the distinctive bow-sided pie shape is protected by PGI. The Vale of Belvoir nearby produces Stilton cheese. The town was for centuries the most fashionable fox-hunting destination in England — society came from London to hunt.
melton-mowbray.knownFor: The Melton Mowbray pork pie (PGI-protected, made here since the 18th century), Stilton cheese (also PGI-protected and made in Leicestershire), the hunting capital of England, and a Leicestershire market town of extraordinary food heritage
melton-mowbray.localTip: Buy a genuine Melton Mowbray pork pie from Ye Olde Pork Pie Shoppe — the real thing is extraordinary. Tuxford & Tebbutt for Stilton. The Tuesday and Saturday markets for local produce. The hunting heritage trail.
melton-mowbray.name: Melton Mowbray

mere.history: Mere is a small market town on the edge of the Blackmore Vale at the Wiltshire-Somerset-Dorset borders. Stourhead — the extraordinary 18th century landscape garden with its temples, lake and grottos — is just 3 miles away. The town's church contains some fine medieval monuments.
mere.knownFor: The Stourhead Estate (one of England's greatest landscape gardens, nearby), the Zeals area, and a small Wiltshire market town at the gateway to the Blackmore Vale
mere.localTip: Stourhead is genuinely one of England's great gardens — the lake circuit is beautiful in any season. The town has good local character. The Blackmore Vale walking from Mere.
mere.name: Mere

merthyr-tydfil.history: Merthyr Tydfil was once the iron capital of the world — Cyfarthfa, Dowlais, Plymouth and Penydarren ironworks made the town the most important industrial centre on earth in the early 19th century. The world's first steam railway journey on rails ran from Merthyr in 1804. The Merthyr Rising of 1831 — a workers' uprising — raised the Red Flag for the first time.
merthyr-tydfil.knownFor: The birthplace of the Industrial Revolution's iron age (more iron was produced here than anywhere in the world in the early 19th century), Cyfarthfa Castle, the Merthyr Rising of 1831, and a Welsh valleys town of global industrial significance
merthyr-tydfil.localTip: Cyfarthfa Castle and Museum (free) is excellent — the story of the iron age is extraordinary. The Brecon Beacons are immediately accessible. The Trevithick trail for the first railway journey.
merthyr-tydfil.name: Merthyr Tydfil

mexborough.history: Mexborough is the birthplace of Ted Hughes — Poet Laureate from 1984 to 1998 — who was born here in 1930. The town grew around the Don Valley navigation and coal mining. The River Don and the Sheffield and South Yorkshire Canal pass through.
mexborough.knownFor: The Don Valley, the canal heritage, the Ted Hughes connection (the poet laureate was born here), and a South Yorkshire town on the River Don
mexborough.localTip: The Ted Hughes birthplace connection is celebrated locally. The Don Valley walk. Conisbrough Castle (Ivanhoe's castle) is just 2 miles away. The canal for walking.
mexborough.name: Mexborough

middlesbrough.history: Middlesbrough grew with extraordinary speed — in 1801 it had 25 inhabitants; by 1900 over 90,000. The iron and steel works forged girders for the Sydney Harbour Bridge, the Tyne Bridge and structures worldwide. The Transporter Bridge (1911) is the world's largest working transporter bridge. Captain James Cook grew up in nearby Marton.
middlesbrough.knownFor: The Transporter Bridge (the world's largest working transporter bridge), the Riverside Stadium (Middlesbrough FC), the Dorman Museum, and a Teesside town that built much of the world's industrial infrastructure
middlesbrough.localTip: The Transporter Bridge is genuinely extraordinary — take the gondola across. The Dorman Museum is free and excellent. The Captain Cook Birthplace Museum in Stewart Park. The Riverside Stadium for Middlesbrough FC.
middlesbrough.name: Middlesbrough

middlewich.history: Middlewich — the Roman Salinae — has been producing salt since Roman times. The town's position at the junction of major canals made it a vital industrial hub. The Folk and Boat Festival, held annually, is one of England's finest canal festivals. The town has three canals passing through it.
middlewich.knownFor: The canal junction (where the Trent and Mersey meets the Middlewich branch), the salt heritage (Middlewich has produced salt since Roman times), the Folk and Boat Festival, and a Cheshire canal town of great character
middlewich.localTip: The canal junction is extraordinary — narrowboats threading through the town. The Folk and Boat Festival in June is genuinely excellent. The salt heritage at the Middlewich Heritage Museum. The canal walks from the town.
middlewich.name: Middlewich

midhurst.history: Cowdray House — burnt out in 1793 — is one of England's finest Tudor ruins, set in 10,000 acres of parkland that includes one of the world's most prestigious polo grounds. H.G. Wells taught at Midhurst Grammar School and later attended it. The South Downs surround the town.
midhurst.knownFor: Cowdray Park (the magnificent Tudor ruin and polo ground), the South Downs National Park, H.G. Wells's connections, and a small West Sussex market town of exceptional beauty
midhurst.localTip: Cowdray Ruins are extraordinary and free. Cowdray Polo for the glamorous sporting spectacle (open to spectators). The South Downs walking from the town. The town centre for excellent independent shops.
midhurst.name: Midhurst

midsomer-norton.history: Midsomer Norton grew as a coal mining centre in the now-defunct Somerset coalfield. The town lent its name (and location) to the ITV series Midsomer Murders — though most scenes are filmed across the surrounding villages. The town has good independent character.
midsomer-norton.knownFor: The Midsomer Murders connection (the TV series uses the town), the Somerset coalfields heritage, and a North Somerset market town with unexpected TV fame
midsomer-norton.localTip: The Midsomer Murders TV filming locations around the town. The town centre for local Somerset shopping. Bath is nearby for wider cultural attractions.
midsomer-norton.name: Midsomer Norton

milford-haven.history: Milford Haven's waterway is one of the finest natural deep-water harbours in the world — Nelson described it as one of the greatest ports he had ever seen. The town was founded in 1790 as a planned Quaker settlement. Today it handles more oil and LNG than any other UK port.
milford-haven.knownFor: The Milford Haven Waterway (one of the world's finest natural harbours and the UK's largest energy port), the Pembrokeshire Coast National Park gateway, the fishing heritage, and a Welsh port town of enormous strategic significance
milford-haven.localTip: The Pembrokeshire Coast National Park is directly accessible. The waterway is beautiful for sailing and kayaking. The Celtic Trail cycling. Dale Peninsula for beaches. Local Welsh seafood is outstanding.
milford-haven.name: Milford Haven

millport.history: The Cathedral of the Isles in Millport — built 1849-51 by William Butterfield — is the smallest cathedral in Britain (and one of the most beautiful). Great Cumbrae island, accessible by ferry from Largs, is 11 miles in circumference — a perfect cycle circuit with views to Arran, Bute and the Ayrshire coast.
millport.knownFor: The Cathedral of the Isles (Britain's smallest cathedral), cycling round Great Cumbrae (11 miles of coastal road around the island), the Garrison, and a tiny Scottish island resort of extraordinary charm
millport.localTip: Cycling round the island is one of Scotland's great easy cycling routes — suitable for all abilities. The Cathedral of the Isles is exquisite and free. The Garrison for the island museum. Ice cream in Millport is a tradition.
millport.name: Millport

milngavie.history: Milngavie (pronounced 'Mull-GUY') is the official start of the West Highland Way — 96 miles of walking from here to Fort William. The obelisk in the town centre marks the official starting point. Mugdock Country Park, just north of the town, provides excellent walking and wildlife habitat close to Glasgow.
milngavie.knownFor: The starting point of the West Highland Way (Scotland's most popular long-distance walk), Mugdock Country Park, and a Glasgow suburb with significant walking significance
milngavie.localTip: The West Highland Way obelisk for the official start. Mugdock Country Park for excellent local walking. The town centre for supplies before setting off on the Way.
milngavie.name: Milngavie

milnthorpe.history: Milnthorpe is the main market town for the Arnside and Silverdale AONB — one of the smallest but most diverse AONBs in England, with limestone pavements, salt marshes and ancient woodland. Dallam Tower's deer park on the edge of town is beautiful. The Kent Estuary gives extraordinary views.
milnthorpe.knownFor: The Arnside and Silverdale AONB, the Kent Estuary, Dallam Tower deer park, and a small South Cumbria market town at the gateway to one of England's most beautiful AONBs
milnthorpe.localTip: The Arnside and Silverdale AONB for exceptional walking and wildlife. The Kent Estuary views from Arnside. Dallam Tower deer park. The market for local Cumbrian produce.
milnthorpe.name: Milnthorpe

milton-keynes.history: Milton Keynes was designated a new city in 1967 — its grid road system, shopping centre and planned parks were revolutionary. Bletchley Park, where the Enigma code was broken in WWII, is within the city boundary and is now one of Britain's most important heritage sites. The concrete cows in Campbell Park have become beloved icons.
milton-keynes.knownFor: The grid roads and roundabouts (the planned new city), the concrete cows (David Kemp's iconic public sculpture), the National Museum of Computing (at Bletchley Park), Bletchley Park itself, and Britain's most architecturally ambitious new city
milton-keynes.localTip: Bletchley Park is genuinely outstanding — the Enigma code-breaking story is extraordinary. The National Museum of Computing. The concrete cows in Campbell Park. The Redways cycling network.
milton-keynes.name: Milton Keynes

milton-keynes-south.history: Milton Keynes was designated a new city in 1967. Bletchley Park, within the city boundary, is where the Enigma code was broken in WWII — one of Britain's most important heritage sites.
milton-keynes-south.knownFor: Bletchley Park (where Enigma was broken), the concrete cows, the grid road city, and the National Museum of Computing
milton-keynes-south.localTip: Bletchley Park is outstanding — the Enigma story is extraordinary. The concrete cows in Campbell Park. The Redways cycling network.
milton-keynes-south.name: Milton Keynes

minehead.history: Minehead is the northern terminus of the South West Coast Path — 630 miles to Poole. The town is also home to one of Butlin's most popular resorts. The West Somerset Railway — a magnificent heritage line running inland to Bishop's Lydeard — departs from the town.
minehead.knownFor: Butlin's (one of Butlin's original holiday camps), the start of the South West Coast Path (the longest footpath in Britain), the Exmoor gateway, and a North Somerset seaside town
minehead.localTip: The South West Coast Path marker on the seafront — the start of 630 miles to Poole. The West Somerset Railway for steam trains to Bishops Lydeard. Exmoor walking directly from the town.
minehead.name: Minehead

mirfield.history: Mirfield is home to the Community of the Resurrection — an Anglican monastic community founded in 1892. The monastery has trained over 1,000 priests and is one of the most respected religious communities in the Church of England. The town sits on the River Calder.
mirfield.knownFor: The Community of the Resurrection (the famous Anglican monastery), the Calder Valley, and a West Yorkshire town between Dewsbury and Huddersfield
mirfield.localTip: The Community of the Resurrection chapel and grounds are open for visitors — a place of remarkable peace. The Calder Valley walking. The town centre for local West Yorkshire character.
mirfield.name: Mirfield

mitcham.history: Mitcham Common has been grazed common land since before Domesday. Mitcham was famous for its lavender and herb cultivation — the 'Mitcham Lavender' was sold across the country. The Cricket Green at Mitcham has been a cricket ground since at least 1685, making it one of the oldest in the world.
mitcham.knownFor: Mitcham Common (one of South London's largest commons), the lavender fields (Mitcham was famous for lavender cultivation), the Cricket Green (one of the oldest cricket grounds in the world), and a South London neighbourhood with unexpected rural heritage
mitcham.localTip: Mitcham Common for the surprisingly wild common land. The Cricket Green for the historic ground. The lavender heritage is celebrated locally. The River Wandle walk through the common.
mitcham.name: Mitcham

moffat.history: Moffat developed as a Victorian spa town on the Annan water. The Grey Mare's Tail — a 60-metre waterfall tumbling from Loch Skeen — is one of Scotland's finest waterfalls. The Devil's Beef Tub, a dramatic natural amphitheatre where Border Reivers hid stolen cattle, is just north of the town.
moffat.knownFor: The Annan Valley spa town, the Grey Mare's Tail waterfall (one of Scotland's highest), the Devil's Beef Tub, Moffat toffee, and a small Dumfriesshire spa town of great natural beauty
moffat.localTip: The Grey Mare's Tail walk is outstanding — the waterfall is spectacular and Loch Skeen above is wild. The Devil's Beef Tub viewpoint. Moffat toffee from the local shop. The spa town architecture on the High Street.
moffat.name: Moffat

moira.history: The Battle of Moira in 637 AD was one of the largest battles in early Irish history — the High King Domhnall II defeated the King of Ulster and the King of Dál Riata in a battle so significant it's mentioned in multiple annals. The village has Moira Demesne (a pleasant public park) and good community amenities.
moira.knownFor: The Battle of Moira 637 AD (one of the largest battles in early medieval Ireland), the Moira Demesne, and a small County Armagh village near the Lagan Valley
moira.localTip: The Moira Demesne for pleasant walking. The village centre has good community shops. Lisburn and Belfast are easily accessible nearby.
moira.name: Moira

mold.history: The Mold Gold Cape — found in 1833 and now in the British Museum — is one of the most astonishing Bronze Age gold objects ever discovered, made around 1900–1600 BC. Theatre Clwyd is consistently rated as one of the finest producing theatres in Wales and the UK. The town is the administrative centre of Flintshire.
mold.knownFor: The Mold Gold Cape (one of the most spectacular Bronze Age gold objects ever found), the Theatre Clwyd (one of Wales's finest), and the county town of Flintshire
mold.localTip: Theatre Clwyd is genuinely excellent — nationally celebrated productions. The replica of the Mold Gold Cape is displayed locally. The town market for local Flintshire produce. The Clwydian Range AONB from the town.
mold.name: Mold

moniaive.history: Moniaive is a tiny village in the Cairn Valley of Dumfriesshire. The Green Man Festival, held here annually, is one of Scotland's most celebrated acoustic and folk music festivals. The village has extraordinary community spirit and has won multiple awards for village life. Annie Laurie, the subject of the famous Scottish song, came from nearby.
moniaive.knownFor: The Craigdarroch Arms (one of Scotland's most celebrated village pubs), the Green Man Festival, the Galloway landscape, and a small Dumfriesshire village of great community spirit
moniaive.localTip: The Green Man Festival in July is genuinely special — intimate, beautiful and musically outstanding. The Craigdarroch Arms is an excellent village pub. The Cairn Valley walking is beautiful.
moniaive.name: Moniaive

monmouth.history: King Henry V — the hero of Agincourt — was born at Monmouth Castle in 1387. The Monnow Bridge with its fortified gate tower, built in 1272, is the only remaining bridge gateway in Britain of its kind. The town is at the gateway to the Wye Valley AONB.
monmouth.knownFor: The birthplace of King Henry V, the medieval Monnow Bridge and Gate (the only fortified medieval bridge gateway remaining in Britain), and a Monmouthshire market town of exceptional medieval heritage
monmouth.localTip: The Monnow Gate is extraordinary — walk through a genuine medieval fortress gate still spanning the river. Monmouth Castle for Henry V's birthplace. The Wye Valley walking from the town. The market for local produce.
monmouth.name: Monmouth

montrose.history: James Graham, 1st Marquess of Montrose — who fought brilliantly for Charles I before his execution in 1650 — was born in Montrose. The Montrose Basin Wildlife Reserve is one of the most important sites for pink-footed geese in Scotland. The town has one of the widest High Streets in Scotland.
montrose.knownFor: The Montrose Basin (one of Scotland's most important estuarine nature reserves), the birthplace of James Graham (the Marquess of Montrose, one of Scotland's greatest generals), the wide High Street, and an Angus port town of great maritime heritage
montrose.localTip: Montrose Basin for the extraordinary pink-footed geese (autumn) and ospreys (summer). The High Street is impressively wide. The Montrose Museum for the Marquess's story. The beach is excellent and rarely crowded.
montrose.name: Montrose

morecambe.history: Eric Morecambe — half of Morecambe and Wise, Britain's most beloved comedy double act — took his stage name from the town where he grew up. The Eric Morecambe statue on the promenade has become one of Britain's most visited and photographed public sculptures. The Midland Hotel, built 1933, is a masterpiece of Art Deco design.
morecambe.knownFor: The Eric Morecambe statue (the most beloved public statue in Britain), the Midland Hotel (a magnificent Art Deco building, restored to glory), the bay views (Snowdonia visible on clear days), and a Lancashire seaside resort of enduring charm
morecambe.localTip: The Eric Morecambe statue — doing the Sunshine pose — is genuinely joyful. The Midland Hotel for afternoon tea and the Art Deco interior. The bay views to Snowdonia on clear days. The RSPB Leighton Moss for extraordinary birdsong.
morecambe.name: Morecambe

morecambe-town.history: Eric Morecambe grew up here and took his stage name from the town. The Eric Morecambe statue is one of Britain's most photographed. The Midland Hotel (1933) is a masterpiece of Art Deco design, fully restored.
morecambe-town.knownFor: The Eric Morecambe statue, the Midland Hotel (Art Deco masterpiece), the bay views to Snowdonia, and Lancashire's most beloved seaside resort
morecambe-town.localTip: The Eric Morecambe statue and its Sunshine pose. The Midland Hotel for afternoon tea. Bay views to Snowdonia on clear days.
morecambe-town.name: Morecambe

moretonhampstead.history: Moretonhampstead is a historic moorland market town on the north-eastern edge of Dartmoor. The 17th century colonnaded almshouses are one of the finest vernacular buildings on Dartmoor. The town serves as a gateway to the central Dartmoor plateau.
moretonhampstead.knownFor: The Dartmoor National Park gateway, the medieval almshouses, and a small Devon market town on the edge of Dartmoor
moretonhampstead.localTip: The colonnaded almshouses are beautiful. The B3212 drive west across Dartmoor from here. The town has good independent shops. Grimspound Bronze Age village is a short drive.
moretonhampstead.name: Moretonhampstead

morley.history: Herbert Henry Asquith — Liberal Prime Minister from 1908 to 1916, who led Britain into World War I — was born in Morley in 1852. The town grew as a textile manufacturing centre. Morley Town Hall is an impressive Victorian civic building.
morley.knownFor: Herbert Asquith's birthplace (the Liberal Prime Minister who led Britain into World War I), the market town heritage, and a West Yorkshire town between Leeds and Wakefield
morley.localTip: The Asquith birthplace connection. Morley Town Hall for the Victorian architecture. The town centre market for local produce. Leeds is easily accessible by road and bus.
morley.name: Morley

morpeth.history: Morpeth has been the county town of Northumberland since medieval times. The Chantry on the bridge houses the Northumbrian Craft Centre and the National Bagpipe Collection — the spiritual home of Northumbrian smallpipes. Emily Davison, the suffragette who threw herself under the King's horse at Epsom in 1913, was born here.
morpeth.knownFor: The Northumbrian Pipes (Morpeth Chantry is the national centre for Northumbrian traditional music), the medieval clock tower, Emily Davison's birthplace, and the county town of Northumberland
morpeth.localTip: The Chantry Bagpipe Museum is genuinely fascinating — the Northumbrian smallpipes sound extraordinary. The medieval clock tower. The Wansbeck riverside walk. The Saturday market for local Northumberland produce.
morpeth.name: Morpeth

motherwell.history: Motherwell was the steel capital of Scotland — Ravenscraig steelworks, which closed in 1992, was at its peak one of the largest in Europe. The town's working-class identity is rooted in steel. Motherwell FC has competed in European football and has strong local support.
motherwell.knownFor: The steel heritage (the Ravenscraig steelworks was once Europe's largest), Motherwell FC (a club with European pedigree), the Dalzell Estate, and a Lanarkshire town defined by steel
motherwell.localTip: The Dalzell Country Park along the River Clyde for walking. Motherwell FC at Fir Park for Scottish football. The town centre for local community character. Hamilton is nearby for wider amenities.
motherwell.name: Motherwell

mountain-ash.history: Mountain Ash grew rapidly with the opening of the Deep Duffryn and Nixon's Navigation collieries. The town's Welsh name — Aberpennar — reflects its position at the mouth of the Pennar stream. The valleys community has maintained strong Welsh language culture and choral traditions.
mountain-ash.knownFor: The Cynon Valley, the coal mining heritage, the annual Mountain Ash carnival, and a South Wales valleys community of strong Welsh identity
mountain-ash.localTip: The Cynon Valley cycle trail. The town centre for local community life. Aberdare nearby for wider amenities. The surrounding hills for dramatic Valleys walking.
mountain-ash.name: Mountain Ash

musselburgh.history: Musselburgh Links Golf Course has continuous records of play from 1672 — the oldest in the world. Six Open Championships were held here. The town was a Roman fort settlement.
