stamford-town.history: England's first conservation area (1967). The honey-coloured limestone town centre is virtually unchanged. Burghley House, built for Elizabeth I's chief minister, is one of England's greatest Elizabethan mansions.
stamford-town.knownFor: England's finest stone town (600+ listed buildings, first conservation area in England), Burghley House, and extraordinary architectural completeness
stamford-town.localTip: Walk every street in the town centre. Burghley House and park are magnificent. The George Hotel for the finest coaching inn.
stamford-town.name: Stamford

stanley.history: Beamish — The Living Museum of the North — sits in the Stanley area. The extraordinary open-air museum recreates life in the North East from 1820s to 1950s with working trams, a colliery, a Georgian town and more. Stanley grew as a coal mining community.
stanley.knownFor: The Beamish Museum (the world's most celebrated open-air museum of northern life), the coalfield heritage, and a County Durham town that gave the world's greatest living museum its setting
stanley.localTip: Beamish Museum is genuinely one of the finest museum experiences in the world — allow a full day. The town has strong local community identity. Chester-le-Street is close.
stanley.name: Stanley

stanmore.history: RAF Stanmore — at Bentley Priory — was the headquarters of Fighter Command during the Battle of Britain in 1940. Air Chief Marshal Hugh Dowding directed the defence of Britain from here. The Bentley Priory Museum tells this extraordinary story. Canons Park provides local parkland.
stanmore.knownFor: RAF Stanmore (Battle of Britain control during the Blitz), the Battle of Britain Bunker, Canons Park, and an outer London suburb with significant wartime heritage
stanmore.localTip: Bentley Priory Battle of Britain Museum is genuinely excellent — the Dowding System and the Battle of Britain story. Canons Park for green space. The Jubilee Line for central London.
stanmore.name: Stanmore

stevenage.history: Stevenage was the first New Town designated under the 1946 New Towns Act. The town centre — built from 1959 — was the first purpose-built pedestrian shopping precinct in Britain. The town has significant aerospace and pharmaceutical industries, including GlaxoSmithKline.
stevenage.knownFor: Britain's first New Town (designated 1946), the first purpose-built pedestrian town centre in Britain, and a Hertfordshire town that pioneered post-war urban planning
stevenage.localTip: The town centre pedestrian precinct is historically significant — the first of its kind in Britain. The Old Town for the historic market character. Knebworth House is close for the rock concerts and the house.
stevenage.name: Stevenage

steyning.history: Steyning is a beautiful small market town at the foot of the South Downs. The High Street has excellent medieval and Georgian buildings. The South Downs ridge is walkable directly from the town. The church is partly Saxon.
steyning.knownFor: The beautiful High Street (one of the finest in West Sussex), the South Downs National Park setting, the Steyning Museum, and a West Sussex market town of great character
steyning.localTip: The High Street is genuinely beautiful. The South Downs walking from Steyning is outstanding. The Steyning Museum for local history. The town centre for excellent independent shops.
steyning.name: Steyning

stirling.history: Stirling has been the key strategic point in Scottish history — controlling the lowest crossing of the Forth. Stirling Castle was the favoured residence of the Scottish royal family. William Wallace defeated the English at Stirling Bridge in 1297; Robert the Bruce at Bannockburn in 1314. The Wallace Monument towers above the town.
stirling.knownFor: Stirling Castle (the finest royal palace in Scotland, where Mary Queen of Scots was crowned), the Wallace Monument (commemorating William Wallace), the Bannockburn battlefield (where Robert the Bruce defeated Edward II), and the gateway to the Highlands
stirling.localTip: Stirling Castle is genuinely magnificent — the Great Hall and the Royal Chapel are outstanding. The Wallace Monument for the dramatic hilltop view and William Wallace's broadsword. Bannockburn Heritage Centre for the 1314 battle. The Old Town for excellent independent food.
stirling.name: Stirling

stockport.history: A medieval market town at the confluence of the Goyt and Tame rivers. The railway viaduct (1840) dominates the town centre. Stockport was the hatting capital of the world. The air raid shelters beneath Stockport are one of the largest civilian WWII shelters in Britain.
stockport.knownFor: The viaduct, Stockport Pyramid, the Hat Works museum, the air raid shelters, Robinsons Brewery and a market town with more Victorian industrial heritage than it knows what to do with
stockport.localTip: The WWII air raid shelters beneath the town centre are extraordinary — one of England's great hidden experiences. Market Place for independent food. Robinsons Brewery for a tour. Didsbury is leafy and excellent.
stockport.name: Stockport

stockton-on-tees.history: On 27 September 1825, the Stockton and Darlington Railway carried its first passengers — the world's first regular steam passenger service. The railway transformed the world. The High Street — once the widest in England — retains impressive Victorian architecture. The Tees Barrage is a dramatic modern river structure.
stockton-on-tees.knownFor: The world's first railway passenger service (the Stockton and Darlington Railway, 1825), the High Street (at its Victorian peak, the widest in England), the Tees Barrage, and a Teesside town that launched the railway age
stockton-on-tees.localTip: The railway heritage is marked throughout — the Head of Steam Museum. The Tees Barrage for the watersports. The High Street for the Victorian architecture. The ARC arts centre for culture.
stockton-on-tees.name: Stockton-on-Tees

stoke-on-trent.history: The Six Towns of the Potteries — Burslem, Fenton, Hanley, Longton, Stoke and Tunstall — federated in 1910. Arnold Bennett immortalised the area in his Five Towns novels
stoke-on-trent.knownFor: Wedgwood, the ceramics heritage, the Potteries Museum, Staffordshire oatcakes and a city whose cultural identity is genuinely distinctive
stoke-on-trent.localTip: The Potteries Museum has one of the UK's best ceramics collections. Middleport Pottery is a working Victorian factory and extraordinary. Try a Staffordshire oatcake — you'll understand immediately.
stoke-on-trent.name: Stoke On Trent

stonehaven.history: Dunnottar Castle — perched on a dramatic sea-cliff stack — is one of Scotland's most iconic ruins. The Scottish Crown Jewels were hidden here from Cromwell's army in 1651. Stonehaven's Hogmanay fireball ceremony — where participants swing blazing balls through the town — is one of Scotland's most extraordinary traditions.
stonehaven.knownFor: Dunnottar Castle (the most dramatically sited castle in Scotland, a cliff-top ruin), the Hogmanay fireball ceremony (one of Scotland's most extraordinary fire festivals), the harbour, and a beautiful Kincardineshire fishing town
stonehaven.localTip: Dunnottar Castle is genuinely extraordinary — the clifftop approach is dramatic. The Hogmanay fireball ceremony (31 December) is spectacular. The harbour for fresh Aberdeenshire seafood. The beach for the North Sea coast.
stonehaven.name: Stonehaven

stornoway.history: Stornoway is the only significant town in the Outer Hebrides — the administrative, commercial and cultural hub of Lewis and Harris. Lews Castle, built in 1847, was given to the community in 1923 by Lord Leverhulme. Harris Tweed — the hand-woven cloth protected by Act of Parliament — is produced across the islands.
stornoway.knownFor: The capital of the Outer Hebrides (Lewis and Harris), the Lews Castle (a Victorian baronial castle), the An Lanntair arts centre, the Harris Tweed industry, and the gateway to some of Scotland's most extraordinary landscape and culture
stornoway.localTip: The An Lanntair arts centre for the best of Hebridean culture. The Lews Castle Museum for the extraordinary Lewis Chessmen story (medieval chess pieces found here). The Callanish Standing Stones are 14 miles away — genuinely extraordinary.
stornoway.name: Stornoway

stourbridge.history: Stourbridge was the world's leading centre of fine glassmaking — Venetian glassmakers brought their craft in the 17th century and the tradition grew. Stuart Crystal — among many famous names — was based here. The glass tradition is celebrated at the Red House Glass Cone (one of only four surviving glass cones in England) and the Broadfield House Glass Museum.
stourbridge.knownFor: The glass heritage (Stourbridge was the glassmaking capital of the world in the 19th century), the Stuart Crystal tradition, the Bonded Warehouse, and a Black Country town of great glass heritage
stourbridge.localTip: The Red House Glass Cone is extraordinary — one of only four surviving 18th century glass cones in Britain. The Broadfield House Glass Museum. The town centre for local shopping.
stourbridge.name: Stourbridge

stourport-on-severn.history: Stourport-on-Severn is unique — the only town in England created by the canal age. When James Brindley connected the Staffordshire and Worcestershire Canal to the Severn in 1772, a new town grew from nothing. The Georgian canal basin is the finest of its kind in England.
stourport-on-severn.knownFor: The canal basin (one of the finest Georgian canal junctions in England, built by James Brindley), the River Severn, and a Worcestershire town built entirely by the canal age
stourport-on-severn.localTip: The canal basin is genuinely beautiful — the Georgian warehouses and the boat traffic are outstanding. The River Severn walk. The town for local Worcestershire character.
stourport-on-severn.name: Stourport-on-Severn

stow-on-the-wold.history: Stow-on-the-Wold — the highest point in the Cotswolds at 244 metres — has been a market town since the medieval period. The large market square hosted famous sheep fairs where up to 20,000 animals were sold. The town now has an extraordinary concentration of antique dealers.
stow-on-the-wold.knownFor: The Cotswolds market square (the largest in the Cotswolds), the antique shops (one of England's great antique destinations), the exposed hilltop setting (the highest town in the Cotswolds), and a Gloucestershire Cotswold town of perfect character
stow-on-the-wold.localTip: The antique shops are genuinely excellent — plan a morning. The market square is beautiful. The Cotswolds walking from Stow. The town for outstanding independent food.
stow-on-the-wold.name: Stow-on-the-Wold

strabane.history: John Dunlap — born in Strabane in 1747 — became the official printer to the Continental Congress and printed the first broadside of the American Declaration of Independence in 1776. The town is on the Foyle at the Donegal border. The Derg Valley is beautiful.
strabane.knownFor: The John Dunlap connection (printer of the American Declaration of Independence, born here), the Derg Valley, and a County Tyrone border town with American heritage
strabane.localTip: The John Dunlap heritage is celebrated in the town. The Derg Valley for walking. Derry/Londonderry is close for wider cultural attractions.
strabane.name: Strabane

stranraer.history: Stranraer was the main ferry port for Northern Ireland for over a century — the Larne crossing carried millions of passengers. The ferry moved to Cairnryan in 2011. Castle Kennedy Gardens — the historic landscape of the Earls of Stair — is one of the finest in Scotland. The town is at the head of Loch Ryan.
stranraer.knownFor: The former ferry to Northern Ireland (Stranraer was the main Larne crossing for a century), Castle Kennedy Gardens (one of Scotland's finest), and the main town of the Rhins of Galloway peninsula
stranraer.localTip: Castle Kennedy and Lochinch Castle Gardens are genuinely outstanding — the rhododendrons in May are spectacular. Loch Ryan for watersports. The town centre for local character.
stranraer.name: Stranraer

stratford-london.history: Stratford hosted the 2012 London Olympics — the Games transformed a post-industrial wasteland into the Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park. The ongoing East Bank development is bringing the V&A's new museum, Sadler's Wells East and BBC Music Studios to the area. Westfield Stratford opened in 2011.
stratford-london.knownFor: The Olympic Park (legacy of the 2012 London Olympics), Westfield Stratford City (one of Europe's largest urban shopping centres), the East Bank cultural hub (V&A East, Sadler's Wells, BBC Music Studios), and East London's most dramatically transformed neighbourhood
stratford-london.localTip: The Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park is excellent — the Velodrome and Aquatics Centre are exceptional. Westfield for retail. The East Bank cultural development is transforming the area rapidly.
stratford-london.name: Stratford

stratford-upon-avon.history: William Shakespeare was born in Henley Street, Stratford-upon-Avon in April 1564. The town's many Shakespeare properties — Birthplace, Anne Hathaway's Cottage, Mary Arden's Farm, Hall's Croft — draw millions of visitors annually. The Royal Shakespeare Company at the RSC theatres performs to audiences worldwide.
stratford-upon-avon.knownFor: The birthplace of William Shakespeare (the most influential writer in the English language), the RSC (Royal Shakespeare Company), Anne Hathaway's Cottage, the River Avon, and the world's most visited literary destination
stratford-upon-avon.localTip: The Shakespeare Birthplace is genuinely moving. Anne Hathaway's Cottage for the beautiful thatched cottage and garden. The RSC for world-class Shakespeare. The Avon riverside walk and the chain ferry.
stratford-upon-avon.name: Stratford-upon-Avon

streatham.history: Streatham was a fashionable spa town in the 18th century — Dr Johnson was a regular visitor to the Thrales' home at Streatham Park. Tooting Bec Lido — opened in 1906 — is the largest outdoor swimming pool in Europe. The area has a strong Caribbean and South Asian community.
streatham.knownFor: Streatham Ice Arena (South London's premier ice rink), Tooting Bec Lido (the largest outdoor freshwater swimming pool in Europe), the diverse food scene on Streatham High Road, and a rapidly improving South London neighbourhood
streatham.localTip: Tooting Bec Lido is extraordinary — the largest outdoor swimming pool in Europe, beautiful and popular. Streatham Ice Arena for ice skating. The High Road for excellent diverse food.
streatham.name: Streatham

stroud.history: Stroud grew as a cloth-making centre in the Stroud Valleys. The town has reinvented itself as a hub of independent businesses, arts and sustainable living. The Saturday market — run with extraordinary care and quality — has been called the finest farmers' market in England. Laurie Lee grew up in the Slad Valley above Stroud.
stroud.knownFor: The Saturday market (the finest farmers' market in England by many accounts), the independent food scene, the arts and craft culture, the Stroud Valleys, and a Gloucestershire town of extraordinary independent spirit
stroud.localTip: The Saturday market is genuinely outstanding — one of England's great food market experiences. The Shambles arts centre. The Stroud Valleys walking — the Slad Valley (Laurie Lee country) is beautiful.
stroud.name: Stroud

stroud-town.history: Stroud reinvented itself from a cloth town to England's most independently spirited community. The Saturday market is celebrated nationally. Laurie Lee grew up above the town in the Slad Valley.
stroud-town.knownFor: England's finest Saturday farmers' market, the independent food scene, the Stroud Valleys, and an extraordinary independent spirit
stroud-town.localTip: The Saturday market is one of England's great food experiences. The Slad Valley walk. The Shambles arts centre.
stroud-town.name: Stroud

sudbury.history: Thomas Gainsborough — the great 18th century portrait and landscape painter — was born in Sudbury in 1727 and grew up in the town. Gainsborough's House, his birthplace, is now an excellent museum and gallery. The River Stour — which Constable also painted downstream — flows through the town.
sudbury.knownFor: The birthplace of Thomas Gainsborough (one of England's greatest painters), the Gainsborough's House museum, the River Stour (Constable Country), and a Suffolk wool town of great artistic heritage
sudbury.localTip: Gainsborough's House is genuinely excellent — the collection and the restored house are outstanding. The Stour Valley walk from Sudbury. The market for local Suffolk produce.
sudbury.name: Sudbury

sunderland.history: Sunderland was one of the world's greatest shipbuilding centres — at its peak launching more ships than anywhere on earth. The last Sunderland-built ship was launched in 1988. The city has reinvented itself with culture and technology. The Glass Centre celebrates the Wearside glass tradition.
sunderland.knownFor: The National Glass Centre (celebrating Sunderland's glass heritage), the Stadium of Light (Sunderland AFC), the Roker and Seaburn beaches, and a North East city reinventing itself after shipbuilding
sunderland.localTip: The National Glass Centre is free and genuinely impressive. The Stadium of Light for Sunderland AFC. Roker Beach for the North East coast. The Winter Gardens at Museum and Winter Gardens.
sunderland.name: Sunderland

surbiton.history: Surbiton is widely believed to have inspired 'The Good Life' — the BBC sitcom about suburban self-sufficiency. The town has excellent Victorian and Edwardian architecture. The Thames towpath provides riverside walking. Kingston upon Thames is immediately adjacent.
surbiton.knownFor: The fictional inspiration for 'The Good Life' TV series, the Thames riverside, the Victorian and Edwardian architecture, and an affluent Kingston suburb with strong community character
surbiton.localTip: The Thames towpath walk. The town centre for excellent independent food. Kingston is minutes away for wider amenities. The Victorian and Edwardian architecture is worth exploring.
surbiton.name: Surbiton

sutton.history: Nonsuch Park — once the site of Henry VIII's extraordinary Nonsuch Palace (built 1538, demolished 1682) — is now a beautiful country park. The town has good community character and improving independent food.
sutton.knownFor: The Nonsuch Park (site of Henry VIII's demolished palace), the town centre, and a South London borough with good green spaces
sutton.localTip: Nonsuch Park is beautiful — the palace site is marked and the grounds are excellent. The town centre for local shopping. Cheam village for historic character.
sutton.name: Sutton

sutton-coldfield.history: Sutton Coldfield has been a Royal Town since 1528 — Henry VIII granted the Royal Charter. Sutton Park — 2,400 acres of ancient woodland, heathland and pools — is one of the largest urban parks in Europe. The town is consistently one of the most desirable places to live in the Midlands.
sutton-coldfield.knownFor: Sutton Park (one of the largest urban parks in Europe), the Royal Town status (one of only a handful of Royal Towns in England), the Gracechurch Centre, and Birmingham's most affluent suburb
sutton-coldfield.localTip: Sutton Park is genuinely outstanding — one of the great urban parks in England, often surprisingly wild. The town centre for excellent independent food. The Gracechurch Centre for retail.
sutton-coldfield.name: Sutton Coldfield

sutton-in-ashfield.history: Sutton-in-Ashfield grew as a hosiery and textiles centre. The town serves the local Ashfield district. Sherwood Forest and Robin Hood country are accessible from the town.
sutton-in-ashfield.knownFor: The hosiery and textile heritage, the Sherwood Forest gateway, and a Nottinghamshire market town in the Ashfield district
sutton-in-ashfield.localTip: Sherwood Forest is close for the Major Oak and Robin Hood heritage. The town centre for local Nottinghamshire shopping. Newstead Abbey (Byron's home) is nearby.
sutton-in-ashfield.name: Sutton-in-Ashfield

swadlincote.history: Swadlincote was a centre of the South Derbyshire coalfield. The National Forest project — creating a new forest across 200 square miles of the East Midlands — has transformed the area. The Conkers visitor centre is the National Forest's main attraction. The town is reinventing itself through green tourism.
swadlincote.knownFor: The National Forest (Swadlincote is at the heart of the National Forest, Britain's largest new forest), the Conkers visitor centre, and a Derbyshire town transformed by the National Forest project
swadlincote.localTip: The National Forest walking and cycling is excellent. Conkers is good for families. The Ashby Canal towpath for peaceful walking. The town centre for local character.
swadlincote.name: Swadlincote

swaffham.history: Howard Carter — who discovered Tutankhamun's tomb in 1922 — was born in Swaffham in 1874. The Market Cross (1783) is a magnificent domed rotunda in the market place. The Saturday market has been held in Swaffham since the medieval period.
swaffham.knownFor: The Bridewell (a magnificent Georgian market place), the Market Cross (an exceptional 18th century domed structure), Howard Carter's connection (the discoverer of Tutankhamun's tomb was from here), and a Norfolk market town of great character
swaffham.localTip: The Market Cross is extraordinary. The Saturday market for local Norfolk produce. The Howard Carter connection at the museum. The Breckland countryside walking.
swaffham.name: Swaffham

swanage.history: Swanage provided much of London's limestone paving and building stone — the town became known as 'the quarrymen's town'. John Mowlem and George Burt brought various London monuments and facades to Swanage when London was redeveloped. The Swanage Railway runs through the beautiful Purbeck Hills to Corfe Castle.
swanage.knownFor: The Swanage Railway (a heritage steam line to Corfe Castle and Wareham), Purbeck limestone (London's pavements are largely Swanage stone), the Victorian seaside character, and a Dorset resort town of great heritage
swanage.localTip: The Swanage Railway to Corfe Castle is outstanding. The stone heritage throughout the town. The Durlston Country Park for the cliff walks. The beach is excellent.
swanage.name: Swanage

swanley.history: Swanley is a commuter town in North Kent with good London connections. Swanley Park provides excellent leisure facilities. The town is well placed for the Kent countryside and Eynsford.
swanley.knownFor: The Swanley Park (excellent leisure park with boating lake), the Crockenhill area, and a North Kent commuter town between London and the Garden of England
swanley.localTip: Swanley Park for boating and family leisure. Eynsford village for the Roman villa and ford. The North Downs for walking. London is 20 minutes.
swanley.name: Swanley

swansea.history: Dylan Thomas — the greatest Welsh poet in the English language — was born in Swansea in 1914 and described it as 'an ugly, lovely town'. The Gower Peninsula, designated Britain's first Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty in 1956, begins at the city's western edge. The National Waterfront Museum tells the story of Welsh industry. Swansea was heavily bombed in WWII.
swansea.knownFor: The birthplace of Dylan Thomas, the Swansea Market (the finest covered market in Wales), the Gower Peninsula (Britain's first AONB), the National Waterfront Museum, and Wales's second city with extraordinary coastal and literary heritage
swansea.localTip: The Dylan Thomas Centre and Birthplace. The Swansea Market for the finest laverbread and cockles in Wales. Three Cliffs Bay on Gower — one of the most beautiful bays in Britain. The National Waterfront Museum (free).
swansea.name: Swansea

swindon.history: Swindon grew from a small village to a major town when the GWR works were established in 1843. At its peak, the GWR locomotive works employed 14,000 people and was one of the largest engineering works in the world. STEAM museum occupies the original engine sheds. The Magic Roundabout (five mini-roundabouts in a circle) has achieved legendary status.
swindon.knownFor: STEAM Museum (the Great Western Railway museum in the original GWR engine sheds), the Designer Outlet (one of England's most popular), the Magic Roundabout (the famous traffic junction), and a Wiltshire new town that grew around the Great Western Railway
swindon.localTip: STEAM is genuinely outstanding and excellent value. The Designer Outlet for retail. The Magic Roundabout for the extraordinary traffic engineering. The Old Town for independent food.
swindon.name: Swindon

swinton-south-yorkshire.history: Swinton grew as part of the South Yorkshire industrial belt. The town serves the Don and Dearne valleys. The surrounding area has strong coalfield and industrial heritage.
swinton-south-yorkshire.knownFor: The Don Valley, the Dearne Valley, and a South Yorkshire community between Rotherham and Mexborough
swinton-south-yorkshire.localTip: Rotherham is close for wider amenities. The Dearne Valley for walking. The community has strong local identity.
swinton-south-yorkshire.name: Swinton

tadcaster.history: Tadcaster's magnesium limestone — the distinctive cream-coloured stone — built York Minster, the Shambles and much of historic York. The town has been a brewing centre for centuries — Samuel Smith's Brewery (the oldest in Yorkshire, founded 1758) and the former John Smith's works are major employers.
tadcaster.knownFor: The brewery town (Samuel Smith's and Molson Coors have major breweries here), the magnesium limestone (the stone that built York Minster), the River Wharfe, and a North Yorkshire market town that supplied York's building stone and beer
tadcaster.localTip: The Samuel Smith's brewery pubs are excellent — the Old Brewery is a genuinely historic experience. The River Wharfe walk and the viaduct. The stone heritage throughout the town.
tadcaster.name: Tadcaster

tain.history: Tain was granted Royal Burgh status by Malcolm III in 1066 — making it the oldest Royal Burgh in Scotland. Glenmorangie Distillery, founded in 1843, is one of the most celebrated Highland single malts. The Tain Through Time museum tells the story of this ancient burgh.
tain.knownFor: The Glenmorangie Distillery (one of Scotland's most celebrated whiskies, made here), the Tain Through Time museum, and the oldest Royal Burgh in Scotland
tain.localTip: Glenmorangie Distillery tours are excellent. The Tain Through Time museum for the oldest Royal Burgh story. The Dornoch Firth views. The town centre for local character.
tain.name: Tain

tamworth.history: Tamworth was the royal capital of Mercia — the most powerful Anglo-Saxon kingdom — in the 8th and 9th centuries. Aethelflaed, the Lady of the Mercians, made it her capital. Tamworth Castle — built on a Saxon motte — still commands the town. Robert Peel, founder of the Metropolitan Police, was MP for Tamworth.
tamworth.knownFor: The Saxon capital of Mercia (Tamworth Castle was the royal palace of the Kings of Mercia), the Snowdome (indoor ski slope), the shopping (Ventura Park), and a Staffordshire town of ancient royal heritage
tamworth.localTip: Tamworth Castle is genuinely excellent — the Saxon royal capital story is well told. The Snowdome for skiing in Staffordshire. The town centre and Ventura Park for retail.
tamworth.name: Tamworth

taunton.history: The county town of Somerset since the medieval period. The castle was built by the Normans and is where Judge Jeffreys held the Bloody Assizes after Monmouth's Rebellion in 1685. The Somerset Levels surrounding the town are a unique landscape.
taunton.knownFor: The Somerset County Cricket Ground (one of England's most beautiful), the castle, the Vivary Park, real cider and a county town that takes its food seriously
taunton.localTip: The Saturday market in Taunton is excellent for Somerset produce — cider, cheese and cider brandy. The cricket ground is beautiful even if you don't follow cricket. East Street for independent food.
taunton.name: Taunton

tavistock.history: Sir Francis Drake was born at Crowndale, near Tavistock, in around 1540. The town is a Dartmoor stannary town — tin and copper were assayed here. The Pannier Market is one of the finest in Devon. Tavistock is a Gateway to the western Dartmoor.
tavistock.knownFor: Sir Francis Drake's birthplace (the circumnavigator of the globe was born near Tavistock), the Pannier Market (one of Devon's finest), the copper mining heritage (Tavistock was a stannary town), and a Devon market town of extraordinary history
tavistock.localTip: The Pannier Market is excellent. The Drake statue and connection. Western Dartmoor walking from Tavistock. The canal trail to Morwellham Quay for the copper mining story.
tavistock.name: Tavistock

tayport.history: Tayport sits opposite Dundee at the southern end of the Tay Rail Bridge. Tentsmuir Point — a National Nature Reserve — has one of Scotland's finest beaches with extensive pinewood behind. The area has good walking and wildlife.
tayport.knownFor: The Tay Bridge views (across to Dundee), the Tentsmuir Forest and Beach (one of Scotland's finest pinewood beaches), and a small Fife community at the southern end of the Tay Rail Bridge
tayport.localTip: Tentsmuir Beach and Forest are genuinely outstanding — the beach backed by pines is beautiful. The Tay Bridge views. The cycle path along the Tay.
tayport.name: Tayport

teignmouth.history: Teignmouth was where John Keats spent the winter of 1818 and wrote some of his finest poetry. The town has a pleasant Regency character. The Shaldon ferry — one of Devon's smallest and most charming — crosses the Teign to the village. The red cliffs of the Jurassic Coast frame the town.
teignmouth.knownFor: The Grand Pier (one of Devon's finest), the Shaldon ferry (the tiny passenger ferry to Shaldon across the Teign), the red cliffs, and a South Devon resort town of great natural beauty
teignmouth.localTip: The Shaldon ferry is charming and free. The beach and pier. The red cliff walk north. The town centre for independent character.
teignmouth.name: Teignmouth

telford.history: The Ironbridge Gorge is where the Industrial Revolution began — Abraham Darby first smelted iron with coke here in 1709. The Iron Bridge of 1779 was the world's first cast-iron structure. Ten museums in the gorge tell the story of how this small valley changed human history. Telford new town was created to house the area's population.
telford.knownFor: The Ironbridge Gorge (a UNESCO World Heritage Site and the birthplace of the Industrial Revolution), the Iron Bridge (the world's first cast-iron bridge, 1779), and a Shropshire new town created around humanity's most significant industrial site
telford.localTip: The Ironbridge Museums are genuinely extraordinary — the Iron Bridge, Blists Hill Victorian Town, the Coalport China Museum. Allow two full days. The gorge itself is beautiful.
telford.name: Telford

tempo.history: Tempo is a small rural village in County Fermanagh. The surrounding Fermanagh Lakelands landscape is beautiful. Tempo Manor was the seat of the Langham family.
tempo.knownFor: The Fermanagh Lakelands, the Tempo Manor area, and a small County Fermanagh village serving the rural community
tempo.localTip: The Fermanagh Lakelands walking and fishing nearby. Enniskillen is the main service centre. The rural Fermanagh landscape.
tempo.name: Tempo

tenby.history: Tenby's medieval walls — built in the 13th century and remarkably complete — make it one of the finest walled towns in Wales. The harbour, with its painted houses and boats, is one of the most photographed scenes in Britain. Caldey Island, accessible by boat, has been a monastic site since the 6th century.
tenby.knownFor: The medieval town walls (one of the finest in Wales), the harbour (one of the most photographed in Britain), Caldey Island (a working Cistercian monastery), and the most picturesque walled town in Wales
tenby.localTip: The town walls walk is outstanding. The harbour at any time is beautiful. The Caldey Island boat trip for the Cistercian monastery and the chocolate and perfume. North and South Beaches are excellent.
tenby.name: Tenby

tenterden.history: Tenterden's High Street — broad, tree-lined and bordered by fine Georgian and weatherboarded buildings — is one of the most beautiful in Kent. The Kent and East Sussex Railway runs from Tenterden to Bodiam Castle in East Sussex. The town was an important cloth centre in the medieval period.
tenterden.knownFor: The Kent and East Sussex Railway (one of England's finest narrow-gauge heritage railways), the broad High Street (one of the finest in Kent), the Weald of Kent, and a Kent market town of great beauty
tenterden.localTip: The Kent and East Sussex Railway to Bodiam is excellent — steam through the Rother Valley. The High Street is genuinely beautiful. The Weald of Kent walking. The town for excellent independent food.
tenterden.name: Tenterden

tetbury.history: Highgrove House — King Charles III's private residence — is 2 miles from Tetbury. The house and organic gardens are open for guided visits. Tetbury has an extraordinary concentration of antique dealers. The Chipping Steps — medieval cobbled steps — are the town's most distinctive feature.
tetbury.knownFor: Prince Charles's Highgrove House (nearby, with gardens open to visitors), the antique shops (Tetbury is one of England's finest antique centres), the Chipping Steps (a medieval flight of steps), and a Gloucestershire Cotswold town of Royal connection
tetbury.localTip: Highgrove garden visits are genuinely outstanding — book well in advance. The antique shops are excellent — one of the Cotswolds' finest destinations. The Chipping Steps for medieval character. The Tetbury Goods Shed for independent food.
tetbury.name: Tetbury

tewkesbury.history: Tewkesbury Abbey — spared at the Dissolution because the townspeople bought it — has one of the finest Norman interiors in England and a magnificent tower. The Battle of Tewkesbury in 1471 effectively ended the Wars of the Roses, with the Lancastrian cause destroyed. The medieval street plan is virtually unchanged.
tewkesbury.knownFor: Tewkesbury Abbey (one of the finest Norman churches in England, surviving the Dissolution with its extraordinary tower), the Battle of Tewkesbury 1471 (the last Lancastrian stand in the Wars of the Roses), the medieval street plan, and a Gloucestershire market town of exceptional completeness
tewkesbury.localTip: The Abbey is magnificent and free. The medieval alleyways behind the High Street are extraordinary. The battlefield walk. The Mythe Bridge walk for the Avon and Severn confluence views.
tewkesbury.name: Tewkesbury

thame.history: Thame has one of Oxfordshire's finest market streets — broad, tree-lined and bordered by medieval and Georgian buildings. The Thame Show — held every September — claims to be the largest one-day agricultural show in England. John Hampden (the Parliamentary hero of the Civil War) was mortally wounded nearby.
thame.knownFor: The broad High Street (one of Oxfordshire's finest), the Thame Show (the largest one-day agricultural show in England), and a beautiful Oxfordshire market town
thame.localTip: The High Street and market are genuinely beautiful. The Thame Show in September is excellent. The town centre for outstanding independent food. The Thame Museum.
thame.name: Thame

thetford.history: Thomas Paine — whose Common Sense (1776) and The Rights of Man (1791) inspired the American and French Revolutions — was born in Thetford in 1737. Thetford Priory is a significant medieval ruin. The Breckland — a distinctive sandy heathland — surrounds the town.
thetford.knownFor: The birthplace of Thomas Paine (the American Revolutionary author of Common Sense), Thetford Priory, the Breckland landscape, and a Norfolk town of American founding heritage
thetford.localTip: The Thomas Paine statue is celebrated. The Ancient House Museum. Thetford Forest for cycling and walking. The Priory ruins for the medieval heritage.
thetford.name: Thetford

thirsk.history: James Herriot — the pen name of Alf Wight — was a vet in Thirsk (the model for his fictional 'Darrowby') for his entire working life. All Creatures Great and Small was set here. The World of James Herriot museum in his actual surgery is excellent. The market square is one of Yorkshire's finest.
thirsk.knownFor: The James Herriot connection (the vet and author was based in Thirsk, the model for 'Darrowby'), the World of James Herriot museum, the market square, and a North Yorkshire market town of great literary and agricultural character
thirsk.localTip: The World of James Herriot museum is genuinely excellent. The market square for local Yorkshire produce. The Hambleton Hills walking. The racecourse for North Yorkshire flat racing.
thirsk.name: Thirsk

thornbury.history: Thornbury Castle — begun by Edward Stafford, 3rd Duke of Buckingham, who was executed in 1521 before it was completed — is the only Tudor castle in England operating as a hotel. Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn stayed here in 1535. The town has a pleasant market character.
thornbury.knownFor: Thornbury Castle (the only Tudor castle in England to have become a hotel, where Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn stayed), and a South Gloucestershire market town
thornbury.localTip: Thornbury Castle hotel is worth visiting even for a meal — the Tudor setting is extraordinary. The town centre for local community character. Bristol is close for wider amenities.
thornbury.name: Thornbury

thurnscoe.history: Thurnscoe grew around Hickleton Main Colliery. The Dearne Valley provides local walking and cycling routes. The community has strong South Yorkshire coalfield identity.
thurnscoe.knownFor: The Dearne Valley, the mining heritage, and a South Yorkshire former colliery community near Barnsley
thurnscoe.localTip: The Dearne Valley Country Park for walking. Barnsley and Doncaster are close for wider amenities. The community has strong local identity.
thurnscoe.name: Thurnscoe

thurso.history: Thurso is the most northerly town on the British mainland. The Pentland Firth — between Thurso and Orkney — has one of the most powerful tidal currents in the world and is being harnessed for tidal energy. Thurso East produces world-class surfing waves. The Dounreay nuclear site, now being decommissioned, dominated the 20th century.
thurso.knownFor: The most northerly town on the British mainland, the Pentland Firth (one of the most powerful tidal currents in the world), the Dounreay nuclear site, the surfing (Thurso East is one of Europe's finest reef breaks), and a remote Highland town at the top of Britain
thurso.localTip: Thurso East for world-class surfing — one of Europe's finest reef breaks. The Pentland Firth views and the Orkney ferry at Scrabster. The Caithness Glass visitor centre. The Castle of Mey (Queen Mother's home) is 20 miles away.
thurso.name: Thurso

tidworth.history: Tidworth is a garrison town on Salisbury Plain — one of Britain's major Army bases. The barracks and training facilities have been here since the late 19th century. Salisbury Plain provides vast military training grounds and is home to many prehistoric sites.
tidworth.knownFor: The garrison town (one of Britain's major military bases, with a long Army history), Salisbury Plain, and a Wiltshire army community
tidworth.localTip: Salisbury Plain archaeology nearby (though access is restricted). Salisbury is close for excellent heritage and food. The community has strong military identity.
tidworth.name: Tidworth

tipton.history: Tipton is a quintessential Black Country town — iron and glass manufacturing, canals and community. The Dudley Canal and Dudley Tunnel (3,172 yards — one of England's longest) pass through the area. The town has strong working-class industrial heritage.
tipton.knownFor: The Black Country heritage, the Dudley Canal (with Dudley Tunnel — one of England's longest canal tunnels), and a Black Country town defined by its industrial heart
tipton.localTip: Dudley Canal Trust boat trips through Dudley Tunnel are genuinely extraordinary. The Black Country Living Museum (nearby) for the industrial story. Dudley Castle for the views.
tipton.name: Tipton

tiverton.history: Tiverton Castle — a mixture of Norman, medieval and Georgian architecture — is an extraordinary survival, still inhabited. The Grand Western Canal — opened 1814 — is one of England's most beautiful remaining horse-drawn barge routes. The town grew as a wool and lace manufacturing centre.
tiverton.knownFor: Tiverton Castle (a surprisingly complete medieval castle), the Grand Western Canal (one of England's most beautiful horse-drawn canal routes), and a Devon market town of great character
tiverton.localTip: Tiverton Castle is genuinely excellent and privately owned — the collections are fascinating. The Grand Western Canal horse-drawn barge trip is charming. The pannier market for local Devon produce.
tiverton.name: Tiverton

tiverton-town.history: Tiverton Castle is an extraordinary inhabited medieval castle. The Grand Western Canal (1814) is one of England's finest horse-drawn barge routes.
tiverton-town.knownFor: Tiverton Castle (a complete medieval castle still inhabited), the Grand Western Canal horse-drawn barges, and a Devon market town
tiverton-town.localTip: Tiverton Castle is excellent. The Grand Western Canal horse barge is charming. The pannier market for local produce.
tiverton-town.name: Tiverton

tividale.history: Tividale is a Black Country community in the Metropolitan Borough of Sandwell. The area has strong industrial heritage. The Rowley Hills provide local green space and walking.
tividale.knownFor: The Black Country heritage, the Rowley Hills, and a West Midlands community in the Sandwell borough
tividale.localTip: The Rowley Hills for walking and views. The Black Country Living Museum is close. Dudley for wider amenities.
tividale.name: Tividale

tobermory.history: Tobermory's multicoloured harbourfront buildings — painted in vivid reds, yellows and blues — are one of Scotland's most famous images. The children's TV show Balamory was set and filmed here. Tobermory Distillery, founded 1798, is the only distillery on Mull. The surrounding waters have excellent whale, dolphin and basking shark sightings.
tobermory.knownFor: The coloured harbourfront (one of Scotland's most photographed — and the inspiration for Balamory), the Tobermory Whisky Distillery, the whale watching (Mull has some of Britain's best), and the capital of the Isle of Mull
tobermory.localTip: The harbourfront is beautiful at any time — the reflections in the water are extraordinary. Tobermory Distillery for the island whisky. Whale watching boat trips from the harbour. The An Tobar arts centre.
tobermory.name: Tobermory

tonbridge.history: Tonbridge Castle's gatehouse — built around 1260 — is one of the finest examples of medieval military architecture in England, virtually intact. Tonbridge School, founded in 1553, is one of England's great public schools. The town sits on the Medway with good walking.
tonbridge.knownFor: Tonbridge Castle (the finest castle gatehouse in England, surviving almost intact), the River Medway, Tonbridge School (one of England's great public schools), and a Kent market town of medieval and educational significance
tonbridge.localTip: The Castle gatehouse (free exterior, paid interior) is genuinely magnificent. The Medway riverside walk. The town centre for good independent food. The local antique and independent shops.
tonbridge.name: Tonbridge

toome.history: Toomebridge has been the site of eel fishing on the lower Bann since the 7th century — the fishery is one of the most ancient continuous fisheries in the world. The eels migrate from the Sargasso Sea to the Bann, a journey of thousands of miles. The fishery has recently declined due to European eel population collapse.
toome.knownFor: The Toome Eel Fishery (the world's largest eel fishery by catch, operating since the 7th century), the River Bann, and a small Antrim village of extraordinary ecological significance
toome.localTip: The Eel Fishery heritage is extraordinary. The River Bann for local walking. Lough Neagh nearby for the largest freshwater lake in the British Isles.
toome.name: Toomebridge

tooting.history: Tooting has one of London's most vibrant South Asian communities. Tooting Market and Broadway Market have been the commercial heart for decades. Tooting Bec Lido — opened 1906 — is Europe's largest outdoor freshwater swimming pool. Sadiq Khan, Mayor of London, was born and grew up here.
tooting.knownFor: Tooting Bec Lido (Europe's largest outdoor pool), the Tooting Market and Broadway Market, the South Asian food scene (one of London's finest), and Sadiq Khan's birthplace
tooting.localTip: Tooting Bec Lido in summer is extraordinary. The South Asian food on Tooting High Street is outstanding — some of London's best curry. The markets for fresh produce. The Tooting Comedy Club.
tooting.name: Tooting

torquay.history: The English Riviera grew from a small fishing village into a premier Victorian resort, drawing visitors for its Mediterranean-like microclimate. Agatha Christie was born here in 1890. The palm trees along the seafront are real.
torquay.knownFor: The English Riviera, Agatha Christie's birthplace, Torre Abbey, the harbour and a Victorian resort that's reinventing itself for the 21st century
torquay.localTip: The harbour area has the best independent restaurants. Cockington village — just inland — is extraordinarily preserved and free. Agatha Christie's Greenway is a National Trust gem.
torquay.name: Torquay

totnes.history: One of Devon's oldest towns, with evidence of settlement since Iron Age times. The Norman castle sits above the Dart. Dartington Hall nearby has been a centre of arts and education since 1925
totnes.knownFor: The Totnes Pound (local currency), Dartington Estate, the independent spirit, the castle and a town that has resisted every chain shop for decades
totnes.localTip: The High Street on a Saturday morning is an event in itself. Dartington Estate for a full afternoon — the gardens, the Barn cinema and the shops. Kayak the Dart if weather allows.
totnes.name: Totnes

tottenham.history: Tottenham Hotspur's new stadium — opened 2019 — is one of the finest sports venues in the world with a retractable grass pitch. Bruce Castle, a remarkable Tudor building in a public park, houses a local history museum. The area has a diverse community with strong Caribbean and West African heritage.
tottenham.knownFor: Tottenham Hotspur FC (the new Spurs stadium is one of the finest in the world), Bruce Castle (a Tudor manor house with a museum), the High Road diverse food scene, and a North London neighbourhood of rapid change
tottenham.localTip: The Spurs Stadium tour is genuinely excellent — one of the world's great football stadiums. Bruce Castle Museum is free and fascinating. The High Road for diverse food. Markfield Park for the River Lea and wetlands.
tottenham.name: Tottenham

tottington.history: Tottington is a village in the Bury Metropolitan Borough. Holcombe Hill — topped by the Peel Monument commemorating Sir Robert Peel — gives extraordinary views over Greater Manchester. The village is close to the East Lancashire Railway.
tottington.knownFor: The Holcombe Hill (with the Peel Monument, a prominent landmark), the East Lancashire Railway nearby, and a Bury borough village in the Irwell Valley
tottington.localTip: The Holcombe Hill walk to the Peel Monument gives outstanding views. Ramsbottom is close for excellent independent food. The East Lancashire Railway nearby.
tottington.name: Tottington

towcester.history: Towcester sits on Watling Street — the Roman road from London to Wales — and was the Roman town of Lactodorum. Silverstone Motor Racing Circuit — home of the British Grand Prix — is 4 miles away. The town has been a coaching stop since the Romans.
towcester.knownFor: The Roman town (the Watling Street staging post, called Lactodorum), the Silverstone Circuit nearby (British Grand Prix), and a Northamptonshire market town on the old Roman road
towcester.localTip: Silverstone for the British Grand Prix and race events. The town centre for local character. The Roman heritage on Watling Street. Stowe Gardens (NT) is close.
towcester.name: Towcester

tredegar.history: Aneurin Bevan — born in Tredegar in 1897 — created the National Health Service as Minister of Health in 1948. The NHS was partly inspired by the Tredegar Workmen's Medical Aid Society, which provided healthcare to local miners long before the NHS. The Tredegar Iron Works was one of Wales's most important.
tredegar.knownFor: Aneurin Bevan's birthplace (the creator of the NHS was born here), the Tredegar Iron Works heritage, and a South Wales valleys town of global healthcare significance
tredegar.localTip: The Aneurin Bevan heritage is celebrated throughout the town. The Bedwellty House and Park for the Tredegar Iron masters' history. The town centre for local community life.
tredegar.name: Tredegar

troon.history: Royal Troon Golf Club has hosted the Open Championship nine times. The town's beach is excellent. The Ayrshire coast gives views to Arran, Ailsa Craig and the Mull of Kintyre.
troon.knownFor: Royal Troon Golf Club (one of the world's great links, host of multiple Open Championships), the beach, and an Ayrshire coastal resort
troon.localTip: Royal Troon for world-class links golf. The beach at Troon South or Barassie. The Ayrshire coast views. Ayr is close for wider amenities.
troon.name: Troon

truro.history: Cornwall's only city and its administrative capital. Truro's Cathedral was the first new Anglican cathedral built in England after St Paul's in London — completed in 1910. The city's Georgian and Victorian architecture reflects its stannary and port history.
truro.knownFor: Cornwall's only city (a cathedral built 1880-1910), the Lemon Quay food market, Hall for Cornwall and a Victorian city that's the commercial and cultural capital of the Duchy
truro.localTip: The Lemon Quay farmers market on Wednesdays and Saturdays is exceptional — the best of Cornwall's food producers. Hall for Cornwall for excellent arts programming. Lemon Street for independent shopping.
truro.name: Truro

tunbridge-wells.history: The chalybeate spring discovered in 1606 made Tunbridge Wells the first English spa resort. The Pantiles — laid out in the 17th century — is one of the finest colonnaded walks in southern England. The town was granted Royal status by Edward VII in 1909. 'Disgusted of Tunbridge Wells' became a byword for suburban conservatism.
tunbridge-wells.knownFor: The Pantiles (the finest Georgian colonnade in southern England), the chalybeate spring (discovered 1606 and still flowing), the phrase 'Disgusted of Tunbridge Wells', Dunorlan Park, and a Kent spa town of extraordinary completeness
tunbridge-wells.localTip: The Pantiles is genuinely beautiful — the spring still flows. The Pantiles area for excellent independent food. Dunorlan Park. The Chalybeate Spring for a sip of the original spa water.
tunbridge-wells.name: Royal Tunbridge Wells

turriff.history: The Turriff Show — held annually since 1864 — is one of the finest agricultural shows in north-east Scotland. Delgatie Castle, a 15th century tower house open to the public, is nearby. The town serves the Formartine farming community.
turriff.knownFor: The Turriff Show (one of Scotland's finest agricultural shows), the Delgatie Castle, and a small Aberdeenshire market town serving the Formartine area
turriff.localTip: The Turriff Show in August is excellent. Delgatie Castle for the tower house heritage. The town centre for local Aberdeenshire character.
turriff.name: Turriff

twickenham.history: Twickenham has hosted England rugby union since 1909. The stadium — capacity 82,000 — is the world's largest dedicated rugby union ground. Marble Hill House is a beautiful 18th century Palladian villa. Alexander Pope had his villa here and planted one of the most famous gardens in England.
twickenham.knownFor: Twickenham Stadium (the world's largest rugby union stadium and home of England rugby), Marble Hill House, Orleans House Gallery, the Thames riverside, and one of the most pleasant of London's riverside suburbs
twickenham.localTip: Twickenham Stadium tours are excellent. Marble Hill for the Palladian house and riverside park. The Thames towpath to Richmond. The Eel Pie Island for the legendary music venue heritage.
twickenham.name: Twickenham

tyldesley.history: Tyldesley grew as a coal mining and cotton manufacturing community. The town has strong working-class community identity. The area has good connections to Leigh and Wigan.
tyldesley.knownFor: The Leigh area coalfield heritage, the Mosley Common Colliery history, and a Greater Manchester community between Leigh and Atherton
tyldesley.localTip: Leigh is close for wider amenities. The community has strong local identity. The surrounding Greater Manchester countryside for walking.
tyldesley.name: Tyldesley

tynemouth.history: Tynemouth Priory — on a dramatic headland at the mouth of the Tyne — was founded in the 7th century and is one of the finest ruined monasteries in the North East. The Long Sands beach is excellent for families and surfers. The Tynemouth Market, held in the Victorian station, is one of the North East's finest.
tynemouth.knownFor: Tynemouth Priory and Castle (a dramatic headland ruin), the Long Sands beach (one of the finest on the North East coast), the Tynemouth Market, and a Tyne and Wear coastal village of great beauty and heritage
tynemouth.localTip: The Priory and Castle headland gives extraordinary Tyne mouth views (English Heritage). Long Sands beach is excellent. The Tynemouth Market in the Victorian station is outstanding. The town centre for excellent independent food.
tynemouth.name: Tynemouth

tywyn.history: The Talyllyn Railway — closed by its owner's death in 1950 — was saved by volunteers in 1951, making it the world's first preserved railway. It runs inland to Nant Gwernol through beautiful Snowdonia scenery. St Cadfan's Stone in the church has the oldest known inscription in the Welsh language (7th century).
tywyn.knownFor: The Talyllyn Railway (the world's first preserved railway, saved by volunteers in 1951), St Cadfan's Church (with the oldest inscription in the Welsh language), and a small Cardigan Bay town of railway and linguistic heritage
tywyn.localTip: The Talyllyn Railway is genuinely excellent and historically significant. St Cadfan's Stone for the oldest Welsh inscription. The beach for Cardigan Bay views. The Cambrian Way cycling.
tywyn.name: Tywyn

uckfield.history: Uckfield is a pleasant East Sussex market town in the High Weald. The Bluebell Railway — one of England's finest heritage steam lines — is accessible from the area. The town serves the local Wealden community.
uckfield.knownFor: The High Weald setting, the Bluebell Railway nearby, and a small East Sussex market town serving the Wealden community
uckfield.localTip: The Bluebell Railway from Sheffield Park is excellent. The High Weald countryside walking. Lewes is close for excellent heritage and food.
uckfield.name: Uckfield

ullapool.history: Ullapool was founded in 1788 as a planned fishing village by the British Fisheries Society. The ferry to Stornoway (Lewis) makes it the main gateway to the Outer Hebrides. The surrounding Northwest Highlands — Beinn Mòr Coigach, An Teallach, Stac Pollaidh — is some of the wildest scenery in Britain.
ullapool.knownFor: The ferry to Stornoway (the main Outer Hebrides crossing), Summer Isles views, the Ullapool Museum, and a remote Highland fishing village of extraordinary natural beauty at the foot of the Northwest Highlands
ullapool.localTip: Stac Pollaidh for an outstanding short hill walk with extraordinary views. The Summer Isles for the boat trip from Ullapool. The Ullapool Museum. The village for excellent local seafood.
ullapool.name: Ullapool

ulverston.history: Stan Laurel — half of Laurel and Hardy, the greatest comedy double act in cinema history — was born in Ulverston in 1890. The Hoad Monument (a replica of the Eddystone Lighthouse) dominates the skyline. The Ulverston Lantern Festival, held in September, is one of England's most extraordinary community arts events.
ulverston.knownFor: The birthplace of Stan Laurel (the comedy legend of Laurel and Hardy), the Lantern Festival (one of Britain's finest community lantern processions), the Hoad Monument, and a Cumbrian market town of great character
ulverston.localTip: The Stan Laurel Museum is charming. The Hoad Monument walk gives panoramic Lakeland and Morecambe Bay views. The Ulverston Lantern Festival in September is genuinely beautiful. The market town centre for independent food.
ulverston.name: Ulverston

ulverston-town.history: Stan Laurel was born here in 1890. The Hoad Monument (lighthouse replica) dominates the skyline. The Ulverston Lantern Festival is one of England's finest community arts events.
ulverston-town.knownFor: Stan Laurel's birthplace, the Hoad Monument, the Ulverston Lantern Festival, and a Cumbrian market town of great character
ulverston-town.localTip: The Stan Laurel Museum. The Hoad Monument walk for panoramic views. The Lantern Festival in September is beautiful.
ulverston-town.name: Ulverston

upminster.history: Upminster is an outer London suburb with a distinct village character. The Upminster Windmill — built in 1803 and well preserved — is a local landmark. Upminster Hall is a fine medieval timber-framed building. The tube gives good central London access.
upminster.knownFor: The Upminster Windmill (a preserved smock mill), Upminster Hall (a fine 15th century timber-framed building), and one of the outer terminus stations on the District and Hammersmith & City lines
upminster.localTip: The Upminster Windmill on open days is worth visiting. The village centre for local character. The tube for central London access. The local Essex countryside is accessible.
upminster.name: Upminster

upton-severn.history: Upton upon Severn is famous for its susceptibility to flooding — and its stoic community response to it. The Pepperpot — the old church tower with its distinctive octagonal lantern — is the town's most distinctive landmark. The Upton Folk Festival, held every year, is one of England's finest acoustic music events.
upton-severn.knownFor: The Upton Folk Festival (one of England's finest), the River Severn (regular flooding and beautiful riverside), the Pepperpot (the distinctive church tower now a heritage centre), and a Worcestershire riverside town of great charm
upton-severn.localTip: The Folk Festival in June is genuinely outstanding. The riverside walk along the Severn. The Pepperpot for the heritage. The town pubs for local Worcestershire character.
upton-severn.name: Upton upon Severn

upton-upon-severn.history: Upton upon Severn floods regularly but responds with community spirit. The Pepperpot octagonal lantern tower is distinctive. The Folk Festival is one of England's finest.
upton-upon-severn.knownFor: The Upton Folk Festival, the Pepperpot tower, the River Severn riverside, and a Worcestershire river town of great character
upton-upon-severn.localTip: The Folk Festival in June is outstanding. The Severn riverside walk. The Pepperpot heritage. Local pubs for Worcestershire character.
upton-upon-severn.name: Upton upon Severn

uxbridge.history: The Treaty of Uxbridge negotiations in 1645 — attempting to end the Civil War — failed, condemning England to further years of conflict. The Battle of Britain Operations Room at RAF Uxbridge (used in the famous 'Never in the field of human conflict' Churchill quote) is preserved. Brunel University is a significant local institution.
uxbridge.knownFor: The Treaty of Uxbridge (a failed 1645 peace negotiation between King Charles and Parliament), Brunel University, the RAF Uxbridge Battle of Britain bunker, and a West London outer borough with significant wartime heritage
uxbridge.localTip: The Battle of Britain Bunker at RAF Uxbridge is genuinely excellent and free. The Treaty House for the Civil War heritage. The town centre for good independent food.
uxbridge.name: Uxbridge

wadebridge.history: Wadebridge's medieval bridge — one of the longest in Cornwall — was reputedly founded on bales of wool. The town sits at the head of the Camel Estuary. The Camel Trail — running along the old railway line to Padstow and Bodmin — is the most popular cycling trail in the South West.
wadebridge.knownFor: The Camel Trail (the finest cycling trail in the South West, 18 miles to Padstow), the Bridge on Wool (the medieval bridge built on bales of wool), and a North Cornwall town at the heart of the Camel Estuary
wadebridge.localTip: The Camel Trail to Padstow is genuinely outstanding — 5 miles of beautiful estuary cycling. The medieval bridge for the wool story. The town centre for independent food. The Royal Cornwall Show at Wadebridge (June).
wadebridge.name: Wadebridge

wakefield.history: Barbara Hepworth — one of the 20th century's greatest sculptors — was born in Wakefield in 1903. The Hepworth Wakefield gallery, opened 2011, celebrates her life and work. The Yorkshire Sculpture Park at Bretton Hall is one of the world's greatest open-air sculpture collections. The Bridge Chapel of St Mary is one of only four surviving chantry chapels on bridges in England.
wakefield.knownFor: The Hepworth Wakefield (one of England's finest galleries, celebrating Barbara Hepworth), the Yorkshire Sculpture Park (one of the world's greatest), the Chantry Chapel (a bridge chapel, one of only four in England), and a West Yorkshire city of extraordinary sculptural significance
wakefield.localTip: The Hepworth Wakefield is free and genuinely outstanding. The Yorkshire Sculpture Park at Bretton is an extraordinary experience. The Bridge Chapel on Wakefield Bridge. The Cathedral Quarter for food.
wakefield.name: Wakefield

wallsend.history: Wallsend marks the eastern end of Hadrian's Wall — Segedunum Roman Fort ('the strong fort') was the easternmost fort on the Wall. The Segedunum Museum, with its reconstructed bath house and tower, is excellent. Swan Hunter shipyard — which built some of the world's most famous ships including the Mauretania — was based here.
wallsend.knownFor: The end of Hadrian's Wall (Segedunum Roman Fort marks where the Wall ends), the Swan Hunter shipyard heritage, and a Tyne and Wear town at the very end of Rome's northern frontier
wallsend.localTip: Segedunum Roman Fort is genuinely excellent — the tower gives extraordinary views. The bath house reconstruction is outstanding. The Hadrian's Wall coastal path from Wallsend.
wallsend.name: Wallsend

walsall.history: Walsall was the leather goods and saddlery capital of England — supplying saddlery to the British cavalry, bridles to the Empire. The Walsall Leather Museum tells this story brilliantly. The New Art Gallery Walsall, opened 2000, holds the Garman Ryan Collection donated by Kathleen Garman (Jacob Epstein's partner) — including works by Epstein, Picasso and Van Gogh.
walsall.knownFor: The Walsall Leather Museum (the saddlery and leather goods capital of England), the New Art Gallery Walsall (a landmark gallery holding the Garman Ryan Collection), and a Black Country town of significant industrial and artistic heritage
walsall.localTip: The New Art Gallery Walsall is free and genuinely outstanding — the Garman Ryan Collection with Picasso and Van Gogh is remarkable. The Leather Museum. The town market.
walsall.name: Walsall

walthamstow.history: William Morris — the Victorian designer, craftsman, poet and socialist who transformed British decorative arts — was born in Walthamstow in 1834. The William Morris Gallery, in his family home, is the world's only public museum dedicated to him. Walthamstow Village has an extraordinary collection of 18th century almshouses and historic buildings.
walthamstow.knownFor: The William Morris Gallery (the world's only public museum dedicated to William Morris, birthplace of the Arts and Crafts movement), the Walthamstow Market (London's longest street market), the Village (a beautifully preserved historic village), and one of East London's most rapidly improving neighbourhoods
walthamstow.localTip: The William Morris Gallery is genuinely excellent and free. The Walthamstow Market (Lloyd Park to Wood Street) on Tuesdays and Saturdays — the longest market in London. Walthamstow Village for the remarkable historic character.
walthamstow.name: Walthamstow

walton-on-thames.history: Walton-on-Thames is a well-connected Surrey town on the Thames. The area has a pleasant riverside character with good amenities. The Elmbridge Xcel leisure centre is a local facility.
walton-on-thames.knownFor: The Thames riverside, Elmbridge museum, and an affluent Surrey town on the Thames with good community character
walton-on-thames.localTip: The Thames towpath walk. The town centre for local character. Weybridge and Kingston are close for wider amenities.
walton-on-thames.name: Walton-on-Thames

wandsworth.history: Young's Brewery operated at the Ram Brewery in Wandsworth from 1831 to 2006 — one of London's oldest commercial breweries. Battersea Power Station — decommissioned in 1983 and now transformed into a major retail, residential and arts complex — is one of London's most dramatic regeneration stories. Wandsworth Common provides excellent green space.
wandsworth.knownFor: Young's Brewery heritage (the Ram Brewery, one of London's oldest), Wandsworth Common, Battersea Power Station (now transformed into one of London's most exciting destinations), and one of London's fastest-growing and most prosperous inner boroughs
wandsworth.localTip: Battersea Power Station's transformation is extraordinary — the Apple store inside is remarkable. Wandsworth Common for walking. The Ram Quarter (former Young's Brewery) for independent food. The Northcote Road for excellent food.
wandsworth.name: Wandsworth

wantage.history: King Alfred the Great — who saved England from the Vikings, reformed the law, promoted learning and arguably created the English nation — was born in Wantage in 849 AD. The town celebrates its royal connection proudly. The Vale of the White Horse — named for the prehistoric Uffington White Horse on the Downs — surrounds the town.
wantage.knownFor: The birthplace of King Alfred the Great (the only English king to be called 'Great'), the King Alfred's Kitchen, the Vale of the White Horse, and a beautiful Oxfordshire market town of royal significance
wantage.localTip: The King Alfred statue in the market place. Uffington White Horse (NT) is 7 miles — genuinely extraordinary. The Ridgeway National Trail for walking. The town for independent food.
wantage.name: Wantage

ware.history: The Great Bed of Ware — a massive 16th century bed famous enough to be mentioned in Twelfth Night — is now in the Victoria and Albert Museum but was made in Ware. The New River, begun in 1609, carried fresh water 38 miles from Hertfordshire to London. Izaak Walton fished the Lea at Ware.
ware.knownFor: The Great Bed of Ware (the famous bed mentioned by Shakespeare, now in the V&A), the New River (the 17th century water supply channel for London), and a Hertfordshire market town of literary and engineering significance
ware.localTip: The Scott's Grotto in the town is genuinely extraordinary — one of England's finest shell grottos. The River Lea walk. The town centre for local character. The New River heritage.
ware.name: Ware

wareham.history: Wareham's Saxon town walls — built by Alfred the Great in the 9th century — are the most complete in England, still enclosing the town on three sides. T.E. Lawrence (Lawrence of Arabia) lived at Clouds Hill, 4 miles away, and is buried at nearby Moreton. The town is the gateway to the Purbeck Hills and Isle of Purbeck.
wareham.knownFor: The Saxon town walls (the most complete in England), the River Frome, Lawrence of Arabia's connection (his cottage at Clouds Hill is 4 miles away), and a Purbeck gateway town of extraordinary Saxon and Lawrencian heritage
wareham.localTip: The Saxon walls walk is excellent — genuinely one of England's finest. Clouds Hill (NT) for Lawrence of Arabia's cottage. Moreton churchyard for Lawrence's grave. The Frome riverside walk.
wareham.name: Wareham

wareham-town.history: Wareham's Saxon walls are the most complete in England. T.E. Lawrence lived at Clouds Hill 4 miles away and is buried at Moreton.
wareham-town.knownFor: England's most complete Saxon town walls, Lawrence of Arabia's connection, and the gateway to Purbeck
wareham-town.localTip: The Saxon walls walk is outstanding. Clouds Hill for Lawrence's cottage. Moreton churchyard for his grave.
wareham-town.name: Wareham

warminster.history: Longleat House — completed 1580, owned by the Marquess of Bath — opened Britain's first drive-through safari park in 1966. The House has magnificent state rooms. Warminster is a garrison town — Battlesbury Hill and Scratchbury Hill are impressive Iron Age hillforts. Cley Hill is an extraordinary geological and prehistoric site.
warminster.knownFor: Longleat (one of England's greatest stately homes and the first safari park in Britain outside Africa), the garrison town heritage, and a Wiltshire town at the gateway to the Longleat Estate
warminster.localTip: Longleat is genuinely extraordinary — the safari and the house are both outstanding. Cley Hill for the Iron Age hillfort and views. The town centre for local character.
warminster.name: Warminster

warrenpoint.history: Warrenpoint suffered the deadliest day for the British Army in the Troubles — the Warrenpoint ambush of 27 August 1979 killed 18 soldiers, the same day Lord Mountbatten was killed in Sligo. The town sits on Carlingford Lough, the beautiful sea inlet between Down and Louth. Narrow Water Castle is a picturesque 16th century tower house.
warrenpoint.knownFor: The Narrow Water Castle, the Carlingford Lough, the 1979 Warrenpoint ambush (the deadliest day for the British Army in the Troubles), and a County Down town on the Irish border
warrenpoint.localTip: Narrow Water Castle for the picturesque tower on the lough. Carlingford Lough for the beautiful water scenery. The town centre for local character. The border location gives access to Carlingford village (excellent food) in the Republic.
warrenpoint.name: Warrenpoint

warrington.history: A Roman ford crossing and medieval market town at the lowest crossing of the Mersey. Warrington grew dramatically with industry and is now one of the UK's major commercial centres, with a high concentration of logistics and technology businesses.
warrington.knownFor: Its position at the heart of England's motorway network, the Golden Gates, Lymm village, Walton Hall and a large town that serves as the commercial hub between Manchester and Liverpool
warrington.localTip: Stockton Heath for independent food and village character — the best part of the Warrington area. Lymm village for independent shopping and the duck pond. Walton Hall Gardens are free and excellent.
warrington.name: Warrington

warwick.history: Warwick Castle — rebuilt by the Beauchamp Earls in the 14th century — is one of England's finest medieval fortresses. The Collegiate Church of St Mary has the magnificent Beauchamp Chapel. Lord Leycester Hospital — founded 1571 by the Earl of Leicester — is one of the finest groups of medieval timber-framed buildings in Britain.
warwick.knownFor: Warwick Castle (one of England's finest, a living medieval castle offering extraordinary experiences), the Collegiate Church of St Mary (one of the finest Gothic churches in England), Lord Leycester Hospital (a medieval almshouse of outstanding beauty), and a Warwickshire county town of extraordinary completeness
warwick.localTip: Lord Leycester Hospital is genuinely extraordinary and free. The Collegiate Church Beauchamp Chapel is magnificent. Warwick Castle for the full medieval experience. The town centre for excellent independent food.
warwick.name: Warwick

washington.history: Washington Old Hall — the ancestral home of George Washington's family — was occupied by Washington's ancestors from 1183 to 1613. The Hall, now a National Trust property, has direct links to the family of America's first President. The town was designated a new town in 1964.
washington.knownFor: The ancestral home of George Washington (the Old Hall is a museum to the first US President's family), the Washington F.C. football club, the WWT Washington Wetland Centre, and a Tyne and Wear new town with American heritage
washington.localTip: Washington Old Hall (NT) is genuinely fascinating — the American connections are well told. The WWT Washington Wetland Centre for birdwatching and wildlife. The Art Centre for local culture.
washington.name: Washington

watchet.history: Samuel Taylor Coleridge visited Watchet harbour in 1797 and was inspired to write The Rime of the Ancient Mariner — there is a statue of the Mariner on the quayside. The town has been a port since Saxon times. The last local Flatner boats — traditional flat-bottomed fishing vessels — were built here.
watchet.knownFor: The Ancient Mariner connection (Coleridge wrote The Rime of the Ancient Mariner after visiting), the harbour, the Flatner boats, and a small Somerset harbour town of literary and maritime heritage
watchet.localTip: The Mariner statue is charming and the harbour beautiful. The West Somerset Railway from nearby Minehead. The Watchet Museum for the maritime heritage. The cliff walks above the town.
watchet.name: Watchet

waterlooville.history: Waterlooville takes its name from the Wellington pub — a coaching inn where the Battle of Waterloo was celebrated in 1815. The town has grown substantially as a Portsmouth commuter town. The South Downs National Park is easily accessible.
waterlooville.knownFor: The original Wellington pub (the inn that gave the Battle of Waterloo its name, according to local legend), the South Downs gateway, and a Hampshire town between Portsmouth and the South Downs
waterlooville.localTip: The South Downs from Waterlooville for excellent walking. Portchester Castle is close — one of the finest Roman and medieval fortresses in Britain. Portsmouth for wider heritage and food.
waterlooville.name: Waterlooville

watford.history: The Warner Bros. Studio Tour at Leavesden — 3 miles from Watford — has attracted over 11 million visitors since opening in 2012, making it one of Britain's most visited paid attractions. Watford FC, nicknamed the Hornets, have played between the Premier League and Championship for decades. Cassiobury Park is one of Hertfordshire's finest.
watford.knownFor: Watford FC (a Premier League club with a devoted fanbase), the Warner Bros. Studio Tour — The Making of Harry Potter (one of Britain's most visited attractions), Cassiobury Park, and a Hertfordshire town of sporting and wizarding significance
watford.localTip: The Warner Bros. Studio Tour must be booked well in advance — genuinely extraordinary for Harry Potter fans. Cassiobury Park for excellent green space. Watford FC at Vicarage Road for the football.
watford.name: Watford

wednesbury.history: Wednesbury was a major centre of enamelware and hollow-ware manufacturing in the 19th century — at its peak producing much of the Empire's metalwork. The town is now the main depot for the West Midlands Metro tram network. The church of St Bartholomew has fine Victorian stained glass.
wednesbury.knownFor: The West Midlands Metro (Wednesbury is the main depot and hub for the tram network), the enamelware heritage, and a Black Country town of industrial significance
wednesbury.localTip: The West Midlands Metro connects the town to Birmingham and Wolverhampton. The town centre for local Black Country character. The church of St Bartholomew for the fine glass.
wednesbury.name: Wednesbury

wednesfield.history: The Battle of Wednesfield in 910 AD — where the Mercians and West Saxons, led by Edward the Elder and Aethelflaed, Lady of the Mercians, decisively defeated the Vikings — was one of the most significant battles in English history. The town grew as a lock and key manufacturing centre. Wednesfield is part of the City of Wolverhampton.
wednesfield.knownFor: The Battle of Wednesfield 910 AD (where the Danes were decisively defeated by the Saxons), the lock and key heritage, and a West Midlands community with deep Anglo-Saxon history
wednesfield.localTip: The battle site heritage. The community for local character. Wolverhampton is close for wider amenities and food.
wednesfield.name: Wednesfield

wellingborough.history: Wellingborough grew as a boot and shoe manufacturing town. The town has a strong market heritage. The church of All Hallows has notable Arts and Crafts features. The town is regenerating with new development.
wellingborough.knownFor: The market (one of Northamptonshire's finest), the shoe-making heritage, the Swansgate Shopping Centre, and a Northamptonshire market town with strong community identity
wellingborough.localTip: The market for local Northamptonshire produce. The town centre for local shopping. Rushden Lakes is close for retail. The surrounding Nene Valley for walking.
wellingborough.name: Wellingborough

wells.history: Wells Cathedral — begun around 1175 — has the most magnificent Early English West Front in the world, with 300 medieval sculptures. The Bishop's Palace moat is home to mute swans that have been trained for generations to ring a bell at the gatehouse when they want to be fed. Wells is England's smallest city by population.
wells.knownFor: The smallest city in England (population under 12,000 but with full cathedral status), Wells Cathedral (one of the finest in the world, with extraordinary West Front sculpture), the Bishop's Palace (with a moat and swans that ring a bell for food), and a Somerset city of extraordinary ecclesiastical beauty
wells.localTip: The Cathedral West Front is genuinely jaw-dropping. The Bishop's Palace and the swans ringing the bell — a genuinely charming tradition. The market for local Somerset produce. Vicar's Close (the oldest complete medieval street in Europe) is extraordinary.
wells.name: Wells

wells-next-the-sea.history: Wells-next-the-Sea was a significant port for centuries, trading with the Baltic. Holkham Beach — a vast expanse of sand with pine forest backed by salt marsh — is one of England's great natural places, owned by the Coke family of Holkham Hall. The town's whelk stalls have been selling seafood for generations.
wells-next-the-sea.knownFor: The beach (Holkham Beach is arguably the finest in England — the National Trust's vast sand backed by pines), the harbour, the whelks (Wells is famous for its seafood), and a North Norfolk harbour town of outstanding natural beauty
wells-next-the-sea.localTip: Holkham Beach is genuinely extraordinary — the scale of the sand and the pine backdrop are magnificent. The Wells harbour for whelks and fresh seafood. Holkham Hall for the Palladian mansion. The narrow gauge railway to the beach.
wells-next-the-sea.name: Wells-next-the-Sea

welshpool.history: Powis Castle — a red sandstone fortress rising above formal terraced gardens — is one of the National Trust's finest properties in Wales. The terraced gardens are among the most outstanding in Britain. The Welshpool and Llanfair Light Railway is a narrow-gauge heritage line through beautiful Mid Wales countryside.
welshpool.knownFor: Powis Castle (one of the finest castles and gardens in Wales, NT), the Welshpool and Llanfair Light Railway, the market town character, and the main market town of central Powys
