welshpool.localTip: Powis Castle and gardens (NT) are genuinely outstanding — the castle interior and terraced gardens are magnificent. The W&L Light Railway for a charming narrow-gauge trip. The Monday livestock market for authentic Welsh farming character.
welshpool.name: Welshpool

welshpool-town.history: Powis Castle's terraced gardens are among the finest in Britain. The W&L Light Railway is a heritage narrow-gauge line. The Monday market is one of Wales's most important agricultural markets.
welshpool-town.knownFor: Powis Castle (NT, one of Wales's finest with magnificent terraced gardens), the Welshpool and Llanfair Light Railway, and the main market town of central Powys
welshpool-town.localTip: Powis Castle and gardens are magnificent. The W&L Light Railway for a narrow-gauge trip. The Monday livestock market.
welshpool-town.name: Welshpool

welwyn-garden-city.history: Welwyn Garden City was the second Garden City founded by Ebenezer Howard in 1920, following Letchworth. The town was designed with tree-lined boulevards, green spaces and planned industry. The Shredded Wheat factory, built in 1925, is one of England's finest early industrial art deco buildings.
welwyn-garden-city.knownFor: The second Garden City in the world (founded 1920 by Ebenezer Howard, following Letchworth), the art deco Shredded Wheat factory (a listed building), the Birchwood Estate, and a Hertfordshire new town of planned community heritage
welwyn-garden-city.localTip: The art deco Shredded Wheat factory (now residential but the exterior is remarkable). The town centre tree-lined boulevards. The Birchwood Estate for the Garden City architecture.
welwyn-garden-city.name: Welwyn Garden City

wem.history: Wem is a quiet Shropshire market town with notable literary connections — William Hazlitt, one of England's great essayists, spent his formative years here. Sweet William flowers were first cultivated at Wem. The town has a pleasant market character.
wem.knownFor: The Sweet William variety (the first Sweet William was cultivated by Col. Henry Aylmer at Wem), William Hazlitt's connection (the essayist grew up here), and a small Shropshire market town
wem.localTip: The town centre for local Shropshire character. Hawkstone Park nearby for the extraordinary Georgian rock landscape gardens. Shrewsbury is close for wider amenities.
wem.name: Wem

wembley.history: The original Wembley Stadium — opened 1923, known for its twin towers — hosted the 1966 World Cup Final, countless FA Cup Finals and countless concerts. The new stadium, opened 2007, has an iconic arch and 90,000 capacity. Wembley's South Asian community has created one of London's great food destinations on Ealing Road and nearby streets.
wembley.knownFor: Wembley Stadium (the home of English football, built 2007 to replace the original with its twin towers), the SSE Arena (a major concert venue), the South Asian community (one of London's largest and finest), and a North West London neighbourhood of sporting and culinary significance
wembley.localTip: Wembley Stadium for major events — the experience is extraordinary. Ealing Road for outstanding South Asian food. The SSE Arena for concerts. The new Wembley area for development.
wembley.name: Wembley

wendover.history: Wendover sits in the heart of the Chilterns AONB. Coombe Hill — topped by the Boer War memorial — is the highest viewpoint in the Chilterns, with extraordinary views over the vale. The Ridgeway National Trail passes through. Chequers — the Prime Minister's country retreat — is 4 miles away.
wendover.knownFor: The Chiltern Hills setting (Wendover is at the heart of the Chilterns AONB), Coombe Hill (the highest viewpoint in the Chilterns with the Boer War memorial), the Ridgeway long-distance path, and a beautiful Buckinghamshire village
wendover.localTip: Coombe Hill walk is outstanding — the Chilterns views are extraordinary. The Ridgeway from Wendover for long-distance walking. The town for excellent independent food. Chequers is close (private).
wendover.name: Wendover

west-bromwich.history: West Bromwich Albion — founded 1878 — is one of English football's most historic clubs, winning the FA Cup and League title. The Old Library and the Town Hall are fine civic buildings. The OakHouse, a 16th century timber-framed manor, is a local heritage gem. Sandwell Valley Country Park provides good green space.
west-bromwich.knownFor: West Bromwich Albion FC (the Baggies, one of football's most historically significant clubs), the Sandwell Valley Country Park, the OakHouse (a fine 16th century half-timbered mansion), and a Black Country town of football heritage
west-bromwich.localTip: The Hawthorns stadium for WBA football. OakHouse Museum for the 16th century manor. Sandwell Valley Country Park for walking. The town centre for local shopping.
west-bromwich.name: West Bromwich

west-ham.history: West Ham United — 'the Academy of Football' — produced Bobby Moore, Geoff Hurst and Martin Peters who together scored all of England's goals in the 1966 World Cup Final. The club has moved to the London Stadium in Stratford. The area has strong working-class and diverse community heritage.
west-ham.knownFor: West Ham United FC (the Academy of Football — birthplace of Moore, Hurst and Peters of the 1966 World Cup), the green spaces, and a culturally significant East London neighbourhood
west-ham.localTip: The Bobby Moore connection is celebrated locally. Queen's Market for the diverse produce market. West Ham Park for green space. The Elizabeth Line and Jubilee Line for connectivity.
west-ham.name: West Ham

westerham.history: Chartwell — Winston Churchill's beloved home from 1922 to 1964 — is one of the National Trust's most visited properties. Churchill painted, wrote and planned the nation's future here. The statue of General Wolfe in the village square celebrates the man who defeated the French at the Plains of Abraham in 1759, making Canada British.
westerham.knownFor: Winston Churchill's home (Chartwell, where he lived from 1922 to 1964, is a National Trust property), the General Wolfe statue (who captured Quebec in 1759), and a beautiful Kent village of historic significance
westerham.localTip: Chartwell is genuinely moving — Churchill's study, the garden and the paintings are extraordinary. The village square and General Wolfe's statue. The North Downs walking from Westerham.
westerham.name: Westerham

weston-super-mare.history: Weston-super-Mare is a traditional British seaside resort. Banksy's Dismaland — a dystopian 'bemusement park' held in the Tropicana in 2015 — became one of the most talked-about art events of the decade. The Grand Pier (rebuilt after fire in 2008) is outstanding. The donkeys on the beach have been a tradition for generations.
weston-super-mare.knownFor: Banksy's Dismaland (held here in 2015 — a temporary bemusement park), the Grand Pier, the Weston donkeys, Tropicana (the restored outdoor pool and events venue), and a Somerset seaside resort of traditional character and contemporary art fame
weston-super-mare.localTip: The Grand Pier is excellent. Banksy's local street art is still visible around the town. The Tropicana for events. The beach donkeys for the traditional seaside experience.
weston-super-mare.name: Weston-super-Mare

wetherby.history: Wetherby is a handsome coaching town on the River Wharfe. The racecourse — one of the finest National Hunt venues in Yorkshire — hosts important jumping races including the Charlie Hall Chase. The town's market square and bridge over the Wharfe are genuinely beautiful.
wetherby.knownFor: The Wetherby Racecourse (one of Yorkshire's finest National Hunt courses), the River Wharfe, the Wetham Market, and a handsome Yorkshire market town on the Wharfe
wetherby.localTip: Wetherby Racecourse for excellent National Hunt racing. The Wharfe bridge and riverside walk. The market town square. Good independent food and shops in the town centre.
wetherby.name: Wetherby

weymouth.history: George III bathed here in 1789, triggering the Georgian resort boom. The seafront is exceptional — a continuous Georgian terrace facing the sea. Weymouth hosted the sailing events of the London 2012 Olympics.
weymouth.knownFor: The Georgian seafront (arguably England's finest), the 2012 Olympic sailing venue, Chesil Beach, the Fleet Lagoon and an extraordinary Dorset coastal setting
weymouth.localTip: The Old Harbour for independent restaurants and fish and chips. The Georgian seafront at dawn or dusk. Chesil Beach is extraordinary — 18 miles of pebble ridge. The ferry to Portland.
weymouth.name: Weymouth

whickham.history: Gibside — a Georgian pleasure ground created by George Bowes in the 18th century and now managed by the National Trust — is one of the finest ruined landscapes in England, with a Palladian chapel, walled garden and mile-long tree-lined avenue. Whickham village has good community character.
whickham.knownFor: The Gibside Estate (one of the finest ruined Georgian landscapes in England, National Trust), the Derwent Valley, and a Tyne and Wear community with extraordinary heritage nearby
whickham.localTip: Gibside is genuinely extraordinary and often uncrowded — the avenue, the chapel and the wilderness are outstanding. The Derwent Valley walk. The community has good local character.
whickham.name: Whickham

whitby.history: A Norse settlement, then a major fishing port and whaling base. Captain Cook apprenticed here. Bram Stoker visited in 1890 and set Dracula's English arrival here. The 13th century Abbey ruins on the clifftop are among England's most dramatic.
whitby.knownFor: The Abbey, 199 steps, Bram Stoker's Dracula, the best fish and chips in England (contested), jet jewellery and a fishing port of extraordinary atmospheric beauty
whitby.localTip: Church Street on the east side before the coaches arrive. Sandgate for fish and chips — Magpie Café or the Quayside. The 199 steps at dusk. The weekly market.
whitby.name: Whitby

whitehaven.history: Whitehaven was a planned Georgian town — one of the finest outside London — built to export coal and import tobacco from Virginia. In 1778, American privateer John Paul Jones raided the harbour — the last hostile foreign attack on mainland Britain. The town has extraordinary Georgian architecture. The Rum Story museum tells the trading heritage.
whitehaven.knownFor: The Georgian planned town (one of the finest in northern England), the harbour (where John Paul Jones attacked in 1778 — the last foreign attack on mainland Britain), and a Cumbrian port town of remarkable historical depth
whitehaven.localTip: The Georgian streets are genuinely beautiful. The Rum Story museum is excellent. The harbour for the John Paul Jones connection. The Cumbrian Coast Railway for the scenic journey.
whitehaven.name: Whitehaven

whithorn.history: St Ninian built the Candida Casa (White House) at Whithorn in 397 AD — making it the earliest recorded Christian site in Scotland, predating Columba's Iona by 165 years. The Whithorn Dig has uncovered extraordinary early Christian material. The priory ruins are significant. Two pilgrimages to Whithorn were worth one to Rome.
whithorn.knownFor: The earliest Christian site in Scotland (St Ninian founded his Candida Casa here in 397 AD), the Whithorn Dig (the most important early Christian archaeological site in Scotland), and a small Galloway village of extraordinary religious heritage
whithorn.localTip: The Whithorn Story visitor centre is excellent — the archaeology is genuinely significant. The priory ruins. The Isle of Whithorn for the atmospheric harbour. The Galloway walking.
whithorn.name: Whithorn

whitley-bay.history: Whitley Bay's Spanish City dome — opened 1910 — was a magnificent Edwardian pleasure palace that fell into disrepair before being spectacularly restored in 2018. The beach is excellent. The town has undergone remarkable independent food and drink revival, making it one of the North East's most exciting destinations.
whitley-bay.knownFor: The Spanish City (the magnificent Edwardian pleasure dome, beautifully restored), the beach (one of the finest on the North East coast), the independent food scene, and a Tyne and Wear coastal resort making a remarkable revival
whitley-bay.localTip: The Spanish City restoration is beautiful — the food and events inside are excellent. The beach is good. The town centre independent food scene is outstanding for its size.
whitley-bay.name: Whitley Bay

whitley-bay-town.history: The Spanish City dome (1910) has been spectacularly restored. Whitley Bay is undergoing a remarkable independent food and drink revival.
whitley-bay-town.knownFor: The Spanish City (restored Edwardian pleasure dome), the excellent beach, and the remarkable independent food revival
whitley-bay-town.localTip: The Spanish City is beautiful. The beach is excellent. The independent food scene is outstanding.
whitley-bay-town.name: Whitley Bay

whitstable.history: Whitstable oysters have been farmed since Roman times and were sent to Rome by the cart-load. The Whitstable Oyster Festival, held every July, is one of England's most celebrated food events. The town has a distinct character — bohemian, artistic and food-focused — that makes it one of the most visited day-trip destinations from London.
whitstable.knownFor: The Whitstable oysters (world famous since Roman times), the Oyster Festival (one of England's most celebrated food festivals), the beach huts, and a Kent seaside town with exceptional food culture
whitstable.localTip: Whitstable Oyster Company and the harbour for fresh oysters. The Oyster Festival in July is genuinely excellent. The beach huts for the classic Whitstable experience. The town for outstanding independent food.
whitstable.name: Whitstable

wick.history: Wick was once the world's busiest herring port — in the 1860s, over 1,000 boats worked from the harbour. The Wick Heritage Museum, run entirely by volunteers, is the finest fishing museum in Scotland. Caithness Glass has been made here since 1961. The Castle of Mey — the Queen Mother's home — is 18 miles away.
wick.knownFor: Wick Heritage Museum (the finest herring fishing museum in Scotland), the Caithness Glass visitor centre, the Castle of Mey (Queen Mother's home nearby), and the most northerly mainland town in Scotland with significant herring heritage
wick.localTip: The Wick Heritage Museum is genuinely extraordinary and free — the scale of the herring fishing industry is astonishing. Caithness Glass for the glassblowing. The Castle of Mey for the Queen Mother's home. The Caithness coast for the dramatic cliffs.
wick.name: Wick

widnes.history: Paul Simon wrote 'Homeward Bound' at Widnes railway station in 1965 while on tour. Widnes was the world's centre of alkali chemical manufacture in the 19th century — creating the chemical industry that transformed the world. The Catalyst Museum tells this story. The Widnes Vikings are a successful rugby league club.
widnes.knownFor: The birthplace of Paul Simon's song 'Homeward Bound' (written at Widnes railway station in 1965), the chemical industry heritage (Widnes was the 19th century capital of the chemical industry), and a Cheshire town on the Mersey with unexpected musical heritage
widnes.localTip: The railway station plaque for Paul Simon's Homeward Bound moment. The Catalyst Museum for the chemical heritage. The Mersey Gateway Bridge for engineering marvel. The Vikings for rugby league.
widnes.name: Widnes

wigan.history: A coal mining and textile town immortalised by George Orwell's 'The Road to Wigan Pier' (1937). The town's rugby league heritage is as important as its mining past. The actual 'pier' was a small loading platform — the irony of its fame was Orwell's point.
wigan.knownFor: Wigan Pier (made famous by Orwell's 1937 book), the Warriors rugby league team, pie-eating culture and a Lancashire mill town with a fiercely proud identity
wigan.localTip: The Galleries shopping centre has the essentials. Market Street for independent food. The Wigan Pier Heritage Centre is genuinely good. Standish village is well worth the short drive.
wigan.name: Wigan

wigtown.history: Wigtown was designated Scotland's National Book Town in 1998 — the town now has numerous independent bookshops in a village of under 1,000 people. The Wigtown Book Festival, held every autumn, is one of Scotland's finest literary events. The Martyrs' Memorial commemorates two Covenanting women drowned in the Solway in 1685.
wigtown.knownFor: Scotland's National Book Town (with more bookshops per capita than anywhere in Scotland), the Wigtown Book Festival (one of Scotland's finest literary festivals), the Martyrs' Memorial, and a small Galloway town of extraordinary literary significance
wigtown.localTip: The bookshops are genuinely excellent — The Bookshop is a wonderful indie. The Book Festival in late September. The Martyrs' Memorial for the Covenanting story. The Solway estuary views.
wigtown.name: Wigtown

willenhall.history: Willenhall was the lock-making capital of Britain — the town produced the majority of British door locks, padlocks and key mechanisms from the 18th century. The Lock Museum, in a Victorian locksmith's house, tells the story. The craft skills of Willenhall locksmiths were unequalled.
willenhall.knownFor: The lock-making capital of the world (Willenhall produced the majority of Britain's locks for 300 years), the Lock Museum, and a Black Country town of industrial lock heritage
willenhall.localTip: The Lock Museum is genuinely fascinating. The town centre for local Black Country character. Wolverhampton is close for wider amenities.
willenhall.name: Willenhall

wilmslow.history: Alan Turing — the father of computer science and the man who broke Enigma — lived and died in Wilmslow. His house at 43 Adlington Road is blue-plaqued. The town is one of Cheshire's most prosperous — sometimes called the 'Golden Triangle' of affluence with Alderley Edge and Knutsford.
wilmslow.knownFor: Alan Turing's home (the computing pioneer lived and died in Wilmslow), the Cheshire affluence, the excellent independent retail, and the Manchester Airport gateway
wilmslow.localTip: The Alan Turing blue plaque at Adlington Road. The town centre for excellent independent shops and food. Alderley Edge is close for the legendary Cheshire escarpment walk.
wilmslow.name: Wilmslow

wimbledon.history: The Wimbledon Championships — held at the All England Club since 1877 — are the oldest and most prestigious tennis tournament in the world. Wimbledon Common (1,100 acres of wild common land) is one of London's finest. The Village has exceptional independent shops and restaurants. Wimbledon AFC — the original — play locally.
wimbledon.knownFor: The Wimbledon Championships (the world's oldest and most prestigious tennis tournament), Wimbledon Common, the Village, and one of South West London's most desirable and complete neighbourhoods
wimbledon.localTip: The Championships in late June/early July are genuinely special. Wimbledon Common for excellent walking and running. The Village for outstanding independent food. The Windmill on the Common.
wimbledon.name: Wimbledon

wimborne-minster.history: Wimborne Minster's collegiate church — begun in the 11th century — has the Chained Library (one of only four surviving in England, books chained to prevent theft) and the quarter-jack — a grenadier soldier figure who strikes the quarter hours. The church's tower gives extraordinary Dorset views.
wimborne-minster.knownFor: Wimborne Minster (the remarkable collegiate church with its chained library and quarter-jack clock figure), the Saturday market, and a beautiful Dorset market town
wimborne-minster.localTip: The Minster is magnificent and the Chained Library is extraordinary. The quarter-jack for the clock-watching. The Saturday market for local Dorset produce. The Stour riverside walk.
wimborne-minster.name: Wimborne Minster

wincanton.history: Wincanton Racecourse is one of England's most popular National Hunt venues, with a fiercely loyal following. The Discworld Emporium — devoted to Terry Pratchett's Discworld series — makes Wincanton the 'twin town' of Ankh-Morpork, Pratchett's fictional city. The town has good independent character.
wincanton.knownFor: Wincanton Racecourse (one of England's most popular National Hunt courses), the Discworld Emporium (the official Terry Pratchett merchandise and tribute shop), and a Somerset market town
wincanton.localTip: Wincanton Racecourse for excellent jumping racing. The Discworld Emporium for Pratchett fans — genuinely charming. The town centre for local Somerset character. Stourhead is close.
wincanton.name: Wincanton

winchester.history: Winchester was the capital of England before London. Alfred the Great is buried here. William the Conqueror was crowned in Winchester Cathedral, which he built between 1079 and 1093
winchester.knownFor: Winchester Cathedral, the Great Hall with its legendary Round Table, the water meadows, the independent food scene and the Christmas market
winchester.localTip: Walk the water meadows south to St Cross Hospital — a medieval almshouse still functioning as it did in the 15th century. The Saturday farmers' market is exceptional. Parchment Street for shopping.
winchester.name: Winchester

windermere.history: Windermere the lake is 10.5 miles long — England's largest. The town grew as a Victorian railway terminus when the line arrived in 1847, transforming what had been a remote farming community
windermere.knownFor: England's largest lake, Beatrix Potter, the Steamboat Museum, the viewpoints from Orrest Head and a food scene that's developed significantly around the tourism economy
windermere.localTip: Walk to Orrest Head first thing — 20 minutes and one of the greatest views in England. Bowness is busy but has good food. Take the ferry across to Far Sawrey for Beatrix Potter's Hill Top farm.
windermere.name: Windermere

windsor.history: Windsor Castle has been a royal residence for almost 1,000 years — William the Conqueror built the first castle in 1070. St George's Chapel within the castle is the finest late Gothic building in England and the burial place of ten monarchs including Henry VIII and Charles III's parents. Eton College, founded 1440, is immediately across the river.
windsor.knownFor: Windsor Castle (the largest inhabited castle in the world and the oldest royal residence in continuous occupation), the Long Walk (a 2.6-mile tree-lined avenue to the castle), Eton College, the Thames, and a Berkshire town of extraordinary royal presence
windsor.localTip: St George's Chapel is genuinely extraordinary — the late Gothic fan vaulting is magnificent. The Long Walk for the castle approach. Eton for the beautiful college. The Thames riverside walk.
windsor.name: Windsor

withernsea.history: Withernsea's lighthouse — built 1894, now inland due to coastal erosion — houses a museum to Kay Kendall, the actress who was born in Withernsea. The Holderness coast is one of the fastest eroding coastlines in Europe. The beach is good and often uncrowded.
withernsea.knownFor: The lighthouse (now a museum to Kay Kendall, the actress), the beach, and a small East Yorkshire seaside resort on the Holderness coast
withernsea.localTip: The lighthouse museum for the Kay Kendall story. The beach for the uncrowded Holderness coast. The town for local East Yorkshire character. Hull is close for wider amenities.
withernsea.name: Withernsea

witney.history: Witney was the world's centre of blanket manufacturing for three centuries — Early's Blankets and other mills supplied the world, including Hudson's Bay trading blankets. The Market Place and the Buttercross are genuinely beautiful. The Cogges Farm Museum at an historic Thames-side farm is excellent.
witney.knownFor: The blanket-making heritage (Witney was the blanket capital of the world for 300 years), the market place (one of Oxfordshire's finest), the Cogges Farm Museum, and a beautiful Oxfordshire market town
witney.localTip: The Buttercross and Market Place are beautiful. The Cogges Farm Museum is excellent. The Windrush Valley walking from Witney. The town for excellent independent food.
witney.name: Witney

witton-gilbert.history: Witton Gilbert is a quiet village in County Durham, a few miles from Durham city. The village has good community character and access to the surrounding County Durham countryside.
witton-gilbert.knownFor: The Durham countryside setting, the village community, and a small County Durham village north of Durham city
witton-gilbert.localTip: Durham City is close for excellent heritage and food. The surrounding countryside for walking. The village has quiet rural character.
witton-gilbert.name: Witton Gilbert

woking.history: The Shah Jahan Mosque in Woking (1889) was the first purpose-built mosque in the United Kingdom. H.G. Wells was living in Woking when he wrote The War of the Worlds — the Martians land at Horsell Common. The town has significant Victorian heritage. The Lightbox gallery is excellent.
woking.knownFor: The first mosque in England (Shah Jahan Mosque, 1889), H.G. Wells's connection (The War of the Worlds is set in Woking), the Victoria Arch, and a Surrey commuter town of surprising historical depth
woking.localTip: The Shah Jahan Mosque is historically extraordinary — the oldest mosque in Britain. Horsell Common for the War of the Worlds connection. The Lightbox gallery for art. The town centre for independent food.
woking.name: Woking

wokingham.history: Wokingham is one of England's most prosperous towns — consistently rated highly for quality of life and repeatedly one of the fastest-growing. The historic town centre has fine buildings including the 17th century Town Hall. Dinton Pastures Country Park provides excellent water sport and walking facilities.
wokingham.knownFor: The Wokingham Bulge (the fastest growing town in Britain for decades), the Dinton Pastures Country Park, and one of the most prosperous market towns in Berkshire
wokingham.localTip: Dinton Pastures for water sports and walking. The town centre for excellent independent food. Reading is close for wider amenities. The town is genuinely prosperous and pleasant.
wokingham.name: Wokingham

wolverhampton.history: Wolverhampton — 'Wolves' — has a civic centre of remarkable Victorian quality: the Civic Hall, the Law Courts and the Wulfrun Hall. Wolverhampton Wanderers were one of the founding members of the Football League (1888). The city has a rich multicultural heritage with significant South Asian, Caribbean and West African communities.
wolverhampton.knownFor: The city with the most Grade I listed buildings per capita in England, Wolverhampton Wanderers FC (one of the oldest professional clubs), the Molineux Stadium, the Civic Hall and Grand Theatre, and a Black Country city of significant Victorian civic ambition
wolverhampton.localTip: The Grand Theatre and Civic Hall for excellent culture. Molineux Stadium for Wolves football. The Wolverhampton Art Gallery (free) for the exceptional Pre-Raphaelite collection. The city centre market.
wolverhampton.name: Wolverhampton

wombwell.history: Wombwell grew around the Mitchell Main and Darfield Main collieries. The town has strong South Yorkshire community identity and coalfield roots. The Dearne Valley Country Park provides local green space.
wombwell.knownFor: The Dearne Valley, the South Yorkshire coalfield heritage, and a Barnsley area community with mining roots
wombwell.localTip: The Dearne Valley Country Park for walking. Barnsley is close for wider amenities. The community has strong local identity.
wombwell.name: Wombwell

wood-green.history: Alexandra Palace — built 1873 on a dramatic hill — hosted the BBC's first television broadcast in 1936. The Palace's role in television history is extraordinary — the world's first regular public TV service began here. The Palace now hosts concerts, events and an ice rink. Wood Green has a strong Turkish, Greek and diverse community.
wood-green.knownFor: Alexandra Palace (the People's Palace with extraordinary hilltop views over London, where the BBC first broadcast television in 1936), the Wood Green shopping centre, and a diverse North London neighbourhood
wood-green.localTip: Alexandra Palace for the extraordinary hilltop views and the BBC television heritage. The ice rink. The Saturday farmers' market at the Palace. Wood Green High Road for diverse food.
wood-green.name: Wood Green

woodbridge.history: Sutton Hoo — 2 miles from Woodbridge — was the site of the extraordinary 1939 discovery of a 7th century Anglo-Saxon ship burial containing the most spectacular treasure in British archaeology, including the iconic Sutton Hoo helmet. The Woodbridge Tide Mill (1170, rebuilt 1793) is the finest working tide mill in Britain. The Deben Estuary is beautiful.
woodbridge.knownFor: The Sutton Hoo Ship Burial (the most spectacular archaeological find in Britain, 2 miles away), the Tide Mill (the finest working tide mill in Britain), the Deben Estuary, and a beautiful Suffolk market town of extraordinary heritage
woodbridge.localTip: Sutton Hoo (NT) is genuinely extraordinary — the helmet, the ship burial and the landscape are outstanding. The Tide Mill on the Deben. The town centre for outstanding independent food. The Deben estuary walk.
woodbridge.name: Woodbridge

woodstock.history: Blenheim Palace — built 1705-22 for the Duke of Marlborough as a gift from the nation — is the only non-royal palace in England and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Winston Churchill was born here in 1874. The palace and Capability Brown's park are extraordinary. Woodstock is one of the most beautiful small towns in Oxfordshire.
woodstock.knownFor: Blenheim Palace (the only non-royal palace in England, birthplace of Winston Churchill, and a UNESCO World Heritage Site), and a beautiful Oxfordshire town at the park gates
woodstock.localTip: Blenheim Palace is genuinely magnificent — the state rooms, the park and the gardens are outstanding. The Churchill birthplace room. The town centre for excellent independent food. The park walk (free).
woodstock.name: Woodstock

worcester.history: The Battle of Worcester in 1651 — where Cromwell defeated Charles II — was the final battle of the Civil War. Worcester Cathedral contains the tomb of King John (died 1216). Royal Worcester Porcelain — founded 1751 — is the world's oldest continuous porcelain factory. The cricket ground beside the Cathedral is one of England's most beautiful.
worcester.knownFor: Worcester Cathedral (where King John is buried, with one of the finest Chapter Houses in England), the final battle of the Civil War (1651), the Royal Worcester Porcelain (the world's oldest continuous porcelain manufacturer), and a Worcestershire city of extraordinary completeness
worcester.localTip: The Cathedral is magnificent — King John's tomb and the Chapter House are outstanding. The Royal Worcester Porcelain Museum. The cricket ground for the Cathedral backdrop. The Guildhall for the elegant Queen Anne building.
worcester.name: Worcester

workington.history: Workington has been one of Britain's most important steel towns — Corus (now Tata Steel) has been the last remaining integrated rail steel-making plant in the UK. Mary Queen of Scots landed at Workington on her flight from Scotland in 1568. Workington Hall, where she was received, is now a ruin.
workington.knownFor: The steel heritage (the last remaining integrated steel rail-making plant in the UK), the Workington Hall ruins, and a Cumbrian port town with steel-making significance
workington.localTip: Mary Queen of Scots' landing is marked locally. Workington Hall ruins for the historic connection. The coast for the Solway Firth views. The town centre for local community character.
workington.name: Workington

worksop.history: The area around Worksop is known as the Dukeries — an extraordinary concentration of ducal estates including Clumber Park (NT), Welbeck Abbey and Thoresby Hall. The Pilgrim Fathers movement grew from churches in the Worksop area. The town is the gateway to the Dukeries landscape.
worksop.knownFor: The Dukeries (the extraordinary concentration of great ducal estates — Clumber, Welbeck, Thoresby — in the area), the Pilgrim Fathers connection (the movement began nearby), and a Nottinghamshire market town at the gateway to the Dukeries
worksop.localTip: Clumber Park (NT) is outstanding — the chapel, park and lakeside are extraordinary. Sherwood Forest from Worksop. The town centre for local shopping. The Pilgrim Fathers heritage trail.
worksop.name: Worksop

worthing.history: Oscar Wilde wrote The Importance of Being Earnest while staying in Worthing in 1894 — he named the main character Ernest Worthing in tribute to the town. The pier is one of the South Coast's finest. The town has a growing independent food scene and arts culture.
worthing.knownFor: The Oscar Wilde connection (he wrote The Importance of Being Earnest in Worthing, naming the central character after the town), the pier, the beach, the Worthing Museum, and a Sussex seaside town of literary heritage and improving independent culture
worthing.localTip: The Worthing Museum is free and excellent. The pier for the classic seaside experience. The town centre for improving independent food. The West Beach and Goring-by-Sea for the best beach stretches.
worthing.name: Worthing

wrexham.history: Wrexham AFC's extraordinary Hollywood takeover by Ryan Reynolds and Rob McElhenney in 2020 — and subsequent rise through the non-league and into the Football League — became an international phenomenon through the Disney+ documentary. St Giles' Church is one of the finest medieval churches in Wales. The town is the largest in North Wales.
wrexham.knownFor: Wrexham AFC (promoted to the Football League by Hollywood co-owners Ryan Reynolds and Rob McElhenney — the Welcome to Wrexham documentary), St Giles' Church (one of the Seven Wonders of Wales), and Wales's largest town
wrexham.localTip: Wrexham AFC at the Racecourse Ground — genuinely extraordinary atmosphere following the Hollywood takeover. St Giles' Church for the Seven Wonders of Wales tower. The town centre for local Welsh character.
wrexham.name: Wrexham

wrexham-town.history: Ryan Reynolds and Rob McElhenney's takeover of Wrexham AFC and the Welcome to Wrexham documentary made the club globally famous. St Giles' Church is one of Wales's finest medieval towers.
wrexham-town.knownFor: Wrexham AFC (Ryan Reynolds and Rob McElhenney's Hollywood takeover), St Giles' Church (Seven Wonders of Wales), and Wales's largest town
wrexham-town.localTip: The Racecourse Ground for the extraordinary Wrexham AFC atmosphere. St Giles' Church for the Seven Wonders tower.
wrexham-town.name: Wrexham

wymondham.history: Wymondham Abbey — with its two remarkable towers at each end of the nave — is one of Norfolk's finest medieval buildings. Robert Kett led the great popular uprising of 1549 — Kett's Rebellion — from a camp outside Wymondham, marching 16,000 men to Norwich in protest at enclosures. The Market Cross (1617) is one of the finest in England.
wymondham.knownFor: The Wymondham Abbey (one of the finest medieval abbey churches in Norfolk, with two towers), the Market Cross (an extraordinary timber-framed market cross), Robert Kett's connection (the leader of Kett's Rebellion 1549), and a Norfolk market town of great character
wymondham.localTip: The Abbey is magnificent and free. The Market Cross for the beautiful timber-framing. The Kett's Rebellion heritage trail. The heritage railway to Dereham.
wymondham.name: Wymondham

yarm.history: The Stockton and Darlington Railway — the world's first steam passenger railway — was planned at a meeting in Yarm's George and Dragon Hotel in 1820. The High Street, set in the beautiful Tees meander, is one of the finest Georgian streets in the North of England.
yarm.knownFor: The George and Dragon Hotel (where the Stockton and Darlington Railway was planned in 1820), the Georgian High Street (one of the finest in the North), the River Tees meander, and a beautiful Yorkshire market town of railway and Georgian heritage
yarm.localTip: The George and Dragon for the railway planning heritage. The Georgian High Street is genuinely beautiful. The Tees meander walk around the town. The town centre for excellent independent food.
yarm.name: Yarm

yeadon.history: Yeadon is home to Leeds Bradford Airport — the main airport for West Yorkshire and the North East. The town has a good community character. Yeadon Tarn provides local wildlife and walking. The town is well connected to Leeds and Bradford.
yeadon.knownFor: Leeds Bradford Airport (the main airport for Yorkshire and the North East, located in Yeadon), the Yeadon Tarn, and an Airedale community near Leeds
yeadon.localTip: Yeadon Tarn for local wildlife and walking. Leeds Bradford Airport for travel connections. The town centre for local character. Guiseley and Rawdon are close for wider food.
yeadon.name: Yeadon

yeovil.history: Yeovil was the glove-making capital of England for centuries — at its peak producing millions of pairs annually. The Fleet Air Arm Museum at nearby RNAS Yeovilton is the world's largest naval aviation museum, including Concorde. Montacute House — a perfect Elizabethan H-plan mansion — is one of the NT's finest properties.
yeovil.knownFor: The glove-making heritage (Yeovil was the glove capital of England), Fleet Air Arm Museum (the world's largest naval aviation museum), Montacute House (one of England's finest Elizabethan mansions, NT), and a Somerset market town of significant military and industrial heritage
yeovil.localTip: The Fleet Air Arm Museum is genuinely excellent — the aircraft collection is outstanding. Montacute House is magnificent. The town centre for local Somerset shopping and food.
yeovil.name: Yeovil

york.history: Founded by the Romans as Eboracum in 71 AD, captured by the Vikings who called it Jorvik. One of the most complete medieval cities in northern Europe, with walls, bars and streets largely intact. Constantine was proclaimed Emperor here.
york.knownFor: The Shambles, York Minster, the city walls, Micklegate, the Jorvik Viking Centre and a medieval street plan that still works 2,000 years after the Romans laid it out
york.localTip: Walk the city walls at dawn before the tourists arrive. Fossgate and Gillygate for independent restaurants. The Shambles is worth seeing once — then escape to Micklegate for real York life. Bishy Road (Bishopthorpe Road) on a Saturday.
york.name: York

ystradgynlais.history: Ystradgynlais sits at the head of the Swansea Valley, at the edge of the Brecon Beacons National Park. Dan yr Ogof Showcaves — the largest showcave complex in northern Europe — are a short drive from the town. The town has strong Welsh language community character.
ystradgynlais.knownFor: The Brecon Beacons gateway, the Swansea Valley, the Dan yr Ogof showcaves (the largest showcave complex in northern Europe), and a South Wales valleys community at the edge of the national park
ystradgynlais.localTip: Dan yr Ogof Showcaves are genuinely extraordinary — the Cathedral Cave is vast. The Brecon Beacons from Ystradgynlais for excellent walking. The Craig y Nos Castle for the Adelina Patti heritage.
ystradgynlais.name: Ystradgynlais
